Genesis

Wheel of Heaven Translation

First book of the Torah — creation, the flood, and the patriarchs

Direct Direct Established or uncontested across mainstream sources, or a direct description of the project itself. Framework Framework Foundational premise of the Wheel of Heaven framework. Explicit in the Raëlian canon; not endorsed by mainstream scholarship. Inferred Inferred A reasonable reading of comparative material — not stated literally, but consistent with the source. Speculative Speculative Interpretive synthesis or hypothesis that goes beyond what any single source states.

An English translation of Genesis from the pointed Hebrew of the Westminster Leningrad Codex (Public Domain), presented in parallel with the source. Per-verse commentary documents the lexical choices that distinguish this rendering from inherited English versions — the plurality of *Elohim*, three distinct creation verbs (*bara*, *asah*, *yatsar*) preserved, *raqia* as a hammered-out dome, the cohortative *na'aseh* in 1:26, *tselem* and *demut* as physical resemblance, the polemical avoidance of *shemesh* and *yareach* in 1:14–16, the seam at *YHWH-Elohim* in 2:4, *ha-adam* held as a category-noun rather than a proper name, *tzela* read as 'side' rather than 'rib', and *ezer kenegdo* read as 'counterpart fitting him' rather than 'helper'. The Wheel of Heaven Translation Program is publishing Genesis chapter by chapter; the draft is prepared with Claude Opus 4.7 against translation-glossary v1.1.0 and awaits human review.

Chapter 1

Genesis 1 — When the Elohim began to shape

1
When the Elohim began to shape the skies and the land—
בְּרֵאשִׁ֖ית בָּרָ֣א אֱלֹהִ֑ים אֵ֥ת הַשָּׁמַ֖יִם וְאֵ֥ת הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Three load-bearing choices in one verse.

Bereshit is grammatically a construct form without the definite article — בְּרֵאשִׁית means "in beginning-of", not "in the beginning" (which would require בָּרֵאשִׁית). The verse is a temporal clause introducing v2 and v3, not a freestanding declaration. The Rashi-favoured reading, also adopted by NJPS, NRSV, and most modern scholarly translations, treats the construct as it stands: When the Elohim began to shape … The traditional "In the beginning" carries a creatio-ex-nihilo doctrinal commitment the Hebrew doesn't grammatically support. Restoring the construct lets the text describe a project beginning, not the absolute origin of all matter.

Bara is one of three creation verbs Genesis 1 deploys deliberately (with asah "make" and yatsar "form"). Standard translations flatten all three into "create/make", losing the pattern. Bara in the Qal denotes shaping or fashioning; the Piel of the same root means "cut out, fashion". Rendering as shape preserves the verb's distinctness without inserting a metaphysical claim either way. The verb returns in v21 (sea-creatures) and v27 (humankind) — three categorical moments out of dozens of creative acts in the chapter.

Elohim is grammatically masculine plural (-im ending). The singular "God" is a theological smoothing inherited from the LXX's choice to render with singular Θεός. The plural form is preserved untranslated. Plural verbs and pronouns governing the Elohim are flagged where they appear (see v26).

Shamayim is grammatically dual (the -ayim ending). Hebrew has no singular form for sky/heaven. Skies preserves the dual. Eretz covers a spectrum from "patch of ground" to "inhabited territory". The capital-E "Earth" (the planet) is a 17th-century English meaning the Hebrew doesn't carry. Land is closer to the register.

2
—the land being wasteland and emptiness, with darkness over the face of the deep, and the breath of the Elohim hovering over the face of the waters—
וְהָאָ֗רֶץ הָיְתָ֥ה תֹ֙הוּ֙ וָבֹ֔הוּ וְחֹ֖שֶׁךְ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י תְה֑וֹם וְר֣וּחַ אֱלֹהִ֔ים מְרַחֶ֖פֶת עַל־פְּנֵ֥י הַמָּֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The single most contested choice in this chapter.

Ruach Elohim — רוּחַ has three lexical fields: breath, wind, spirit. Hebrew does not disambiguate; context is the only guide. KJV/ASV's "Spirit of God" is a Trinitarian-tradition reading retrojected onto Gen 1:2; the Hebrew supports "wind" (NJPS: "a wind from God"), "breath", or "spirit" equally. The verb that follows — merachefet — specifically denotes hovering/fluttering (cf. Deut 32:11, an eagle hovering over its young). Wind hovers; breath hovers; Spirit doesn't, in any natural register. The WoH choice is breath of the Elohim hovering, preserving the verb's bird-image and the verse's physical/atmospheric register without importing a Trinitarian doctrinal claim.

Tohu vavohu — KJV "without form and void" reads philosophical (Aristotelian matter without form); the Hebrew is concrete. The pair appears together in Jer 4:23 describing a post-disaster depopulated landscape, and in Isa 34:11 (similar). Wasteland and emptiness preserves the alliterative pair (the -ohu / -ohu echo) and the concrete imagery.

Tehom is etymologically and conceptually cognate with Akkadian tiāmtu — the same root behind Tiamat of the Enuma Elish, where the goddess is the primordial salt-sea. Genesis has demythologized the term but preserved the word. The link is recorded in the glossary; the translation itself stays measured ("the deep").

3
The Elohim said, "Let there be light"; and there was light.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֖ים יְהִ֣י א֑וֹר וַֽיְהִי־אֽוֹר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Under the construct reading begun in v1, v3 is the main clause of the v1–v3 temporal structure: When the Elohim began to shape ... the land being wasteland and emptiness ... the Elohim said, "Let there be light". The verse-initial "And" of older English translations is dropped — this is the first clause, not a continuation.

4
And the Elohim saw that the light was good; and the Elohim separated between the light and the darkness.
וַיַּ֧רְא אֱלֹהִ֛ים אֶת־הָא֖וֹר כִּי־ט֑וֹב וַיַּבְדֵּ֣ל אֱלֹהִ֔ים בֵּ֥ין הָא֖וֹר וּבֵ֥ין הַחֹֽשֶׁךְ׃
5
And the Elohim called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And there was evening and there was morning, day one.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א אֱלֹהִ֤ים ׀ לָאוֹר֙ י֔וֹם וְלַחֹ֖שֶׁךְ קָ֣רָא לָ֑יְלָה וַֽיְהִי־עֶ֥רֶב וַֽיְהִי־בֹ֖קֶר י֥וֹם אֶחָֽד׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Yom echad — day one, cardinal echad, not the ordinal rishon. Hebrew uses the cardinal uniquely for day one, then switches to ordinals (sheni, shlishi, …) for days 2–6. Most English translations smooth to "first day" / "a second day" which loses the deliberate signaling. The numbered-day pattern in Hebrew is bookended: cardinal echad opens, definite ha-shishi ("the sixth day") closes (v31).

6
And the Elohim said, "Let there be a dome in the midst of the waters, and let it separate between waters and waters."
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֔ים יְהִ֥י רָקִ֖יעַ בְּת֣וֹךְ הַמָּ֑יִם וִיהִ֣י מַבְדִּ֔יל בֵּ֥ין מַ֖יִם לָמָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Raqia derives from the verb רקע — to hammer out (a metal sheet). The ANE cosmology is a solid vault hammered out across the sky to hold back the upper waters (cf. v6–7, where the raqia is explicitly placed between waters and waters). The LXX renders στερέωμα ("solid/firm thing"), whence Vulgate firmamentum, whence English firmament. Modern translations soften further to "expanse" (ESV, NIV), which papers over the solid-dome cosmology. The WoH choice is dome — accurate to the Hebrew picture, honest about the cosmology, and lets the reader make their own peace with the picture.

7
And the Elohim made the dome, and separated between the waters which were under the dome and the waters which were above the dome. And it was so.
וַיַּ֣עַשׂ אֱלֹהִים֮ אֶת־הָרָקִיעַ֒ וַיַּבְדֵּ֗ל בֵּ֤ין הַמַּ֙יִם֙ אֲשֶׁר֙ מִתַּ֣חַת לָרָקִ֔יעַ וּבֵ֣ין הַמַּ֔יִם אֲשֶׁ֖ר מֵעַ֣ל לָרָקִ֑יעַ וַֽיְהִי־כֵֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

Verb pattern point. The dome is made (asah), not shaped (bara). The chapter alternates the verbs deliberately: bara is reserved for v1 (skies and land), v21 (the unprecedented sea-creatures), and v27 (humankind). Everything else — including the dome here — is asah. Most English translations interchange the two verbs freely; preserving them apart surfaces the deliberate categorical work the chapter is doing.

8
And the Elohim called the dome Skies. And there was evening and there was morning, day two.
וַיִּקְרָ֧א אֱלֹהִ֛ים לָֽרָקִ֖יעַ שָׁמָ֑יִם וַֽיְהִי־עֶ֥רֶב וַֽיְהִי־בֹ֖קֶר י֥וֹם שֵׁנִֽי׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Note the absence of "the Elohim saw that it was good" on day 2. This is a textual feature, not an omission, and has drawn rabbinic and modern scholarly comment for centuries. The standard explanations: (1) the project of separating the waters is not complete until day 3, when the dry land appears, so the "good" lands then (v10 closes day 2 and day 3's first work with a single "good"); (2) day 2's work is structural plumbing, not a final result. The omission is deliberate and intelligible. The translation preserves the absence without comment in the verse; the lens surfaces it here.

9
And the Elohim said, "Let the waters under the skies be gathered to one place, and let the dry ground appear." And it was so.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים יִקָּו֨וּ הַמַּ֜יִם מִתַּ֤חַת הַשָּׁמַ֙יִם֙ אֶל־מָק֣וֹם אֶחָ֔ד וְתֵרָאֶ֖ה הַיַּבָּשָׁ֑ה וַֽיְהִי־כֵֽן׃
10
And the Elohim called the dry ground Land, and the gathering of the waters he called Seas. And the Elohim saw that it was good.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א אֱלֹהִ֤ים ׀ לַיַּבָּשָׁה֙ אֶ֔רֶץ וּלְמִקְוֵ֥ה הַמַּ֖יִם קָרָ֣א יַמִּ֑ים וַיַּ֥רְא אֱלֹהִ֖ים כִּי־טֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Capitalized Land here is a proper name (the Elohim is naming the dry ground), parallel to Seas. This is a naming moment within the chapter, distinct from the default-register eretz = "land" elsewhere.

11
And the Elohim said, "Let the land sprout vegetation: seed-bearing plants, and fruit trees making fruit according to their kind, in which is their seed, upon the land." And it was so.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים תַּֽדְשֵׁ֤א הָאָ֙רֶץ֙ דֶּ֔שֶׁא עֵ֚שֶׂב מַזְרִ֣יעַ זֶ֔רַע עֵ֣ץ פְּרִ֞י עֹ֤שֶׂה פְּרִי֙ לְמִינ֔וֹ אֲשֶׁ֥ר זַרְעוֹ־ב֖וֹ עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַֽיְהִי־כֵֽן׃
12
And the land brought forth vegetation: seed-bearing plants according to their kind, and trees making fruit in which is their seed according to their kind. And the Elohim saw that it was good.
וַתּוֹצֵ֨א הָאָ֜רֶץ דֶּ֠שֶׁא עֵ֣שֶׂב מַזְרִ֤יעַ זֶ֙רַע֙ לְמִינֵ֔הוּ וְעֵ֧ץ עֹֽשֶׂה־פְּרִ֛י אֲשֶׁ֥ר זַרְעוֹ־ב֖וֹ לְמִינֵ֑הוּ וַיַּ֥רְא אֱלֹהִ֖ים כִּי־טֽוֹב׃
13
And there was evening and there was morning, day three.
וַֽיְהִי־עֶ֥רֶב וַֽיְהִי־בֹ֖קֶר י֥וֹם שְׁלִישִֽׁי׃ (פ)
14
And the Elohim said, "Let there be luminaries in the dome of the skies to separate between the day and the night; and let them be for signs and for appointed times, and for days and years;
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים יְהִ֤י מְאֹרֹת֙ בִּרְקִ֣יעַ הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם לְהַבְדִּ֕יל בֵּ֥ין הַיּ֖וֹם וּבֵ֣ין הַלָּ֑יְלָה וְהָי֤וּ לְאֹתֹת֙ וּלְמ֣וֹעֲדִ֔ים וּלְיָמִ֖ים וְשָׁנִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Meorot — luminaries, not "lights". The Hebrew is a more technical word than or (light): it denotes light-source objects. The plural form returns through v14–v16 as the cosmos-organizing category that the chapter is positioning here.

Moadim — appointed times, not "seasons". The Hebrew has a technical calendrical sense — fixed times, festival appointments, predetermined astronomical occasions. The KJV/ASV "seasons" reads natural-cycles (spring/summer/autumn/winter); the Hebrew is structural-calendrical. The luminaries are positioned here as functional time-keepers, not as deities.

15
and let them be as luminaries in the dome of the skies, to give light upon the land." And it was so.
וְהָי֤וּ לִמְאוֹרֹת֙ בִּרְקִ֣יעַ הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם לְהָאִ֖יר עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַֽיְהִי־כֵֽן׃
16
And the Elohim made the two great luminaries: the greater luminary for the governance of the day, and the lesser luminary for the governance of the night; and the stars.
וַיַּ֣עַשׂ אֱלֹהִ֔ים אֶת־שְׁנֵ֥י הַמְּאֹרֹ֖ת הַגְּדֹלִ֑ים אֶת־הַמָּא֤וֹר הַגָּדֹל֙ לְמֶמְשֶׁ֣לֶת הַיּ֔וֹם וְאֶת־הַמָּא֤וֹר הַקָּטֹן֙ לְמֶמְשֶׁ֣לֶת הַלַּ֔יְלָה וְאֵ֖ת הַכּוֹכָבִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The most polemically charged verse in the chapter. Hebrew has perfectly good words for sun (shemesh) and moon (yareach) and they are not used. Both terms were divinized in surrounding ANE traditions — Shamash (the sun god of Babylon, Sippar, Larsa), Sin (the Sumero-Akkadian moon god of Ur, Harran). Genesis 1 demythologizes them by naming them only by function: the greater luminary to govern day, the lesser luminary to govern night. The stars get one terminal half-line, almost as an afterthought ("and the stars"). The deliberate refusal of personal names is the polemic. Preserving "luminary" rather than smoothing back to "sun/moon" is non-optional under the WoH lens.

Memshelet — governance, a relatively technical political term ("dominion over a territory", "jurisdiction"), not just "rule". The luminaries are granted functional jurisdiction over day and night — the cosmos is being organized into administrative compartments assigned to functional bodies.

17
And the Elohim set them in the dome of the skies to give light upon the land,
וַיִּתֵּ֥ן אֹתָ֛ם אֱלֹהִ֖ים בִּרְקִ֣יעַ הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם לְהָאִ֖יר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
18
and to rule in the day and in the night, and to separate between the light and the darkness. And the Elohim saw that it was good.
וְלִמְשֹׁל֙ בַּיּ֣וֹם וּבַלַּ֔יְלָה וּֽלֲהַבְדִּ֔יל בֵּ֥ין הָא֖וֹר וּבֵ֣ין הַחֹ֑שֶׁךְ וַיַּ֥רְא אֱלֹהִ֖ים כִּי־טֽוֹב׃
19
And there was evening and there was morning, day four.
וַֽיְהִי־עֶ֥רֶב וַֽיְהִי־בֹ֖קֶר י֥וֹם רְבִיעִֽי׃ (פ)
20
And the Elohim said, "Let the waters swarm with swarms of living beings, and let birds fly above the land across the face of the dome of the skies."
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֔ים יִשְׁרְצ֣וּ הַמַּ֔יִם שֶׁ֖רֶץ נֶ֣פֶשׁ חַיָּ֑ה וְעוֹף֙ יְעוֹפֵ֣ף עַל־הָאָ֔רֶץ עַל־פְּנֵ֖י רְקִ֥יעַ הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Nefesh chayah — living being, not "living creature" or "living soul". Nefesh is famously hard: its lexical core is breath / throat / self / life, not a Platonic-detachable soul. The KJV "soul" (in Gen 2:7 for ha-adam) is the retrojection to avoid; modern Hebrew Bible studies consensus is firmly "being" or "creature". The translation uses living being consistently for nefesh chayah.

21
And the Elohim shaped the great sea-creatures, and every living being that moves, with which the waters swarmed, according to their kinds, and every winged bird according to its kind. And the Elohim saw that it was good.
וַיִּבְרָ֣א אֱלֹהִ֔ים אֶת־הַתַּנִּינִ֖ם הַגְּדֹלִ֑ים וְאֵ֣ת כָּל־נֶ֣פֶשׁ הַֽחַיָּ֣ה ׀ הָֽרֹמֶ֡שֶׂת אֲשֶׁר֩ שָׁרְצ֨וּ הַמַּ֜יִם לְמִֽינֵהֶ֗ם וְאֵ֨ת כָּל־ע֤וֹף כָּנָף֙ לְמִינֵ֔הוּ וַיַּ֥רְא אֱלֹהִ֖ים כִּי־טֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The verb returns. The sea-creatures are shaped (bara), not made (asah). Why? Because tanninim (sea-monsters / great sea-creatures) appears elsewhere in mythological contexts (Job 7:12, Isa 27:1, Ps 74:13) where it denotes rival sea-dragon figures. The deliberate use of bara here is a demythologizing move — these are made by the Elohim, not rivals to it. Preserving bara as shape lets the reader see the verb pattern across v1 → v21 → v27.

Taninim — great sea-creatures. KJV "whales" is a domesticating mistranslation that loses the mythological-cousin register entirely; modern translations correctly retain "sea-monsters" or "great sea-creatures". The lens does not need to commit to the mythological reading here; the text has already demythologized.

22
And the Elohim blessed them, saying, "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the waters in the seas; and let the birds multiply on the land."
וַיְבָ֧רֶךְ אֹתָ֛ם אֱלֹהִ֖ים לֵאמֹ֑ר פְּר֣וּ וּרְב֗וּ וּמִלְא֤וּ אֶת־הַמַּ֙יִם֙ בַּיַּמִּ֔ים וְהָע֖וֹף יִ֥רֶב בָּאָֽרֶץ׃
23
And there was evening and there was morning, day five.
וַֽיְהִי־עֶ֥רֶב וַֽיְהִי־בֹ֖קֶר י֥וֹם חֲמִישִֽׁי׃ (פ)
24
And the Elohim said, "Let the land bring forth living beings according to their kind: cattle, and creeping things, and beasts of the land according to their kind." And it was so.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים תּוֹצֵ֨א הָאָ֜רֶץ נֶ֤פֶשׁ חַיָּה֙ לְמִינָ֔הּ בְּהֵמָ֥ה וָרֶ֛מֶשׂ וְחַֽיְתוֹ־אֶ֖רֶץ לְמִינָ֑הּ וַֽיְהִי־כֵֽן׃
25
And the Elohim made the beasts of the land according to their kind, and the cattle according to their kind, and every creeping thing of the ground according to its kind. And the Elohim saw that it was good.
וַיַּ֣עַשׂ אֱלֹהִים֩ אֶת־חַיַּ֨ת הָאָ֜רֶץ לְמִינָ֗הּ וְאֶת־הַבְּהֵמָה֙ לְמִינָ֔הּ וְאֵ֛ת כָּל־רֶ֥מֶשׂ הָֽאֲדָמָ֖ה לְמִינֵ֑הוּ וַיַּ֥רְא אֱלֹהִ֖ים כִּי־טֽוֹב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Verb pattern note. Land animals are made (asah), not shaped (bara). The chapter has now used bara twice (v1, v21) and asah many times. The third and final cluster of bara arrives in v27 for humankind.

26
And the Elohim said, "Let us make humankind in our image, according to our likeness; and let them rule over the fish of the sea, and over the birds of the skies, and over the cattle, and over all the land, and over every creeping thing that creeps upon the land."
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֔ים נַֽעֲשֶׂ֥ה אָדָ֛ם בְּצַלְמֵ֖נוּ כִּדְמוּתֵ֑נוּ וְיִרְדּוּ֩ בִדְגַ֨ת הַיָּ֜ם וּבְע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֗יִם וּבַבְּהֵמָה֙ וּבְכָל־הָאָ֔רֶץ וּבְכָל־הָרֶ֖מֶשׂ הָֽרֹמֵ֥שׂ עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

The central textual datum of the chapter, and the most-cited verse of the chapter for the WoH lens. Na'aseh is the cohortative first-person plural of asah — let us make. It is grammatically unambiguous in Hebrew. The pronouns and possessives that follow are also plural: b'tsalmenu ("in our image"), ki-dmutenu ("according to our likeness").

Standard theological glosses include:

  1. Plural of majesty — the royal "we". Ad hoc and not well-attested elsewhere in Hebrew for divine speech; the strongest objection is that Elohim never elsewhere governs first-person plural verbs of self-deliberation. Several Jewish commentators (Ibn Ezra, others) have endorsed this read but the textual support is weak.
  2. Trinitarian pre-figuring — Christian retrojection; not a Hebrew-internal reading.
  3. Divine-council — the modern scholarly consensus. the Elohim deliberates with attendant divine beings (cf. Job 1:6, Ps 82, 1 Kgs 22:19–22). The council reading reads the plural at face value but diffuses the agency.
  4. Literal plural agency — the WoH lens reading. the Elohim deliberate as a plural agent in their own right.

The translation does not adjudicate. It preserves the grammar. The lens reading is one of the two textually defensible readings (alongside divine-council), and is the one the rest of the WoH corpus operates on.

Adam → humankind, not "man". Ha-adam is a category here, not a personal name (the named individual Adam appears later, in Gen 2–5). Gendered "man" is corrected (NJPS, NRSV both render "humankind"). The naming wordplay with adamah ("ground") will surface in Gen 2.

27
And the Elohim shaped humankind in his image; in the image of the Elohim he shaped him; male and female he shaped them.
וַיִּבְרָ֨א אֱלֹהִ֤ים ׀ אֶת־הָֽאָדָם֙ בְּצַלְמ֔וֹ בְּצֶ֥לֶם אֱלֹהִ֖ים בָּרָ֣א אֹת֑וֹ זָכָ֥ר וּנְקֵבָ֖ה בָּרָ֥א אֹתָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The verb pattern peaks: bara three times in one verse. Humankind is shaped (bara), not just made (asah). The triple bara — once for the act, once for the image-specification, once for the male-and-female dimorphism — bookends the verb pattern of the chapter: v1 (skies and land), v21 (sea-creatures), v27 (humankind ×3). Categorical creation moments only.

The plural-singular shift v26 → v27. v26 has plural "Let us make … in our image" (na'aseh … b'tsalmenu), v27 has singular "in his image" (b'tsalmo) and singular subject bara ×3. The shift is textually real and the lens must address it. The natural reading: deliberative project speech (plural) → executing agency assignment (singular) within the Elohim group. The text supports a divine-council reading equally well; neither is forced.

Tselem and demut — image and likeness. The lexical core in Hebrew is physical resemblance:

  • Tselem elsewhere denotes physical statues/representations: Num 33:52 (cast metal images), 1 Sam 6:5 (golden tumours/mice), Ezek 7:20, 16:17 (idol-statues).
  • Demut elsewhere denotes bodily likeness: Ezek 1:5,10 ("the likeness as the appearance of a man"), 2 Kgs 16:10 (the pattern of an altar), 2 Chron 4:3.

Traditional Jewish and Christian readings spiritualize: humankind bears the Elohim's moral or spiritual image. The Hebrew lexicon supports physical resemblance directly, without interpretive extension. The lens reading is that the lexical core is the load-bearing data; the spiritualizing readings are extensions that may or may not be doing additional work.

28
And the Elohim blessed them. And the Elohim said to them, "Be fruitful and multiply, and fill the land, and subdue it; and rule over the fish of the sea, and over the birds of the skies, and over every living thing that moves upon the land."
וַיְבָ֣רֶךְ אֹתָם֮ אֱלֹהִים֒ וַיֹּ֨אמֶר לָהֶ֜ם אֱלֹהִ֗ים פְּר֥וּ וּרְב֛וּ וּמִלְא֥וּ אֶת־הָאָ֖רֶץ וְכִבְשֻׁ֑הָ וּרְד֞וּ בִּדְגַ֤ת הַיָּם֙ וּבְע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וּבְכָל־חַיָּ֖ה הָֽרֹמֶ֥שֶׂת עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 28

Kivshuha ("subdue it") and redu ("rule") are strong verbs and not softened. Kavash in the Qal is forceful — "conquer", "bring under". Radah in the Qal denotes dominant rule, occasionally with the spatial sense of treading. Both verbs have been the subject of intense exegetical debate in environmental ethics since Lynn White Jr.'s 1967 thesis ("The Historical Roots of Our Ecologic Crisis"). The WoH translation does not paper over the strength; the interpretive question is a lens question, not a translation one.

29
And the Elohim said, "Behold, I have given you every seed-bearing plant upon the face of all the land, and every tree in which is the fruit of a tree bearing seed; to you it shall be for food.
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים הִנֵּה֩ נָתַ֨תִּי לָכֶ֜ם אֶת־כָּל־עֵ֣שֶׂב ׀ זֹרֵ֣עַ זֶ֗רַע אֲשֶׁר֙ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י כָל־הָאָ֔רֶץ וְאֶת־כָּל־הָעֵ֛ץ אֲשֶׁר־בּ֥וֹ פְרִי־עֵ֖ץ זֹרֵ֣עַ זָ֑רַע לָכֶ֥ם יִֽהְיֶ֖ה לְאָכְלָֽה׃
30
And to every living thing of the land, and to every bird of the skies, and to every thing that moves upon the land in which is a living being—every green plant for food." And it was so.
וּֽלְכָל־חַיַּ֣ת הָ֠אָרֶץ וּלְכָל־ע֨וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֜יִם וּלְכֹ֣ל ׀ רוֹמֵ֣שׂ עַל־הָאָ֗רֶץ אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ֙ נֶ֣פֶשׁ חַיָּ֔ה אֶת־כָּל־יֶ֥רֶק עֵ֖שֶׂב לְאָכְלָ֑ה וַֽיְהִי־כֵֽן׃
31
And the Elohim saw all that he had made, and behold, it was very good. And there was evening and there was morning, the sixth day.
וַיַּ֤רְא אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־כָּל־אֲשֶׁ֣ר עָשָׂ֔ה וְהִנֵּה־ט֖וֹב מְאֹ֑ד וַֽיְהִי־עֶ֥רֶב וַֽיְהִי־בֹ֖קֶר י֥וֹם הַשִּׁשִּֽׁי׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 31

The chapter closes. Yom ha-shishi — the sixth day, with the definite article ha-. Days 2–5 lack the article; day 6 has it. The ordinal pattern with definite-article-on-day-6 marks day 6 as the culminating day, parallel to day one being marked by the cardinal echad. The numbered-day structure is deliberate and bookended. The closing tov me'od ("very good") intensifies the recurring formulaic "good" — six days of tov, peaking on day 6 with tov me'od.

Chapter 2

Genesis 2 — The garden in Eden

1
The skies and the land were completed, and all their array.
וַיְכֻלּ֛וּ הַשָּׁמַ֥יִם וְהָאָ֖רֶץ וְכָל־צְבָאָֽם׃
2
On the seventh day the Elohim completed the work he had made; and he ceased on the seventh day from all the work he had made.
וַיְכַ֤ל אֱלֹהִים֙ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י מְלַאכְתּ֖וֹ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עָשָׂ֑ה וַיִּשְׁבֹּת֙ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י מִכָּל־מְלַאכְתּ֖וֹ אֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשָֽׂה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Vayishbot — he ceased, not he rested. The verb שָׁבַת shabat is the root behind שַׁבָּת shabbat; its primary sense is cessation of activity, not rest in the modern sense of recuperation. The English 'rested' (KJV, ASV) imports a register of fatigue that the Hebrew does not have — the Elohim does not need to recover. The verse is about completion: the work is done, and the activity stops. Sabbath as a structure later acquires the additional meaning of recuperative rest for humans and animals (Ex 20:10, 23:12), but the verb here marks the end of a project, not a recovery from one.

3
The Elohim blessed the seventh day and set it apart, because on it he ceased from all the work which the Elohim had shaped to do.
וַיְבָ֤רֶךְ אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־י֣וֹם הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י וַיְקַדֵּ֖שׁ אֹת֑וֹ כִּ֣י ב֤וֹ שָׁבַת֙ מִכָּל־מְלַאכְתּ֔וֹ אֲשֶׁר־בָּרָ֥א אֱלֹהִ֖ים לַעֲשֽׂוֹת׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Bara ... la'asot — shaped ... to do. The two creation-verbs of chapter 1 reappear together in the closing colon, bracketing the chapter. Bara (the categorical-shaping verb) and asah (the general-making verb) together summarize the seven days. The construction אֲשֶׁר־בָּרָא אֱלֹהִים לַעֲשׂוֹת — literally 'which the Elohim shaped to make' — is grammatically unusual: it doubles up the act of making in a way most translations smooth. Keeping the doubling preserves the chapter's compositional seal.

4
This is the tale of the skies and the land in their being shaped — on the day YHWH-Elohim made land and skies.
אֵ֣לֶּה תוֹלְד֧וֹת הַשָּׁמַ֛יִם וְהָאָ֖רֶץ בְּהִבָּֽרְאָ֑ם בְּי֗וֹם עֲשׂ֛וֹת יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶ֥רֶץ וְשָׁמָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The seam. Verse 4 is the structural and source-critical hinge of Genesis 1–2. Two things happen at once.

Eleh toledot is the formula that recurs eleven more times in Genesis to mark new sections (5:1, 6:9, 10:1, 11:10, 11:27, 25:12, 25:19, 36:1, 36:9, 37:2). From יָלַד 'to give birth', the noun is literally 'begettings'. The translation 'tale' captures the narrative function without smoothing the genealogical force.

At the same instant, the divine name shifts: where chapter 1 spoke only of אֱלֹהִים Elohim, chapter 2 introduces יְהוָה אֱלֹהִים YHWH-Elohim and uses the compound through v22. Source critics read this as the seam between the Priestly account (P, ch. 1:1–2:4a) and the Yahwist account (J, 2:4b–3:24); whatever one makes of the source theory, the vocabulary shift is real and deliberate. WoH leaves both names untranslated: Elohim is plural, YHWH is the unpronounceable Tetragrammaton. Rendering as 'the LORD God' (KJV, ASV) follows the Masoretic Qere-perpetuum convention of reading Adonai for YHWH — a Second Temple-era worship practice, not a translation choice. Restoring YHWH-Elohim restores the seam.

Note also the order reversal in the same verse: 'skies and land' (echoing 1:1) becomes 'land and skies' in the second clause. The Yahwist account is going to focus on the land, and v4 is already gesturing in that direction.

5
Now no bush of the field was yet on the land, and no herb of the field had yet sprouted — for YHWH-Elohim had not made it rain on the land, and there was no human to work the ground.
וְכֹ֣ל ׀ שִׂ֣יחַ הַשָּׂדֶ֗ה טֶ֚רֶם יִֽהְיֶ֣ה בָאָ֔רֶץ וְכָל־עֵ֥שֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶ֖ה טֶ֣רֶם יִצְמָ֑ח כִּי֩ לֹ֨א הִמְטִ֜יר יְהוָ֤ה אֱלֹהִים֙ עַל־הָאָ֔רֶץ וְאָדָ֣ם אַ֔יִן לַֽעֲבֹ֖ד אֶת־הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
6
A stream would rise from the land and water the whole face of the ground.
וְאֵ֖ד יַֽעֲלֶ֣ה מִן־הָאָ֑רֶץ וְהִשְׁקָ֖ה אֶֽת־כָּל־פְּנֵֽי־הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
7
YHWH-Elohim formed the human, dust from the ground, and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life; and the human became a living being.
וַיִּיצֶר֩ יְהוָ֨ה אֱלֹהִ֜ים אֶת־הָֽאָדָ֗ם עָפָר֙ מִן־הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה וַיִּפַּ֥ח בְּאַפָּ֖יו נִשְׁמַ֣ת חַיִּ֑ים וַֽיְהִ֥י הָֽאָדָ֖ם לְנֶ֥פֶשׁ חַיָּֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

Four load-bearing choices in one verse.

Vayyitser — formed. יָצַר yatsar is the third of Genesis' creation verbs, alongside בָּרָא bara (ch. 1:1, 'shape') and עָשָׂה asah ('make'). The image is the potter at the wheel: hands working clay. Same root as יוֹצֵר yotser 'potter' (Jer 18:2-6). Standard translations collapse all three verbs into 'made/created'; preserving the distinction surfaces what the text is actually saying: this is hands-on craft, not categorical fiat.

Ha-adam ... afar min ha-adamah — the human, dust from the ground. The triple-link adam / adamah / afar is the verse's etymological signature. Adam is not yet a personal name — the definite article הָ ha- marks it as a category-noun, 'the human' (the proper name doesn't appear unmarked until 4:25). Adamah is the ground he is made from. Afar is the dust of that ground. The picture is geological and material: the human is the one from the ground. Rendering as 'Adam' (KJV, ASV) loses the category-noun and the wordplay both. WoH keeps the human anonymous and earth-bound until the text itself names him.

Nishmat chayim — breath of life. The Hebrew has three distinct breath-words. רוּחַ ruach (1:2) covered wind, breath, spirit. נֶפֶשׁ nefesh (1:20, 24, 30) covered living-being, throat, appetite. נְשָׁמָה neshamah is the third: specifically exhaled breath, what is breathed out. The verb here is נָפַח napach 'to blow', mouth-to-nostril — the craftsman animating his work. The three lexemes are not synonyms; the chapter 1 creator who spoke light into being is now a chapter 2 creator who breathes life into a clay figure.

Nefesh chayah — living being. The phrase that named the animals of 1:20–24 names the human here too. The human is not a different kind of being than the animals at this point in the narrative; he is nefesh chayah like them — except that the neshamah came directly mouth-to-nostril from YHWH-Elohim. The differentiation is the breath, not the category.

8
YHWH-Elohim planted a garden in Eden, eastward — and there he placed the human he had formed.
וַיִּטַּ֞ע יְהוָ֧ה אֱלֹהִ֛ים גַּן־בְעֵ֖דֶן מִקֶּ֑דֶם וַיָּ֣שֶׂם שָׁ֔ם אֶת־הָֽאָדָ֖ם אֲשֶׁ֥ר יָצָֽר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

Gan b'eden miqedem — a garden in Eden, eastward. Two adjustments. First, the preposition בְּ b- 'in', not the construct relation that would give 'of': Eden is a region, the garden is within it. KJV/ASV 'Garden of Eden' has hardened into proper-noun usage, but the Hebrew is 'a garden in [the place called] Eden'. Second, miqedem — מִקֶּדֶם — is ambiguous between eastward (spatially, in the east) and of old / from ancient times (temporally). LXX read temporally (κατὰ ἀνατολάς can carry either sense; the LXX translators glossed it spatially). Most modern translations read spatially; the temporal reading would push the garden into a primordial-time register. WoH takes the spatial reading as the dominant sense while letting the verse's poetry carry the second register.

9
YHWH-Elohim caused every tree desirable to the sight and good for food to sprout from the ground — and within the garden, the tree of life and the tree of knowing good and bad.
וַיַּצְמַ֞ח יְהוָ֤ה אֱלֹהִים֙ מִן־הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה כָּל־עֵ֛ץ נֶחְמָ֥ד לְמַרְאֶ֖ה וְט֣וֹב לְמַאֲכָ֑ל וְעֵ֤ץ הַֽחַיִּים֙ בְּת֣וֹךְ הַגָּ֔ן וְעֵ֕ץ הַדַּ֖עַת ט֥וֹב וָרָֽע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

Etz hada'at tov vara — the tree of knowing good and bad. Two notes. First, דַּעַת da'at is experiential knowing — the same verb covers intimate acquaintance (Gen 4:1: 'and the human knew Eve his wife'), not abstract cognition. 'Knowing' rather than 'knowledge' keeps the verbal force. Second, tov vara is the Hebrew merism for everything within a range — Solomon's request in 1 Kgs 3:9 for the ability to discern tov vara reads as 'sound judgment across the spectrum'. The pair is closer to 'good and bad' (the full practical-moral range) than to 'good and evil' (Manichaean polarity). The tree is not the tree of metaphysical morality but the tree of experiential acquaintance with the full range of what is.

10
A river went out from Eden to water the garden; from there it divided and became four head-streams.
וְנָהָר֙ יֹצֵ֣א מֵעֵ֔דֶן לְהַשְׁק֖וֹת אֶת־הַגָּ֑ן וּמִשָּׁם֙ יִפָּרֵ֔ד וְהָיָ֖ה לְאַרְבָּעָ֥ה רָאשִֽׁים׃
11
The first is named Pishon; it is the one encircling all the land of Havilah, where the gold is.
שֵׁ֥ם הָֽאֶחָ֖ד פִּישׁ֑וֹן ה֣וּא הַסֹּבֵ֗ב אֵ֚ת כָּל־אֶ֣רֶץ הַֽחֲוִילָ֔ה אֲשֶׁר־שָׁ֖ם הַזָּהָֽב׃
12
The gold of that land is good; there is bdellium and the shoham stone.
וּֽזֲהַ֛ב הָאָ֥רֶץ הַהִ֖וא ט֑וֹב שָׁ֥ם הַבְּדֹ֖לַח וְאֶ֥בֶן הַשֹּֽׁהַם׃
13
The name of the second river is Gihon; it is the one encircling all the land of Kush.
וְשֵֽׁם־הַנָּהָ֥ר הַשֵּׁנִ֖י גִּיח֑וֹן ה֣וּא הַסּוֹבֵ֔ב אֵ֖ת כָּל־אֶ֥רֶץ כּֽוּשׁ׃
14
The name of the third river is Hiddekel; it is the one flowing east of Ashur. And the fourth river is the Perat.
וְשֵׁ֨ם הַנָּהָ֤ר הַשְּׁלִישִׁי֙ חִדֶּ֔קֶל ה֥וּא הַֽהֹלֵ֖ךְ קִדְמַ֣ת אַשּׁ֑וּר וְהַנָּהָ֥ר הָֽרְבִיעִ֖י ה֥וּא פְרָֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Two known, two unknown. Chiddekel is the Tigris (Aramaic and Akkadian cognates both transparent). Perat is the Euphrates. The third and fourth rivers anchor the geography in Mesopotamia. The first two — Pishon and Gichon — are unidentified: Havilah and Kush are real places (Arabia and Nubia, respectively, in the Hebrew Bible's wider geography), but no river connects them to the Tigris-Euphrates headwaters in any reading of ancient geography. The mismatch is structural to the passage: two rivers in the world we can map, two in a world we cannot. The garden sits where the four streams meet.

15
YHWH-Elohim took the human and set him in the garden of Eden to work it and to guard it.
וַיִּקַּ֛ח יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶת־הָֽאָדָ֑ם וַיַּנִּחֵ֣הוּ בְגַן־עֵ֔דֶן לְעָבְדָ֖הּ וּלְשָׁמְרָֽהּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

Le-ovdah ul-shomrah — to work it and to guard it. The two verbs are עָבַד avad ('work, serve, till') and שָׁמַר shamar ('keep, guard, watch over'). The human is positioned as worker-and-watchman, not as owner. Both verb-suffixes are feminine (-ah), while גַּן gan 'garden' is masculine — the antecedent is likely אֲדָמָה adamah (feminine), making the stewardship language reach beyond the gardened parcel to the ground itself. KJV 'to dress it and to keep it' reads horticultural; the Hebrew register is closer to 'till the ground and guard it' — agricultural and custodial.

16
YHWH-Elohim commanded the human, saying, "From every tree of the garden you may freely eat;
וַיְצַו֙ יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֔ים עַל־הָֽאָדָ֖ם לֵאמֹ֑ר מִכֹּ֥ל עֵֽץ־הַגָּ֖ן אָכֹ֥ל תֹּאכֵֽל׃
17
but from the tree of knowing good and bad — you shall not eat from it. For on the day you eat from it, you shall surely die."
וּמֵעֵ֗ץ הַדַּ֙עַת֙ ט֣וֹב וָרָ֔ע לֹ֥א תֹאכַ֖ל מִמֶּ֑נּוּ כִּ֗י בְּי֛וֹם אֲכָלְךָ֥ מִמֶּ֖נּוּ מ֥וֹת תָּמֽוּת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Mot tamut — you shall surely die. The infinitive absolute מוֹת mot before the finite imperfect תָּמוּת tamut intensifies the verb: 'dying you will die' is the construction. The same form appears in v16 with אָכֹל תֹּאכֵל akhol tokhel ('you may freely eat') — permission and prohibition framed in matching grammar. What the construction settles is certainty of consequence. What it leaves open is kind of death: same-day biological death (which does not happen in the narrative), or mortality entering (a death-bearing transformation, which does), or expulsion from the unfallen state (which also does). The Hebrew picks none; the chapter saves its hand for later.

18
YHWH-Elohim said, "It is not good for the human to be alone — I will make for him a counterpart fitting him."
וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֔ים לֹא־ט֛וֹב הֱי֥וֹת הָֽאָדָ֖ם לְבַדּ֑וֹ אֶֽעֱשֶׂהּ־לּ֥וֹ עֵ֖זֶר כְּנֶגְדּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

Ezer kenegdo — a counterpart fitting him. Two of the most often-mistranslated words in the Hebrew Bible.

Ezer is not 'helper-subordinate'. The same noun is used 16 times in the Hebrew Bible for divine help: Pss 33:20, 70:5, 121:1-2 ('My help comes from YHWH'), Deut 33:7, 33:26, 33:29. The semantic field is substantial aid — sometimes critical, sometimes salvific. The 'helpmeet' English tradition (KJV 'help meet for him' → contraction → 'helpmate') has slid the word down into junior-partner territory in a way the Hebrew never supports.

Kenegdo is from נֶגֶד neged 'opposite, in front of, corresponding to'. The relation is face-to-face equivalence, not behind-and-supporting. The two words together describe a counterpart of matching standing.

'A counterpart fitting him' tries to recover both senses: equal standing (kenegdo) and substantive aid (ezer). The verse declares the human's solitude not good — the only thing in the Genesis creation accounts so labeled. The remedy is not subordination but symmetry.

19
YHWH-Elohim formed from the ground every wild animal of the field and every bird of the skies, and brought them to the human to see what he would call them; and whatever the human called each living being, that became its name.
וַיִּצֶר֩ יְהוָ֨ה אֱלֹהִ֜ים מִן־הָֽאֲדָמָ֗ה כָּל־חַיַּ֤ת הַשָּׂדֶה֙ וְאֵת֙ כָּל־ע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וַיָּבֵא֙ אֶל־הָ֣אָדָ֔ם לִרְא֖וֹת מַה־יִּקְרָא־ל֑וֹ וְכֹל֩ אֲשֶׁ֨ר יִקְרָא־ל֧וֹ הָֽאָדָ֛ם נֶ֥פֶשׁ חַיָּ֖ה ה֥וּא שְׁמֽוֹ׃
20
The human gave names to all the cattle, to the birds of the skies, and to every wild animal of the field — but for the human himself no counterpart fitting him was found.
וַיִּקְרָ֨א הָֽאָדָ֜ם שֵׁמ֗וֹת לְכָל־הַבְּהֵמָה֙ וּלְע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וּלְכֹ֖ל חַיַּ֣ת הַשָּׂדֶ֑ה וּלְאָדָ֕ם לֹֽא־מָצָ֥א עֵ֖זֶר כְּנֶגְדּֽוֹ׃
21
YHWH-Elohim caused a deep sleep to fall upon the human, and while he slept, he took one of his sides and closed up the flesh in its place.
וַיַּפֵּל֩ יְהוָ֨ה אֱלֹהִ֧ים ׀ תַּרְדֵּמָ֛ה עַל־הָאָדָ֖ם וַיִּישָׁ֑ן וַיִּקַּ֗ח אַחַת֙ מִצַּלְעֹתָ֔יו וַיִּסְגֹּ֥ר בָּשָׂ֖ר תַּחְתֶּֽנָּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

Two corrections.

Tardemah is not ordinary sleep (שֵׁנָה shenah). It denotes a divinely-induced trance — the same word falls on Abram during the covenant theophany of Gen 15:12, and recurs in Job 4:13, 33:15, Isa 29:10. The verb הִפִּיל hippil 'caused to fall' is causative: YHWH-Elohim makes the trance descend. The register is closer to anaesthetic or oneiric than to natural slumber.

Tzela — side, not rib. The noun appears 41 times in the Hebrew Bible. It is the architectural term for the side of structures — the side of the Ark (Ex 25:12), the Tabernacle (Ex 26:20), the altar (Ex 27:7), the Temple (1 Kgs 6:34). It never means 'rib' outside this passage's traditional reading. The LXX rendered πλευρά 'side'; the Vulgate's costa is itself ambiguous in classical Latin. 'Rib' is medieval anatomical guesswork. Reading the Hebrew straight, YHWH-Elohim takes one side of the human — not a single bone — and from that side builds the counterpart. The picture is symmetrical: a whole divided into two halves, which v23 ('bone of my bones, flesh of my flesh') and v24 ('one flesh') reinforce as halves rejoined.

22
YHWH-Elohim built the side he had taken from the human into a woman, and brought her to the human.
וַיִּבֶן֩ יְהוָ֨ה אֱלֹהִ֧ים ׀ אֶֽת־הַצֵּלָ֛ע אֲשֶׁר־לָקַ֥ח מִן־הָֽאָדָ֖ם לְאִשָּׁ֑ה וַיְבִאֶ֖הָ אֶל־הָֽאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

Vayyiven — built. The verb is בָּנָה banah 'to build', the architectural verb used for cities (Gen 4:17), towers (11:4), altars (8:20), houses (Deut 25:9). It is not יָצַר yatsar 'form' (the potter's verb of v7) or עָשָׂה asah 'make'. Chapter 2 has now deployed four distinct creation verbs: bara (1:1), asah (1:7, 1:16), yatsar (2:7, 2:19), and now banah. The choice signals an act of construction — the woman is built like a structure, from a structural component (the side). The traditional rendering 'made' or 'fashioned' (KJV, ASV) collapses this distinction. Restoring 'built' preserves the architectural register and the verb-pattern.

23
The human said, "This one at last is bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh — this one shall be called *Ishah* [Woman], for from *Ish* [Man] this one was taken."
וַיֹּאמֶר֮ הָֽאָדָם֒ זֹ֣את הַפַּ֗עַם עֶ֚צֶם מֵֽעֲצָמַ֔י וּבָשָׂ֖ר מִבְּשָׂרִ֑י לְזֹאת֙ יִקָּרֵ֣א אִשָּׁ֔ה כִּ֥י מֵאִ֖ישׁ לֻֽקֳחָה־זֹּֽאת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

Zot hapa'am — this one at last. The phrase carries relief, recognition: after the animals failed to provide a counterpart (v20), the human encounters something matching. Hapa'am — 'this time' — marks a turn.

Ish / Ishah — the wordplay turns on sound, not etymology. The two words come from different roots (ish from a root meaning 'man, mortal'; ishah from אנשׁ), but the consonantal near-identity carries the naming. Most English translations capitalize ('Woman ... Man') to signal that v23 is a naming event, parallel to the namings of v19–20. WoH retains the Hebrew transliterations Ishah and Ish in italics in this verse to preserve the soundplay that drives the naming.

24
Therefore a man shall leave his father and his mother and cling to his wife, and they shall become one flesh.
עַל־כֵּן֙ יַֽעֲזָב־אִ֔ישׁ אֶת־אָבִ֖יו וְאֶת־אִמּ֑וֹ וְדָבַ֣ק בְּאִשְׁתּ֔וֹ וְהָי֖וּ לְבָשָׂ֥ר אֶחָֽד׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

V'hayu l'vasar echad — they shall become one flesh. The phrase closes the narrative arc of v21–22. The human was divided: one side taken from him, the flesh closed up in its place. The verse-narrator's aside in v24 reads that division as the etiology of marriage — the marital bond is the re-unification of what was structurally divided. בָּשָׂר basar covers body, kin, common substance; basar echad covers both physical union and kinship-by-substance.

The verse breaks the narrative voice — neither YHWH-Elohim nor the human is speaking; this is the editor stepping forward to comment. Such asides are rare in Genesis; this one frames the entire chapter.

25
The two of them were naked, the human and his woman, and they felt no shame.
וַיִּֽהְי֤וּ שְׁנֵיהֶם֙ עֲרוּמִּ֔ים הָֽאָדָ֖ם וְאִשְׁתּ֑וֹ וְלֹ֖א יִתְבֹּשָֽׁשׁוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Arummim — naked. The chapter closes on a single sentence whose key word is set up to detonate in the next chapter. עֲרוּמִּים arummim 'naked' (plural of עָרוֹם arom) shares a consonantal skeleton with עָרוּם arum 'shrewd, crafty' — the very word that opens Gen 3:1, describing the serpent. The two words are not etymologically the same root, but the sound-play is unmistakable in Hebrew and almost certainly deliberate: nakedness without shame in 2:25 ↔ shrewdness without disclosure in 3:1. The chapter ends with the two unashamed and the serpent unintroduced; the soundplay binds the two verses across the chapter break. English translations cannot replicate the pun, but the commentary preserves it.

Chapter 3

Genesis 3 — The serpent and the expulsion

1
Now the serpent was shrewder than any wild animal of the field that YHWH-Elohim had made. He said to the woman, "Has the Elohim really said, 'You shall not eat from any tree of the garden'?"
וְהַנָּחָשׁ֙ הָיָ֣ה עָר֔וּם מִכֹּל֙ חַיַּ֣ת הַשָּׂדֶ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשָׂ֖ה יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֑ים וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ אֶל־הָ֣אִשָּׁ֔ה אַ֚ף כִּֽי־אָמַ֣ר אֱלֹהִ֔ים לֹ֣א תֹֽאכְל֔וּ מִכֹּ֖ל עֵ֥ץ הַגָּֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The chapter opens on a sound-bridge. עָרוּם arum ('shrewd') and עֲרוּמִּים arummim ('naked', the closing word of 2:25) share consonants but not roots — the wordplay is sonic, not etymological. Genesis is deliberate: nakedness-without-shame in 2:25 hands directly into shrewdness-without-disclosure in 3:1. The serpent is cleverer than any field-animal — note YHWH-Elohim is named as the maker — and his clever turn is rhetorical: he opens with a question that misquotes the original prohibition. 2:17 said the human (singular) might not eat from one tree; the serpent's question generalizes to any tree. The reframing forces the woman to defend the prohibition — and in defending it, in v3, she adds her own hedge to it. Note also that the serpent uses bare Elohim, not the compound YHWH-Elohim used by the narrator. The personal-name distance is the serpent's first move.

2
The woman said to the serpent, "From the fruit of the trees of the garden we may eat;
וַתֹּ֥אמֶר הָֽאִשָּׁ֖ה אֶל־הַנָּחָ֑שׁ מִפְּרִ֥י עֵֽץ־הַגָּ֖ן נֹאכֵֽל׃
3
but from the fruit of the tree which is in the midst of the garden, the Elohim has said, 'You shall not eat from it, and you shall not touch it, lest you die.'"
וּמִפְּרִ֣י הָעֵץ֮ אֲשֶׁ֣ר בְּתוֹךְ־הַגָּן֒ אָמַ֣ר אֱלֹהִ֗ים לֹ֤א תֹֽאכְלוּ֙ מִמֶּ֔נּוּ וְלֹ֥א תִגְּע֖וּ בּ֑וֹ פֶּן־תְּמֻתֽוּן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Three slips in the woman's quotation. First, and you shall not touch it: nothing in the original prohibition (2:17) addresses touch — only eating. The woman has added a fence to the commandment. Second, lest you die (פֶּן־תְּמֻתֻן pen-temutun): the original was מוֹת תָּמוּת mot tamut 'you shall surely die' — the infinitive-absolute intensifier removed, the certainty softened to a contingent fear. Third, the tree is described only as the one in the midst of the garden — not by its actual identifier (the tree of knowing good and bad). The woman is paraphrasing from a distance, and the serpent will exploit the looseness in the next verse. The original command, of course, was given to the human alone (2:16–17), before the woman was built (2:22) — she received it secondhand.

4
The serpent said to the woman, "You shall surely not die.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר הַנָּחָ֖שׁ אֶל־הָֽאִשָּׁ֑ה לֹֽא־מ֖וֹת תְּמֻתֽוּן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Lo mot temutun — you shall surely not die. The serpent mirrors YHWH-Elohim's grammar exactly: the infinitive absolute מוֹת mot before the finite verb תְּמֻתוּן temutun, intensifying — but with the negation לֹא lo placed before the infinitive absolute itself. The strict grammatical reading is not 'you shall not surely die' (uncertainty about death) but 'you shall surely not die' (certainty about not-death). The serpent is not hedging; he is asserting the opposite certainty. The construction is unusual — placing לֹא before the infinitive absolute is rare and emphatic. The next verse will give the reason: not because death is unreal, but because the consequence YHWH-Elohim named is, the serpent claims, a cover story.

5
For the Elohim knows that on the day you eat from it your eyes will be opened, and you will be like the Elohim, knowing good and bad."
כִּ֚י יֹדֵ֣עַ אֱלֹהִ֔ים כִּ֗י בְּיוֹם֙ אֲכָלְכֶ֣ם מִמֶּ֔נּוּ וְנִפְקְח֖וּ עֵֽינֵיכֶ֑ם וִהְיִיתֶם֙ כֵּֽאלֹהִ֔ים יֹדְעֵ֖י ט֥וֹב וָרָֽע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The chapter's keystone verse. The serpent's claim has three components, each of which the chapter will independently confirm.

Beyom acholchem ... v'niphqecho eineichem — on the day you eat ... your eyes will be opened. Confirmed at v7: 'the eyes of the two of them were opened'. The verb פָּקַח paqach is the same in both — the serpent's promise and the narrative's report use identical vocabulary. The eyes do open.

Vihyitem ke-elohim — you will be like the Elohim. Confirmed at v22 by YHWH-Elohim's own words: 'the human has become like one of us'. The serpent's promise of class-elevation is not refuted — it is acknowledged from above.

Yodei tov vara — knowing good and bad. Confirmed at v22, same phrase, same root יָדַע yada. The contested knowledge is granted.

What the serpent gets wrong — or omits — is the second consequence: that the opening of eyes also opens shame, that the class-rise costs the garden, that knowing comes with mortality surfacing. The serpent's three claims are all literally true; his lie is one of omission. The traditional reading that 'the serpent lies' has to either spiritualize the deaths-by-mortality the chapter delivers or contest the verses themselves; the simpler reading is that everything the serpent says happens, and the warnings YHWH-Elohim gave are also true. Both are.

6
The woman saw that the tree was good for food, that it was a delight to the eyes, and that the tree was desirable for gaining insight; and she took of its fruit and ate. She also gave to her husband with her, and he ate.
וַתֵּ֣רֶא הָֽאִשָּׁ֡ה כִּ֣י טוֹב֩ הָעֵ֨ץ לְמַאֲכָ֜ל וְכִ֧י תַֽאֲוָה־ה֣וּא לָעֵינַ֗יִם וְנֶחְמָ֤ד הָעֵץ֙ לְהַשְׂכִּ֔יל וַתִּקַּ֥ח מִפִּרְי֖וֹ וַתֹּאכַ֑ל וַתִּתֵּ֧ן גַּם־לְאִישָׁ֛הּ עִמָּ֖הּ וַיֹּאכַֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Three goods, then the act. The tree is good for food, a delight to the eyes, and desirable for gaining insight — the triple description echoes the language of chapter 1, where the Elohim repeatedly saw that what was made was good. The woman applies the same evaluative grammar to the tree she has been told not to eat from. The act follows judgment, not appetite.

She also gave to her husband with her. The phrase עִמָּהּ immah ('with her') is structurally crucial: the man is present, not off in another part of the garden. He is beside her through the serpent-dialogue, the seeing, the taking. The reading tradition that imagines Adam absent and the woman alone-with-the-serpent has to ignore the immah. He is there. The decision is hers (she sees, she takes, she eats); the agreement is his (he eats, with no recorded objection). His subsequent claim in v12 ('the woman whom you gave to be with me — she gave me') will be technically accurate and morally insufficient.

7
Then the eyes of the two of them were opened, and they knew that they were naked; and they sewed fig leaves together and made for themselves belts.
וַתִּפָּקַ֙חְנָה֙ עֵינֵ֣י שְׁנֵיהֶ֔ם וַיֵּ֣דְע֔וּ כִּ֥י עֵֽירֻמִּ֖ם הֵ֑ם וַֽיִּתְפְּרוּ֙ עֲלֵ֣ה תְאֵנָ֔ה וַיַּעֲשׂ֥וּ לָהֶ֖ם חֲגֹרֹֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The promise opens, the nakedness closes. The first half of v7 fulfills the serpent's promise word for word — same verb, same construction. The second half completes the arc from 2:25: they were naked and unashamed; now they are naked and aware of it. The knowing (יָדַע yada) here is exactly the kind the tree was named for — experiential acquaintance with their own condition. The first thing they know with their newly-opened eyes is themselves.

Belts (חֲגֹרֹת chagorot), not aprons (KJV) or loincloths (NRSV): the noun is from חָגַר chagar 'to gird', and refers to a wraparound waist-garment. The improvisation is partial — the chest and limbs are uncovered — and YHWH-Elohim will upgrade the covering substantially in v21.

8
They heard the voice of YHWH-Elohim walking about in the garden at the breeze of the day; and the human and his woman hid themselves from the face of YHWH-Elohim among the trees of the garden.
וַֽיִּשְׁמְע֞וּ אֶת־ק֨וֹל יְהוָ֧ה אֱלֹהִ֛ים מִתְהַלֵּ֥ךְ בַּגָּ֖ן לְר֣וּחַ הַיּ֑וֹם וַיִּתְחַבֵּ֨א הָֽאָדָ֜ם וְאִשְׁתּ֗וֹ מִפְּנֵי֙ יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֔ים בְּת֖וֹךְ עֵ֥ץ הַגָּֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The mode of divine presence. Mithallech — the hithpael of הָלַךְ — is iterative walking about, the same form used of Enoch (5:22, 24) and Noah (6:9) walking-about with the Elohim. The picture is at-home: YHWH-Elohim is not visiting the garden but moving through it as resident. The disruption isn't the arrival of a stranger but the changed posture of the hosts.

L'ruach ha-yom — at the breeze of the day. רוּחַ ruach (the breath/wind/spirit of 1:2) returns. In construct with יוֹם 'day' it points to the evening wind that rises when the heat falls — the time of relief. The garden's daily rhythm is intact; only the humans inside it have shifted. The chapter's keynote of perceptual change holds in geography too: yesterday's familiar sound is today's signal to hide.

9
YHWH-Elohim called to the human and said to him, "Where are you?"
וַיִּקְרָ֛א יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶל־הָֽאָדָ֑ם וַיֹּ֥אמֶר ל֖וֹ אַיֶּֽכָּה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

Ayekka — where are you? A single word in Hebrew, the interrogative אֵי 'where' fused with the 2ms suffix. The question opens a chain (v11 'who told you?', v13 'what is this you have done?', v14 implicit 'what have you done?') that has the shape of a judicial inquiry — but also the shape of pastoral pursuit. The Hebrew settles neither register. The human's reply does not actually answer the question — he names what he heard (the voice), what he was (afraid), what he did (hid), and what he was not (clothed). Of his location: nothing.

10
He said, "I heard your voice in the garden, and I was afraid because I was naked; and I hid myself."
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר אֶת־קֹלְךָ֥ שָׁמַ֖עְתִּי בַּגָּ֑ן וָאִירָ֛א כִּֽי־עֵירֹ֥ם אָנֹ֖כִי וָאֵחָבֵֽא׃
11
He said, "Who told you that you were naked? Have you eaten from the tree of which I commanded you not to eat?"
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר מִ֚י הִגִּ֣יד לְךָ֔ כִּ֥י עֵירֹ֖ם אָ֑תָּה הֲמִן־הָעֵ֗ץ אֲשֶׁ֧ר צִוִּיתִ֛יךָ לְבִלְתִּ֥י אֲכָל־מִמֶּ֖נּוּ אָכָֽלְתָּ׃
12
The human said, "The woman whom you gave to be with me — she gave me from the tree, and I ate."
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר הָֽאָדָ֑ם הָֽאִשָּׁה֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר נָתַ֣תָּה עִמָּדִ֔י הִ֛וא נָֽתְנָה־לִּ֥י מִן־הָעֵ֖ץ וָאֹכֵֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The first deflection. The human's reply has two interlocutors in it: he answers YHWH-Elohim by gesturing past the woman to the one who gave him the woman. The construction is grammatically tight (the relative clause אֲשֶׁר נָתַתָּה עִמָּדִי 'whom you gave to be with me' modifies the woman) and rhetorically loose (the implied agency is YHWH-Elohim's). The human admits eating but locates the fault upstream — first in the woman, then in the gift-giver. The structure of blame-deflection is set; the woman will deflect to the serpent in the next verse, and the serpent will receive judgment with no deflection available to him.

13
YHWH-Elohim said to the woman, "What is this you have done?" And the woman said, "The serpent deceived me, and I ate."
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֧ה אֱלֹהִ֛ים לָאִשָּׁ֖ה מַה־זֹּ֣את עָשִׂ֑ית וַתֹּ֙אמֶר֙ הָֽאִשָּׁ֔ה הַנָּחָ֥שׁ הִשִּׁיאַ֖נִי וָאֹכֵֽל׃
14
YHWH-Elohim said to the serpent, "Because you have done this, cursed are you above all cattle and above every wild animal of the field; on your belly you shall go, and dust you shall eat all the days of your life.
וַיֹּאמֶר֩ יְהוָ֨ה אֱלֹהִ֥ים ׀ אֶֽל־הַנָּחָשׁ֮ כִּ֣י עָשִׂ֣יתָ זֹּאת֒ אָר֤וּר אַתָּה֙ מִכָּל־הַבְּהֵמָ֔ה וּמִכֹּ֖ל חַיַּ֣ת הַשָּׂדֶ֑ה עַל־גְּחֹנְךָ֣ תֵלֵ֔ךְ וְעָפָ֥ר תֹּאכַ֖ל כָּל־יְמֵ֥י חַיֶּֽיךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Arur attah — cursed are you. The serpent receives the curse formula directly, the only one of the three to do so. The judgment has two parts: posture (belly-locomotion, dust-eating) and the enmity-clause that follows in v15. The dust-eating is etiological — explaining why snakes appear belly-down — and rhetorical: the serpent feeds on the same substance the human came from (afar of 2:7, repeated in 3:19's 'dust you are, and to dust you shall return'). The three are now tied by dust: the human came from it and returns to it; the serpent eats it. The garden's hierarchy has reorganized around the material.

15
I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your seed and her seed; he will strike you at the head, and you will strike him at the heel."
וְאֵיבָ֣ה ׀ אָשִׁ֗ית בֵּֽינְךָ֙ וּבֵ֣ין הָֽאִשָּׁ֔ה וּבֵ֥ין זַרְעֲךָ֖ וּבֵ֣ין זַרְעָ֑הּ ה֚וּא יְשׁוּפְךָ֣ רֹ֔אשׁ וְאַתָּ֖ה תְּשׁוּפֶ֥נּוּ עָקֵֽב׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 15

Head and heel. The verb שׁוּף shuf appears only here and in two parallels (Ps 139:11, 'darkness shall cover me'; Job 9:17, 'he crushes me with a tempest'). The semantic core is contested across 'crush, strike, bruise, lie in wait for'. Both clauses use the same verb — whatever the seed does to the serpent's head, the serpent does to the seed's heel. The symmetry argues for a single verb of injurious contact rather than two different actions.

The protoevangelium tradition (Latin: 'the first gospel') reads this verse as a messianic prediction — particularly so via the Vulgate's text-critically dubious ipsa conteret 'she shall crush' (with 'she' = Mary). The Hebrew has only הוּא hu 'he' (or 'it', referring back to זֶרַע zera 'seed', a collective). Plenty of Jewish and Christian commentary treats this as etiological — explaining the human-snake antipathy — without the messianic load. WoH renders the symmetry plain and leaves the protoevangelium reading flagged as a separate interpretive tradition.

The ANE backdrop matters: serpent-versus-warrior is a stock combat motif in Mesopotamian and Canaanite mythology. Genesis has demythologized the serpent (he is a field-animal now, however cunning), but the head-versus-heel grammar still echoes the older battle.

16
To the woman he said, "I will greatly multiply your hardship and your conceiving; in hardship you shall bear children; and your longing shall be for your husband, and he shall rule over you."
אֶֽל־הָאִשָּׁ֣ה אָמַ֗ר הַרְבָּ֤ה אַרְבֶּה֙ עִצְּבוֹנֵ֣ךְ וְהֵֽרֹנֵ֔ךְ בְּעֶ֖צֶב תֵּֽלְדִ֣י בָנִ֑ים וְאֶל־אִישֵׁךְ֙ תְּשׁ֣וּקָתֵ֔ךְ וְה֖וּא יִמְשָׁל־בָּֽךְ׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Two phrases, one structural detail.

First, itzavon and etzev — both from the same root (עצב), both rendered hardship. The woman receives the same word for childbearing that the human will receive in v17 for working the ground. The two parallel curses pair: human-and-ground, woman-and-childbearing, bound by the same hardship-noun.

Second, t'shuqah and yimshal — your longing shall be for your husband, and he shall rule over you. The same construction reappears in 4:7, where sin's t'shuqah is for Cain and Cain must rule over it. The 4:7 parallel turns the v16 verse darker: not a tender longing but a contested impulse, with the rule-clause as countervailing rather than complementary. WoH renders 'longing' as the most semantically minimal English option and lets the parallel speak in commentary.

Structural note: the woman does not receive the curse formula אֲרוּרָה arurah (as the serpent does in v14 and the ground does in v17). She is told what is intensified (childbearing-hardship) and what the new relational dynamic will be (longing-and-rule), but the explicit curse word is reserved.

17
And to the human he said, "Because you have listened to the voice of your woman and have eaten from the tree of which I commanded you, saying, 'You shall not eat from it' — cursed is the ground because of you; in hardship you shall eat of it all the days of your life.
וּלְאָדָ֣ם אָמַ֗ר כִּֽי־שָׁמַעְתָּ֮ לְק֣וֹל אִשְׁתֶּךָ֒ וַתֹּ֙אכַל֙ מִן־הָעֵ֔ץ אֲשֶׁ֤ר צִוִּיתִ֙יךָ֙ לֵאמֹ֔ר לֹ֥א תֹאכַ֖ל מִמֶּ֑נּוּ אֲרוּרָ֤ה הָֽאֲדָמָה֙ בַּֽעֲבוּרֶ֔ךָ בְּעִצָּבוֹן֙ תֹּֽאכֲלֶ֔נָּה כֹּ֖ל יְמֵ֥י חַיֶּֽיךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The curse falls on the ground, not on the human. Arurah ha-adamah ba-avurecha — 'cursed is the ground because of you'. Read precisely: of the three speeches in v14–19, the serpent is cursed (v14, arur attah 'cursed are you'), the ground is cursed (v17, arurah ha-adamah 'cursed is the ground'), but the human is not. The text refuses to use the curse formula on him directly. He is told the ground he came from is now cursed on his account (בַּעֲבוּרֶךָ ba-avurecha). His relation to the adamah turns adversarial — itzavon now mediates the bond — but he himself is not pronounced cursed.

This structural restraint is theologically significant. The traditional reading sometimes speaks of 'the curse on humanity'; the text speaks of a curse on the ground and a hardship-relationship between human and ground. The mortality-clause that follows in v19 ('dust you are, and to dust you shall return') is etiology, not curse: it names what the human always was and what he therefore returns to.

18
It shall sprout thorns and thistles for you; and you shall eat the herb of the field.
וְק֥וֹץ וְדַרְדַּ֖ר תַּצְמִ֣יחַֽ לָ֑ךְ וְאָכַלְתָּ֖ אֶת־עֵ֥שֶׂב הַשָּׂדֶֽה׃
19
By the sweat of your face you shall eat bread until you return to the ground, for from it you were taken; for you are dust, and to dust you shall return.
בְּזֵעַ֤ת אַפֶּ֙יךָ֙ תֹּ֣אכַל לֶ֔חֶם עַ֤ד שֽׁוּבְךָ֙ אֶל־הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה כִּ֥י מִמֶּ֖נָּה לֻקָּ֑חְתָּ כִּֽי־עָפָ֣ר אַ֔תָּה וְאֶל־עָפָ֖ר תָּשֽׁוּב׃
Translation Commentary Verse 19

Ki afar attah v'el afar tashuv — for you are dust, and to dust you shall return. The closing line of the human's speech is not announcing a new fate but stating the standing one. Afar 'dust' is the same word as in 2:7, where the human was formed from dust of the ground. The verse closes the etymological arc: dust → human → dust. Mot tamut 'you shall surely die' (2:17) is here unfolded — not as a single-day execution but as the now-active condition of mortality. The garden suspended this trajectory by giving access to the tree of life (v22 will name it explicitly); the chapter's ending removes that access.

20
The human called his woman's name Chavvah, because she became the mother of all living.
וַיִּקְרָ֧א הָֽאָדָ֛ם שֵׁ֥ם אִשְׁתּ֖וֹ חַוָּ֑ה כִּ֛י הִ֥וא הָֽיְתָ֖ה אֵ֥ם כָּל־חָֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

The third naming. The species was named adam by the Elohim (1:26–27); the gender-counterpart was named Ishah by the human (2:23); now in 3:20 the human gives his woman a personal name — Chavvah, glossed by the verse as connected to chai 'living, life'. The naming comes after the death-prediction of v19, and against it. The human's first interpretive act in the post-garden world names his woman not by mortality but by life. The English form Eve derives via the LXX (Εὕα) and Vulgate (Eva); WoH keeps the Hebrew Chavvah so the chai-resonance is visible.

21
YHWH-Elohim made for the human and his woman garments of skin, and clothed them.
וַיַּעַשׂ֩ יְהוָ֨ה אֱלֹהִ֜ים לְאָדָ֧ם וּלְאִשְׁתּ֛וֹ כָּתְנ֥וֹת ע֖וֹר וַיַּלְבִּשֵֽׁם׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 21

An upgrade from improvisation. The fig-leaf belts of v7 are replaced with full-coverage tunics of hide. The verse is given in three terse verbs: made … garments … clothed. The text is silent on where the skins came from — the first implied animal death in Genesis sits in this gap. Tradition has filled it in many ways; the text refuses to. What is recorded is the act of clothing — YHWH-Elohim dresses them himself, on the way out of the garden. Whatever else the gesture is, it is not abandonment.

22
YHWH-Elohim said, "Behold, the human has become like one of us, knowing good and bad. And now, lest he reach out his hand and take also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever—"
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר ׀ יְהוָ֣ה אֱלֹהִ֗ים הֵ֤ן הָֽאָדָם֙ הָיָה֙ כְּאַחַ֣ד מִמֶּ֔נּוּ לָדַ֖עַת ט֣וֹב וָרָ֑ע וְעַתָּ֣ה ׀ פֶּן־יִשְׁלַ֣ח יָד֗וֹ וְלָקַח֙ גַּ֚ם מֵעֵ֣ץ הַֽחַיִּ֔ים וְאָכַ֖ל וָחַ֥י לְעֹלָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The second explicit divine plural. The first was 1:26 ('let us make humankind in our image'); this is the second. Both pivot on what humans share with the speaker(s). In 1:26 the Elohim made humankind in their (the Elohim-class') image; in 3:22 the human has — by the eating — become like one of them, specifically in the domain of knowing good and bad. The serpent's promise (v5) is now confirmed from the divine side using the exact same vocabulary.

Three observations.

First, the speech is internal to the Elohim-class: YHWH-Elohim is speaking to other members (the us), not to the human. The chapter's framing here is closer to a council deliberation than a pronouncement.

Second, the speech is grammatically incomplete: 'lest he reach out his hand and take also from the tree of life, and eat, and live forever—' has no apodosis. The sentence stops; the next verse acts. YHWH-Elohim doesn't finish the sentence because the action is the finish.

Third, the threat motivating the expulsion is not punishment but limit-keeping: if the human, now in possession of the knowing-good-and-bad, also gains access to perpetual life, the class-rise the serpent promised completes. The expulsion is to keep the boundary — to prevent a full join into the Elohim-class. The garden contained both trees; only the tree of life is now made inaccessible. The knowing-tree is no longer dangerous because its fruit has already been eaten.

23
So YHWH-Elohim sent him out of the garden of Eden, to work the ground from which he had been taken.
וַֽיְשַׁלְּחֵ֛הוּ יְהוָ֥ה אֱלֹהִ֖ים מִגַּן־עֵ֑דֶן לַֽעֲבֹד֙ אֶת־הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר לֻקַּ֖ח מִשָּֽׁם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The work of v15 of chapter 2 continues, but outside. The human was placed in the garden 'to work it and to guard it' (2:15, לְעָבְדָהּ וּלְשָׁמְרָהּ). He is now sent to work the ground (לַעֲבֹד אֶת־הָאֲדָמָה) — the same verb עָבַד avad, but the object has shifted from the garden (or the adamah-of-the-garden) to the adamah in general, the cursed-because-of-him ground of v17. The work continues; its conditions change. The original mandate is not revoked, only relocated.

24
He drove out the human, and placed at the east of the garden of Eden the cherubim and the flaming blade that turns itself, to guard the way to the tree of life.
וַיְגָ֖רֶשׁ אֶת־הָֽאָדָ֑ם וַיַּשְׁכֵּן֩ מִקֶּ֨דֶם לְגַן־עֵ֜דֶן אֶת־הַכְּרֻבִ֗ים וְאֵ֨ת לַ֤הַט הַחֶ֙רֶב֙ הַמִּתְהַפֶּ֔כֶת לִשְׁמֹ֕ר אֶת־דֶּ֖רֶךְ עֵ֥ץ הַֽחַיִּֽים׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 24

Two guardians, one access denied. K'ruvim are the composite hybrid figures of ANE iconography — winged sphinxes flanking thrones and temple thresholds, not the cherubic putti of later Christian art. They reappear in the Hebrew Bible as the throne-bearers of YHWH (1 Sam 4:4, Ezek 10), as flanking the Ark (Ex 25:18), as a temple-throne motif (1 Kgs 6:23–28). Here, plural, they take up station at the garden's eastern approach.

Lahat ha-cherev ha-mithappechet — the flaming blade that turns itself. The hithpael participle mithappechet gives the blade autonomous motion: it turns itself, not 'turned every way' (KJV's vague rendering, which leaves the agent unspecified). The image is striking: an unhanded weapon. Combined with the cherubim, the garden is now protected by two distinct guardians — animate (cherubim) and self-animating (blade). The way back to the tree of life is sealed.

The chapter that opened with humans in the garden, naked and unashamed, closes with humans outside the garden, clothed in hide, and the garden's eastern gate guarded against return. The arc from 2:25 to 3:24 has bent decisively. What was begun in 4:1 — childbearing, naming, the first generation outside — will follow.

Chapter 4

Genesis 4 — Cain and Hevel

1
The human knew his woman Chavvah, and she conceived and bore Cain. She said, "I have made a man — YHWH himself!"
וְהָ֣אָדָ֔ם יָדַ֖ע אֶת־חַוָּ֣ה אִשְׁתּ֑וֹ וַתַּ֙הַר֙ וַתֵּ֣לֶד אֶת־קַ֔יִן וַתֹּ֕אמֶר קָנִ֥יתִי אִ֖ישׁ אֶת־יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

A startling birth-saying. The verse ends with one of the strangest constructions in Genesis: קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת־יְהוָה qaniti ish et-YHWH. The verb קָנָה qana spans 'acquire, get, possess' and 'create, beget' (used of divine creation in 14:19, Ps 139:13). אֶת־יְהוָה et-YHWH is the direct-object marker followed by the Tetragrammaton — read straight, this means 'I have created/acquired a man, YHWH' (with YHWH in apposition to a man).

Most translations supply a preposition that isn't there. KJV: 'I have gotten a man from the LORD'. NJPS: 'I have gained a male child with the help of the LORD'. Alter: 'I have got me a man with the LORD'. The Hebrew has only the direct-object marker — no 'from', no 'with', no 'help of'.

Reading the Hebrew straight yields a Chavvah who frames her firstborn in unmistakably divine-creative language. The first human birth outside the garden is described with the same verb used elsewhere for the Elohim's making of the cosmos. Whether et-YHWH is appositional ('a man — YHWH', the strongest reading), associative ('a man, with YHWH'), or instrumental ('a man, via YHWH'), the Hebrew leaves un-decided. WoH takes the strong reading — the saying claims an unmistakably divine-creative scale for the act of birth — and lets commentary surface the softer alternatives.

Note that Chavvah here invokes YHWH, not Elohim or YHWH-Elohim. The personal name has come fully forward in her speech; by v25 she will shift back to Elohim.

2
She bore again — his brother Hevel. Hevel became a shepherd of flock, and Cain became a worker of the ground.
וַתֹּ֣סֶף לָלֶ֔דֶת אֶת־אָחִ֖יו אֶת־הָ֑בֶל וַֽיְהִי־הֶ֙בֶל֙ רֹ֣עֵה צֹ֔אן וְקַ֕יִן הָיָ֖ה עֹבֵ֥ד אֲדָמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The second son arrives in silence. Where Cain's birth was crowned by Chavvah's elaborate etymology (v1), Hevel's birth is given in five Hebrew words with no naming-speech, no etymology, no comment. He is his brother before he is named. The name itself — הֶבֶל hevel — is the word that opens Qohelet ('vapor of vapors, all is vapor'). It denotes breath, mist, fleetingness, futility. The text gives him no naming-ceremony because the name is the foreshadowing.

The brothers split the post-garden economic order between them: עֹבֵד אֲדָמָה oved adamah (worker of the ground) for Cain, רֹעֵה צֹאן roeh tzon (shepherd of flock) for Hevel. Cain inherits the human-and-ground bond from 2:15, 3:23 — same verb עָבַד avad. Hevel takes up the pastoral counterpart. ANE life's two foundational economies — settled agriculture and nomadic pastoralism — stand side by side, in two brothers.

3
After some time, Cain brought from the fruit of the ground an offering to YHWH.
וַֽיְהִ֖י מִקֵּ֣ץ יָמִ֑ים וַיָּבֵ֨א קַ֜יִן מִפְּרִ֧י הָֽאֲדָמָ֛ה מִנְחָ֖ה לַֽיהוָֽה׃
4
And Hevel also brought — from the firstborn of his flock, and from their fat portions. YHWH looked favorably on Hevel and on his offering;
וְהֶ֨בֶל הֵבִ֥יא גַם־ה֛וּא מִבְּכֹר֥וֹת צֹאנ֖וֹ וּמֵֽחֶלְבֵהֶ֑ן וַיִּ֣שַׁע יְהוָ֔ה אֶל־הֶ֖בֶל וְאֶל־מִנְחָתֽוֹ׃
5
but on Cain and on his offering he did not look favorably. Cain burned greatly, and his face fell.
וְאֶל־קַ֥יִן וְאֶל־מִנְחָת֖וֹ לֹ֣א שָׁעָ֑ה וַיִּ֤חַר לְקַ֙יִן֙ מְאֹ֔ד וַֽיִּפְּל֖וּ פָּנָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The text gives no reason. YHWH regards one offering and not the other; no verse explains why. Tradition fills the gap: the rabbinic and patristic readings note that Hevel brought firstborn and fat portions — the choicest parts — while Cain's offering is described only as 'from the fruit of the ground' with no equivalent superlatives. Whether this carries the explanatory weight tradition gives it is debated. The Hebrew's silence is conspicuous: the differential is data, not justified outcome.

The asymmetry generates the chapter. Note that the response to the differential is what is judged — not the offering itself, not the differential. YHWH's next speech (vv. 6–7) addresses Cain's anger, not his offering, and offers a path forward (if you do well, lifting; if not, sin couches). The differential is the setup; what Cain does with it is the trial.

6
YHWH said to Cain, "Why have you burned, and why has your face fallen?
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר יְהוָ֖ה אֶל־קָ֑יִן לָ֚מָּה חָ֣רָה לָ֔ךְ וְלָ֖מָּה נָפְל֥וּ פָנֶֽיךָ׃
7
Is it not so: if you do well, lifting; and if you do not do well, sin couches at the door — and its longing is for you, but you shall rule over it."
הֲל֤וֹא אִם־תֵּיטִיב֙ שְׂאֵ֔ת וְאִם֙ לֹ֣א תֵיטִ֔יב לַפֶּ֖תַח חַטָּ֣את רֹבֵ֑ץ וְאֵלֶ֙יךָ֙ תְּשׁ֣וּקָת֔וֹ וְאַתָּ֖ה תִּמְשָׁל־בּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

Two grammatical features and one cross-textual parallel.

First, sin couches at the door. חַטָּאת chatat is grammatically feminine, but רֹבֵץ rovetz is masculine — a grammatical mismatch suggesting sin is being personified as a male figure (an animal, a demon) crouching at the doorway. The verb רָבַץ ravats is the verb for animals lying-down-to-watch-or-ambush (a wolf in Gen 49:9, predators in Ps 104:22). The picture is bestial-predatory, not abstract-moral.

Second, the word חַטָּאת chatat carries a double range: sin (the act) and sin-offering (the cultic remedy, the leading sense in Leviticus). At the door of Cain's life, either sin is crouching (about to overtake him) or a sin-offering is crouching (available, waiting to be brought). The double sense is productive ambiguity, not error.

Third, the second clause — its longing is for you, but you shall rule over it — is verbatim parallel to Gen 3:16, with pronouns swapped. In 3:16: 'your longing shall be for your husband, and he shall rule over you'. Here in 4:7: 'its longing is for you, and you shall rule over it'. Same noun t'shuqah, same verb mashal. The parallel forces a re-reading of 3:16 — if sin's longing in 4:7 is a hostile, predatory drive that must be mastered, then the woman's longing in 3:16 may also be contested-drive rather than tender affection. The verses comment on each other across the chapter break.

8
Cain spoke to his brother Hevel. And while they were in the field, Cain rose up against his brother Hevel and killed him.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר קַ֖יִן אֶל־הֶ֣בֶל אָחִ֑יו וַֽיְהִי֙ בִּהְיוֹתָ֣ם בַּשָּׂדֶ֔ה וַיָּ֥קָם קַ֛יִן אֶל־הֶ֥בֶל אָחִ֖יו וַיַּהַרְגֵֽהוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The unfinished sentence. The Masoretic text leaves a gap: Cain spoke to his brother Hevel — and then nothing. What Cain said is not recorded. The verse continues with and while they were in the field. The Samaritan Pentateuch, the LXX, and several other ancient versions fill the gap with a clause like 'Let us go out to the field' — likely an early scribal addition to smooth the syntax. The Masoretic gap is conspicuous: Cain's words are absent. Whatever passed between brothers in the field, the text refuses to record. Only the act is preserved: וַיָּקָם vayyaqam ('and he rose up'), וַיַּהַרְגֵהוּ vayyahargehu ('and he killed him'). The first homicide is described with the bare verb הָרַג harag, the same verb that will return six verses later in Lamech's escalating song.

9
YHWH said to Cain, "Where is Hevel your brother?" And he said, "I do not know — am I my brother's keeper?"
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ אֶל־קַ֔יִן אֵ֖י הֶ֣בֶל אָחִ֑יךָ וַיֹּ֙אמֶר֙ לֹ֣א יָדַ֔עְתִּי הֲשֹׁמֵ֥ר אָחִ֖י אָנֹֽכִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The second divine 'where' question. In 3:9 YHWH-Elohim asked the human ayekka — 'where are you?'. Now YHWH asks Cain ei hevel achikha — 'where is Hevel your brother?'. The first question opened a chain of self-disclosure; this one opens a chain of denial. Cain answers with a lie (I do not know) and a rhetorical question (am I my brother's keeper?).

The verb in 'keeper' is שָׁמַר shamar — the same verb used of the human's mandate in 2:15 (to work the garden and to keep it) and of the cherubim's mandate in 3:24 (to guard the way to the tree of life). The keeping-vocabulary returns, now in the form of a denial. Cain refuses the relational structure the verb names. The chapter's most famous line is a rejection of a category the text takes as given.

10
He said, "What have you done? The voice of your brother's bloods cries out to me from the ground."
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר מֶ֣ה עָשִׂ֑יתָ ק֚וֹל דְּמֵ֣י אָחִ֔יךָ צֹעֲקִ֥ים אֵלַ֖י מִן־הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 10

Demei achikha tzo'aqim — your brother's bloods cries out. Two precise Hebrew features. דְּמֵי demei is the plural of דָּם dam — Hebrew uses plural blood for shed blood, blood as forensic evidence (Lev 17:4, Isa 33:15, Hab 2:8). The plural is a register of testimony. And the participle צֹעֲקִים tzo'aqim is itself plural, agreeing with the plural noun: the bloods cry out, multiple voices. The ground has not absorbed and silenced; it amplifies. The same adamah that received Cain's offering in v3 — now refused to give him produce in v12 — is here a testifying witness against him.

The verb צָעַק tza'aq is the cry of the oppressed (used of the Hebrews in Egypt, Ex 2:23; of the widow and orphan, Ex 22:22). The Genesis text positions Hevel's spilled blood in the same vocabulary register as the cry of the wronged-and-voiceless throughout the Torah. The shed blood is, in the text's grammar, an oppressed witness.

11
And now, cursed are you, from the ground which opened its mouth to take your brother's bloods from your hand.
וְעַתָּ֖ה אָר֣וּר אָ֑תָּה מִן־הָֽאֲדָמָה֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר פָּצְתָ֣ה אֶת־פִּ֔יהָ לָקַ֛חַת אֶת־דְּמֵ֥י אָחִ֖יךָ מִיָּדֶֽךָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The first human to receive the curse formula directly. In 3:14 the serpent received arur attah ('cursed are you'). In 3:17 the ground received arurah ha-adamah ('cursed is the ground'). The human and the woman did not. Now in 4:11, Cain receives arur attah — the same formula given to the serpent. The line that began in chapter 3 with the serpent under direct curse now adds Cain to that category.

The preposition מִן min has range: separative ('cursed away from the ground' — exile) or causative ('cursed by the ground' — the ground is now agent against him). Both readings work; the Hebrew leaves both in play. The ground that bore Hevel's blood as testimony in v10 is now the curse-bearer or curse-source against Cain. The same adamah Cain was supposed to work (v2) has turned actively hostile.

Note the personification: the ground which opened its mouth. The adamah is given a mouth — not metaphorical here but quasi-mythic, with an active swallow-and-receive action. Genesis often imagines the ground as a quasi-living surface; here it has actively taken the blood from Cain's hand.

12
When you work the ground, it shall no longer give its strength to you; a wanderer and a fugitive you shall be on the land.
כִּ֤י תַֽעֲבֹד֙ אֶת־הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה לֹֽא־תֹסֵ֥ף תֵּת־כֹּחָ֖הּ לָ֑ךְ נָ֥ע וָנָ֖ד תִּֽהְיֶ֥ה בָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The mandate withdrawn. The verb עָבַד avad ('to work') is Cain's defining occupation (v2: oved adamah). YHWH does not forbid the work — Cain is told 'when you work the ground' — but the response of the ground is withdrawn: it shall no longer give its strength to you. The relation of work-to-yield is broken for him.

In its place: נָע וָנָד na va-nad. Two participles of restless motion. נָע na from נוּעַ 'to wander aimlessly'. נָד nad from נוּד 'to wander, flee'. The alliterative pair signals continuous, purposeless movement. In v16 this condition will fuse with the place-name נוֹד Nod ('Wandering') — the address becomes the condition. Cain is exiled from settled relation to the ground; the worker's mandate ends in his line.

13
Cain said to YHWH, "My iniquity is too great to bear."
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר קַ֖יִן אֶל־יְהוָ֑ה גָּד֥וֹל עֲוֺנִ֖י מִנְּשֹֽׂא׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The triple ambiguity. עָוֹן avon spans three senses: (1) the act of sin, (2) the guilt it carries, (3) the punishment/consequence of the sin. The lexicon does not separate them — one word holds all three. The verb נָשָׂא nasa is similarly multivalent: 'to bear, carry, lift' — and in theological register, 'to bear away, to forgive'.

Cain's cry reads three ways at once: (a) my guilt is too great to bear — remorse, (b) my punishment is too great to bear — complaint, (c) my iniquity is too great to be lifted (i.e., forgiven) — despair of mercy. Tradition has read each. KJV ('my punishment is greater than I can bear') chose (b). Some Jewish commentaries (Rashi) chose (a). The Augustinian tradition is sometimes drawn to (c). The Hebrew supports all three; the text refuses to disambiguate. Cain's anguish is grammatically as multivalent as his act.

14
Behold, you have driven me out today from the face of the ground, and from your face I shall be hidden; I shall be a wanderer and a fugitive on the land, and anyone who finds me will kill me.
הֵן֩ גֵּרַ֨שְׁתָּ אֹתִ֜י הַיּ֗וֹם מֵעַל֙ פְּנֵ֣י הָֽאֲדָמָ֔ה וּמִפָּנֶ֖יךָ אֶסָּתֵ֑ר וְהָיִ֜יתִי נָ֤ע וָנָד֙ בָּאָ֔רֶץ וְהָיָ֥ה כָל־מֹצְאִ֖י יַֽהַרְגֵֽנִי׃
15
YHWH said to him, "Therefore — whoever kills Cain, he will be avenged sevenfold." And YHWH placed for Cain a sign, so that no one who found him would strike him.
וַיֹּ֧אמֶר ל֣וֹ יְהוָ֗ה לָכֵן֙ כָּל־הֹרֵ֣ג קַ֔יִן שִׁבְעָתַ֖יִם יֻקָּ֑ם וַיָּ֨שֶׂם יְהוָ֤ה לְקַ֙יִן֙ א֔וֹת לְבִלְתִּ֥י הַכּוֹת־אֹת֖וֹ כָּל־מֹצְאֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

A protection, not a brand. The conventional 'mark of Cain' has a long medieval afterlife, often imagined as a forehead-stamp visible to other humans. The Hebrew is more open. אוֹת ot is sign in the generic sense — covering the rainbow (9:13), circumcision (17:11), the Sabbath (Ex 31:13), and miraculous portents (Ex 4:8–9). The preposition is לְ l- ('for/to'), not עַל al ('on/upon'). The sign is for Cain, not on him. It could be carried, accompanied, or stationed near. The text refuses to specify.

The sevenfold-vengeance formula is the structural counterpart of the sign: an absolute disproportion-protection. The same numeral (7×) will return in v24 with Lamech multiplying it to 77× — but in Cain's case, it is defensive (the multiplier shields him from being killed).
