The formula is given by YHWH, not claimed by Cain.

16
Cain went out from the presence of YHWH, and settled in the land of Nod, east of Eden.
וַיֵּ֥צֵא קַ֖יִן מִלִּפְנֵ֣י יְהוָ֑ה וַיֵּ֥שֶׁב בְּאֶֽרֶץ־נ֖וֹד קִדְמַת־עֵֽדֶן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The condition becomes the address. נוֹד Nod is morphologically identical to the participle נָד nad ('wandering') from v12. The land's name is Cain's punishment — 'the land of Wandering'. He is sentenced to wander and then settles in Wandering. The geography fuses with the verdict.

East of Eden — קִדְמַת־עֵדֶן qidmat-eden. The east-axis returns. In 3:24 the cherubim were placed at the east of the garden; now Cain settles further east still. The west-east axis tracks an exilic motion: humans began in Eden, were placed in the garden eastward (2:8), driven out east-ward (3:24), and now Cain has extended the eastward direction. The garden is irrecoverably to the west of every subsequent step. The chapter's geography is moral as well as spatial.

17
Cain knew his wife, and she conceived and bore Hanokh. And he was building a city, and he called the name of the city after the name of his son — Hanokh.
וַיֵּ֤דַע קַ֙יִן֙ אֶת־אִשְׁתּ֔וֹ וַתַּ֖הַר וַתֵּ֣לֶד אֶת־חֲנ֑וֹךְ וַֽיְהִי֙ בֹּ֣נֶה עִ֔יר וַיִּקְרָא֙ שֵׁ֣ם הָעִ֔יר כְּשֵׁ֖ם בְּנ֥וֹ חֲנֽוֹךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The first city. Cain — sentenced to wander — builds a city. The Hebrew is precise about the contradiction: בֹּנֶה עִיר boneh ir, a present-participle of habitual action ('he was building'). The verse does not present a finished city but an ongoing project. The city is named after Cain's son Hanokh (חֲנוֹךְ from חָנַךְ 'to dedicate, initiate'). The line that fled the worker-of-the-ground mandate begins urbanization — building, naming, fixing a place. The Cain line will further specialize in tent-dwelling, music, and metalwork (vv. 20–22). Civilization, in this telling, begins in the line of the exile.

Note that the wife arrives in the verse without introduction. The text is silent on her origin (a long-standing interpretive question — sister, separately-created human, an outside lineage). Genesis takes the population for granted at this point; the reader has only Cain, Hevel (now dead), Adam, and Chavvah explicitly on the page.

18
To Hanokh was born Irad; and Irad fathered Mehuyael; and Mehuyael fathered Methushael; and Methushael fathered Lamech.
וַיִּוָּלֵ֤ד לַֽחֲנוֹךְ֙ אֶת־עִירָ֔ד וְעִירָ֕ד יָלַ֖ד אֶת־מְחֽוּיָאֵ֑ל וּמְחִיּיָאֵ֗ל יָלַד֙ אֶת־מְת֣וּשָׁאֵ֔ל וּמְתוּשָׁאֵ֖ל יָלַ֥ד אֶת־לָֽמֶךְ׃
19
Lamech took for himself two wives: the name of the first was Adah, and the name of the second Tzillah.
וַיִּֽקַּֽח־ל֥וֹ לֶ֖מֶךְ שְׁתֵּ֣י נָשִׁ֑ים שֵׁ֤ם הָֽאַחַת֙ עָדָ֔ה וְשֵׁ֥ם הַשֵּׁנִ֖ית צִלָּֽה׃
20
Adah bore Yaval; he was the ancestor of those who dwell in tents and have livestock.
וַתֵּ֥לֶד עָדָ֖ה אֶת־יָבָ֑ל ה֣וּא הָיָ֔ה אֲבִ֕י יֹשֵׁ֥ב אֹ֖הֶל וּמִקְנֶֽה׃
21
And his brother's name was Yuval; he was the ancestor of all who take up the lyre and the pipe.
וְשֵׁ֥ם אָחִ֖יו יוּבָ֑ל ה֣וּא הָיָ֔ה אֲבִ֕י כָּל־תֹּפֵ֥שׂ כִּנּ֖וֹר וְעוּגָֽב׃
22
And Tzillah also bore — Tuval-Cain, forger of every cutting-tool of bronze and iron. And Tuval-Cain's sister was Na'amah.
וְצִלָּ֣ה גַם־הִ֗וא יָֽלְדָה֙ אֶת־תּ֣וּבַל קַ֔יִן לֹטֵ֕שׁ כָּל־חֹרֵ֥שׁ נְחֹ֖שֶׁת וּבַרְזֶ֑ל וַֽאֲח֥וֹת תּֽוּבַל־קַ֖יִן נַֽעֲמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The Cain line specializes. Three crafts emerge in three sons across two wives: Yaval — pastoralism (tent-dwelling and livestock); Yuval — music (string and wind instruments); Tuval-Cain — metallurgy (bronze and iron working). The triad represents the foundational technical-civilizational arts of the ANE world. The line that began with a fratricide and an exile produces the first specialists of urban life. Whether the chapter is reading this as triumphal advance or as the moral ambiguity of skilled labor downstream of violence — the text refuses to comment. The achievement and the lineage are placed side by side without judgment.

23
Lamech said to his wives, "Adah and Tzillah, hear my voice — wives of Lamech, listen to my speech: for a man I have killed for my wounding, and a child for my bruising.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר לֶ֜מֶךְ לְנָשָׁ֗יו עָדָ֤ה וְצִלָּה֙ שְׁמַ֣עַן קוֹלִ֔י נְשֵׁ֣י לֶ֔מֶךְ הַאְזֵ֖נָּה אִמְרָתִ֑י כִּ֣י אִ֤ישׁ הָרַ֙גְתִּי֙ לְפִצְעִ֔י וְיֶ֖לֶד לְחַבֻּרָתִֽי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The first poem in the Hebrew Bible. The structure is poetic — parallel lines, formal address, chiastic patterning. Lamech opens with a vocative doubling (his wives' names, then wives of Lamech), then states the matched pair: a man for my wounding, a child for my bruising. The Hebrew is tightly compressed: אִישׁ הָרַגְתִּי לְפִצְעִי וְיֶלֶד לְחַבֻּרָתִי — a man I have killed for my pesa, a child for my chabburah.

The verbal structure is parallelistic, as is the verb tense: הָרַגְתִּי haragti ('I have killed' or 'I would kill' — perfect tense with declarative force). The grammar is itself a boast: I killed a man for a wound, I killed a child for a bruise. The verse can be read as past-tense brag or as conditional threat ('I would kill anyone'); both are within the Hebrew's range. Either way, the line of Cain has moved from one fratricide to a poetic celebration of disproportionate vengeance.

24
If Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-and-seven."
כִּ֥י שִׁבְעָתַ֖יִם יֻקַּם־קָ֑יִן וְלֶ֖מֶךְ שִׁבְעִ֥ים וְשִׁבְעָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

The multiplier escalates. YHWH placed a sevenfold-vengeance protection on Cain in v15 — a defensive shield. Lamech inverts the formula's direction: he takes the same numeral structure and uses it to claim his own offensive vengeance-multiplier. Where Cain's was given (a divine ot), Lamech's is self-arrogated (a song). Where Cain's was protective (against anyone who would strike him), Lamech's is anticipatory (against anyone who might offend him).

The shift from 7× to 77× is the chapter's most compressed moral commentary. It is also the structural inverse of Yeshua's reply to Peter in Matthew 18:22 ('not seven times, but seventy-and-seven times' — the same numeral, but for forgiveness rather than vengeance). The numerical echo across canon is intentional in the receiving tradition; the original verse stands alone, in the line of Cain, naming the multiplication of violence.

25
Adam knew his wife again, and she bore a son. She called his name Shet — "for the Elohim has appointed for me another seed in place of Hevel, since Cain killed him."
וַיֵּ֨דַע אָדָ֥ם עוֹד֙ אֶת־אִשְׁתּ֔וֹ וַתֵּ֣לֶד בֵּ֔ן וַתִּקְרָ֥א אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ שֵׁ֑ת כִּ֣י שָֽׁת־לִ֤י אֱלֹהִים֙ זֶ֣רַע אַחֵ֔ר תַּ֣חַת הֶ֔בֶל כִּ֥י הֲרָג֖וֹ קָֽיִן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

Two transitions in a single verse.

First, the human's name shifts. Throughout chapters 2–4 he has been הָאָדָם ha-adam — 'the human', the article holding him as category-noun. Here in 4:25 the article drops: וַיֵּדַע אָדָם vayyeda adam — 'Adam knew'. The proper-name reading takes over. From this verse forward in Genesis, Adam will function as a personal name. The category-noun has become a person.

Second, the divine-reference vocabulary shifts. In 4:1 Chavvah named Cain with reference to YHWH (the personal name). Now in 4:25 she names Shet with reference to Elohim (the class/category name). The shift may be a P/J source seam, or it may register Chavvah's own changed relation to the divine after the loss of Hevel — from intimate personal-name to formal class-name. The text neither explains nor resolves the shift.

Shet (שֵׁת) is given the etymology immediately: שָׁת־לִי אֱלֹהִים shat-li Elohim — 'the Elohim has appointed/placed for me'. Same root, simple wordplay. The naming closes the chapter's family arc: Cain disinherited from the ground, Hevel killed, Shet appointed in his place. The Shet line will carry forward — and v26 will declare that the calling on the name of YHWH begins in it.

26
And to Shet also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. Then it began to be called on the name of YHWH.
וּלְשֵׁ֤ת גַּם־הוּא֙ יֻלַּד־בֵּ֔ן וַיִּקְרָ֥א אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ אֱנ֑וֹשׁ אָ֣ז הוּחַ֔ל לִקְרֹ֖א בְּשֵׁ֥ם יְהוָֽה׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 26

The chapter closes on an origin. The verb הוּחַל huchal is hophal (passive of the hiphil) of חָלַל — 'it was begun', impersonal passive. Hebrew here has no explicit subject — the conventional 'then men began' (KJV, NRSV) supplies one to smooth the English. The Hebrew leaves the subject open: 'then it was begun to be called on the name of YHWH'. Some practice of name-calling started in this generation. Whether it was cultic invocation, public utterance, formal worship, or something less defined — the text refuses to specify.

The verse is the chapter's terminal contrast. The Cain line ended in metallurgy and the vengeance-song (v22–24). The Shet line ends with a son named Enosh ('mortal, frail human') and the inauguration of name-calling on YHWH. Both genealogies have produced foundational human practices. The chapter places them side by side, naming the one that will become Israel's worship — but not before naming the other one with full lineage and craft-specialty.

Chapter 5

Genesis 5 — The record of the begettings of Adam

1
This is the record of the begettings of Adam. On the day the Elohim shaped Adam, in the likeness of Elohim he made him.
זֶ֣ה סֵ֔פֶר תּוֹלְדֹ֖ת אָדָ֑ם בְּי֗וֹם בְּרֹ֤א אֱלֹהִים֙ אָדָ֔ם בִּדְמ֥וּת אֱלֹהִ֖ים עָשָׂ֥ה אֹתֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

A formal new beginning. This is the second toledot in Genesis (after 2:4 'these are the toledot of the skies and the land'), and the first one to introduce a person's line. Notable: only here in Genesis is the phrase sefer toledot — 'a record/book of the begettings' — used. The other ten toledot-formulae in the book use the simpler eleh toledot 'these are the begettings'. The word סֵפֶר sefer signals a written list-document — a possible embedded source that the chapter incorporates. Many source-critical readings take Genesis 5 as drawing on a once-independent genealogical document; whatever the source-critical view, the register here is documentary.

The verse also makes a tight verbal callback to 1:26–27: the Elohim shaped Adam, in the likeness of Elohim. The two creation verbs bara (shape) and asah (make) appear together in adjacent clauses, recapping chapter 1's anthropology in compressed form. Only demut (likeness) is invoked; tselem (image) is held back until the next verse below — distributed across v1–3 rather than paired together as in 1:26.

2
Male and female he shaped them. He blessed them, and he called their name Adam on the day they were shaped.
זָכָ֥ר וּנְקֵבָ֖ה בְּרָאָ֑ם וַיְבָ֣רֶךְ אֹתָ֗ם וַיִּקְרָ֤א אֶת־שְׁמָם֙ אָדָ֔ם בְּי֖וֹם הִבָּֽרְאָֽם׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The species-name precedes the personal name. The grammar is precise: שְׁמָם shemam is 'their name' (third-person masculine plural suffix) on the singular noun שֵׁם shem — their name [singular] [is] Adam. The Elohim gives the male-and-female pair a single species-name. Adam here is still category-noun ('humankind') even though it will function as a proper name from v3 onward. The verse straddles the transition between ha-adam of chapters 2–4 (the category) and Adam of chapter 5 forward (the person). The text uses the same word for both because in the world of the chapter they are the same word.

3
Adam lived one hundred and thirty years, and fathered a son in his own likeness, after his own image, and called his name Shet.
וַֽיְחִ֣י אָדָ֗ם שְׁלֹשִׁ֤ים וּמְאַת֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בִּדְמוּת֖וֹ כְּצַלְמ֑וֹ וַיִּקְרָ֥א אֶת־שְׁמ֖וֹ שֵֽׁת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

The image is heritable. Genesis 1:26–27 said humankind was made in the image and likeness of the Elohim. Now 5:3 says Seth was fathered in Adam's own likeness, after his own image — the exact same pair (demut and tselem) applied to human-to-human transmission. What Adam received from the Elohim he passes to Seth. The chapter quietly asserts that the imago is inheritable — propagated through reproduction. The genealogy that follows is, in this frame, a chain of imago-bearing across generations. The chapter does not need to repeat the claim — the heritability is established here and assumed for every subsequent name on the list.

4
The days of Adam after he fathered Shet were eight hundred years, and he fathered sons and daughters.
וַיִּֽהְי֣וּ יְמֵי־אָדָ֗ם אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־שֵׁ֔ת שְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵאֹ֖ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
5
All the days that Adam lived were nine hundred and thirty years, and he died.
וַיִּֽהְי֞וּ כָּל־יְמֵ֤י אָדָם֙ אֲשֶׁר־חַ֔י תְּשַׁ֤ע מֵאוֹת֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The first complete lifespan. Adam's 930 years opens the chapter's running question: how to read the pre-Flood ages. The numbers across the chapter range from 365 (Enoch, removed before death) to 969 (Methuselah, the longest); the non-Enoch average is in the 900s. Three principal frameworks for reading them:

Literal — pre-Flood conditions supported lifespans now lost. The post-Flood drop (Gen 11 patriarchs live 148–600, dropping toward 100s by Abraham) marks a real biological/atmospheric shift.

Symbolic/numerological — the numbers encode meaning. Enoch's 365 mirrors the solar year. Methushelach's 969 = 187 + 182 + 600, exactly the years from his fathering of Lamech (5:25) through Lamech's fathering of Noah (5:28) through Noah's age at the Flood (7:6) — Methushelach dies the year the Flood begins.

ANE comparative — Mesopotamian king-lists give pre-Flood reigns of 28,800; 36,000; 43,200 years. Genesis 5's ages, however striking by modern standards, are demythologized relative to these traditions — scaled drastically down. The chapter is in conversation with longer-still numbers it does not adopt.

The text gives the numbers flat. The fact that they hold together arithmetically, mirror astronomical cycles, and stand in conversation with ANE precedents is recoverable by reading. The Hebrew demands no defense and offers no apology.

The verse closes with the formula that will repeat for every patriarch except Enoch: vayamot — and he died.

6
Shet lived one hundred and five years, and fathered Enosh.
וַֽיְחִי־שֵׁ֕ת חָמֵ֥שׁ שָׁנִ֖ים וּמְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־אֱנֽוֹשׁ׃
7
Shet lived after he fathered Enosh eight hundred and seven years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־שֵׁ֗ת אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־אֱנ֔וֹשׁ שֶׁ֣בַע שָׁנִ֔ים וּשְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
8
All the days of Shet were nine hundred and twelve years, and he died.
וַיִּֽהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵי־שֵׁ֔ת שְׁתֵּ֤ים עֶשְׂרֵה֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּתְשַׁ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (ס)
9
Enosh lived ninety years, and fathered Kenan.
וַֽיְחִ֥י אֱנ֖וֹשׁ תִּשְׁעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־קֵינָֽן׃
10
Enosh lived after he fathered Kenan eight hundred and fifteen years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִ֣י אֱנ֗וֹשׁ אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־קֵינָ֔ן חֲמֵ֤שׁ עֶשְׂרֵה֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
11
All the days of Enosh were nine hundred and five years, and he died.
וַיִּֽהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵ֣י אֱנ֔וֹשׁ חָמֵ֣שׁ שָׁנִ֔ים וּתְשַׁ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (ס)
12
Kenan lived seventy years, and fathered Mahalalel.
וַֽיְחִ֥י קֵינָ֖ן שִׁבְעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־מַֽהֲלַלְאֵֽל׃
13
Kenan lived after he fathered Mahalalel eight hundred and forty years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַיְחִ֣י קֵינָ֗ן אַחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־מַֽהֲלַלְאֵ֔ל אַרְבָּעִ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
14
All the days of Kenan were nine hundred and ten years, and he died.
וַיִּֽהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵ֣י קֵינָ֔ן עֶ֣שֶׂר שָׁנִ֔ים וּתְשַׁ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (ס)
15
Mahalalel lived sixty-five years, and fathered Yered.
וַֽיְחִ֣י מַֽהֲלַלְאֵ֔ל חָמֵ֥שׁ שָׁנִ֖ים וְשִׁשִּׁ֣ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־יָֽרֶד׃
16
Mahalalel lived after he fathered Yered eight hundred and thirty years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִ֣י מַֽהֲלַלְאֵ֗ל אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־יֶ֔רֶד שְׁלֹשִׁ֣ים שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
17
All the days of Mahalalel were eight hundred and ninety-five years, and he died.
וַיִּהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵ֣י מַהֲלַלְאֵ֔ל חָמֵ֤שׁ וְתִשְׁעִים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (ס)
18
Yered lived one hundred and sixty-two years, and fathered Chanoch.
וַֽיְחִי־יֶ֕רֶד שְׁתַּ֧יִם וְשִׁשִּׁ֛ים שָׁנָ֖ה וּמְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־חֲנֽוֹךְ׃
19
Yered lived after he fathered Chanoch eight hundred years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־יֶ֗רֶד אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־חֲנ֔וֹךְ שְׁמֹנֶ֥ה מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
20
All the days of Yered were nine hundred and sixty-two years, and he died.
וַיִּֽהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵי־יֶ֔רֶד שְׁתַּ֤יִם וְשִׁשִּׁים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּתְשַׁ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (פ)
21
Chanoch lived sixty-five years, and fathered Methushelach.
וַֽיְחִ֣י חֲנ֔וֹךְ חָמֵ֥שׁ וְשִׁשִּׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־מְתוּשָֽׁלַח׃
22
Chanoch walked about with the Elohim after he fathered Methushelach three hundred years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַיִּתְהַלֵּ֨ךְ חֲנ֜וֹךְ אֶת־הָֽאֱלֹהִ֗ים אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־מְתוּשֶׁ֔לַח שְׁלֹ֥שׁ מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The verbal echo. Chanoch vayithallech — hithpael of הָלַךְ — walks about. This is the same form used in 3:8 of YHWH-Elohim walking about in the garden. The verb's iterative force means habitual, at-home walking; it is not a single event. Chanoch is with (אֶת et) the Elohim in the way the Elohim was in the garden — present, moving, at home. The chapter has set up an inversion of the post-garden estrangement: where 3:8 ended with the human and his woman hiding from the divine presence in the garden, 5:22 has Chanoch walking with the divine presence for three centuries. The estrangement is not the final state.

23
All the days of Chanoch were three hundred and sixty-five years.
וַיְהִ֖י כָּל־יְמֵ֣י חֲנ֑וֹךְ חָמֵ֤שׁ וְשִׁשִּׁים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁלֹ֥שׁ מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

365 — a number that means. Chanoch's lifespan is the shortest in the chapter, and the number is unmistakably resonant: 365 days = one solar year. The number signals completion / cycle / solar circuit. Later traditions (1 Enoch, Jubilees, the Astronomical Book) build extensive solar-calendar cosmologies around Chanoch — the figure becomes the founder of the calendrical and astronomical sciences in the apocryphal tradition. The number's resonance may be the seed of those later traditions, or those traditions may be reading the resonance the chapter already carries. Either way, the lifespan-number is doing work the other patriarchs' numbers do not.

24
Chanoch walked about with the Elohim — and he was not, for the Elohim took him.
וַיִּתְהַלֵּ֥ךְ חֲנ֖וֹךְ אֶת־הָֽאֱלֹהִ֑ים וְאֵינֶ֕נּוּ כִּֽי־לָקַ֥ח אֹת֖וֹ אֱלֹהִֽים׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 24

The broken formula. Every patriarch in the chapter ends with vayamot — and he died. Chanoch is the exception. The chapter's stereotyped death-formula is replaced with: v'einennu ki laqach oto elohim — and he was not, for the Elohim took him.

The verb לָקַח laqach is the same verb used in 2 Kgs 2:9–11 for Elijah's removal ('YHWH was about to take Elijah up to heaven by a whirlwind'). The Chanoch and Elijah passages are the two clearest cases in the Hebrew Bible of bodily removal-without-death, and they share vocabulary. The post-canonical tradition that develops around Chanoch — 1 Enoch, 2 Enoch, the Hekhalot literature, the Watchers narratives — builds on this single broken-pattern verse and what is not said in it.

What the text does say: he was not, the Elohim took him. What the text does not say: where to, in what form, for what purpose. The silence is at least as significant as the taking. WoH renders straight and notes that the Elohim-as-takers register (a plural collective acting on a human) connects to 1:26 let us make humankind and 3:22 the human has become like one of us — the third clear instance in Genesis of the plural Elohim acting in concert. Adam is removed from access to the tree of life (3:22–24); Chanoch is taken to whatever place the Elohim go to. The traffic is asymmetric: the Elohim can take, the humans cannot reach back.

25
Methushelach lived one hundred and eighty-seven years, and fathered Lamech.
וַיְחִ֣י מְתוּשֶׁ֔לַח שֶׁ֧בַע וּשְׁמֹנִ֛ים שָׁנָ֖ה וּמְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד אֶת־לָֽמֶךְ׃
26
Methushelach lived after he fathered Lamech seven hundred and eighty-two years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִ֣י מְתוּשֶׁ֗לַח אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־לֶ֔מֶךְ שְׁתַּ֤יִם וּשְׁמוֹנִים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁבַ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
27
All the days of Methushelach were nine hundred and sixty-nine years, and he died.
וַיִּהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵ֣י מְתוּשֶׁ֔לַח תֵּ֤שַׁע וְשִׁשִּׁים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּתְשַׁ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 27

969 — the year of the Flood. Methushelach's lifespan is the longest recorded in the Hebrew Bible. The number is structurally tied to the chronology of the Flood: Methushelach is 187 when he fathers Lamech (v25); Lamech is 182 when he fathers Noah (v28); Noah is 600 when the Flood begins (7:6). 187 + 182 + 600 = 969 — exactly Methushelach's recorded lifespan. The midrashic reading (Rashi) takes this as deliberate: Methushelach dies in the year the Flood begins, and the Flood is held back during his lifetime out of regard for him. The arithmetic is the kind of structural detail the chapter expects its reader to notice — names mean things, ages mean things, and the chapter's numerical chains carry meaning beyond mere chronology.

28
Lamech lived one hundred and eighty-two years, and fathered a son.
וַֽיְחִי־לֶ֕מֶךְ שְׁתַּ֧יִם וּשְׁמֹנִ֛ים שָׁנָ֖ה וּמְאַ֣ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֖וֹלֶד בֵּֽן׃
29
He called his name Noah, saying, "This one will comfort us from our work and from the hardship of our hands, from the ground which YHWH has cursed."
וַיִּקְרָ֧א אֶת־שְׁמ֛וֹ נֹ֖חַ לֵאמֹ֑ר זֶ֠ה יְנַחֲמֵ֤נוּ מִֽמַּעֲשֵׂ֙נוּ֙ וּמֵעִצְּב֣וֹן יָדֵ֔ינוּ מִן־הָ֣אֲדָמָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר אֵֽרְרָ֖הּ יְהוָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

The chapter's hope. Lamech (the Shet-line Lamech, not the Cain-line Lamech of 4:18–24 with the vengeance-song) names his son נֹחַ Noach. The naming-speech supplies a folk etymology: זֶה יְנַחֲמֵנוּ zeh yenachmenu — this one will comfort us. The verb נָחַם nacham 'comfort' is the etymological gloss, though the actual derivation of the name is more likely from נוּחַ nuach 'to rest, settle'. The naming is by sound-association, not strict morphology.

What the speech registers is the curse still operating: עִצְּבוֹן itzavon (the same hardship-noun applied to Cain's mother in 3:16 and to the ground-cursed human in 3:17) is the term Lamech uses for his generation's condition. The ground YHWH cursed in 3:17 is named explicitly; the curse-formula return after twenty centuries of generations. The Shet-line acknowledges the burden and names a child in hope of relief from it.

The verse closes Genesis 5 on a note of expectation. The hope will be partially answered: in 8:21, YHWH says 'I will not again curse the ground because of the human' — the same curse, partially lifted. Noah's name carries the chapter's longing forward into the Flood narrative.

30
Lamech lived after he fathered Noah five hundred and ninety-five years, and fathered sons and daughters.
וַֽיְחִי־לֶ֗מֶךְ אַֽחֲרֵי֙ הוֹלִיד֣וֹ אֶת־נֹ֔חַ חָמֵ֤שׁ וְתִשְׁעִים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וַחֲמֵ֥שׁ מֵאֹ֖ת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֥וֹלֶד בָּנִ֖ים וּבָנֽוֹת׃
31
All the days of Lamech were seven hundred and seventy-seven years, and he died.
וַֽיְהִי֙ כָּל־יְמֵי־לֶ֔מֶךְ שֶׁ֤בַע וְשִׁבְעִים֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וּשְׁבַ֥ע מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 31

The number Lamech's namesake claimed. The Cain-line Lamech (4:24) sang his self-arrogated vengeance-multiplier: if Cain is avenged sevenfold, then Lamech seventy-and-seven. The Shet-line Lamech lives exactly 777 years — the same numeric structure. The numerical echo across the two Lamechs is unmistakable, though the chapter offers no comment. The Cain-Lamech boasted 77; the Shet-Lamech lived 777. The two are placed in the text at a numerical distance the reader can either notice or not.

32
Noah was five hundred years old; and Noah fathered Shem, Ham, and Yafet.
וַֽיְהִי־נֹ֕חַ בֶּן־חֲמֵ֥שׁ מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וַיּ֣וֹלֶד נֹ֔חַ אֶת־שֵׁ֖ם אֶת־חָ֥ם וְאֶת־יָֽפֶת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 32

The chapter ends without closing Noah's life. Every other patriarch in chapter 5 has both ages — at fathering and at death. Noah's death-formula is held back; only his age at fathering is given (500). The next chapter opens the Flood narrative, and Noah's lifespan will close only at 9:29, after the Flood, the covenant, and the vineyard incident. The chapter's pattern is broken structurally: Chanoch had no death (he was taken); Noah has no death yet (the Flood is coming). The toledot-pattern that has structured 30 verses is opened back up at the end. The three sons named — Shem, Ham, Yafet — will become the three lines of the post-Flood world (chapter 10's table of nations).

Chapter 6

Genesis 6 — The sons of the Elohim and the Flood announced

1
When humankind began to multiply on the face of the ground, and daughters were born to them,
וַֽיְהִי֙ כִּֽי־הֵחֵ֣ל הָֽאָדָ֔ם לָרֹ֖ב עַל־פְּנֵ֣י הָֽאֲדָמָ֑ה וּבָנ֖וֹת יֻלְּד֥וּ לָהֶֽם׃
2
the sons of the Elohim saw that the daughters of the human were good; and they took wives for themselves — any they chose.
וַיִּרְא֤וּ בְנֵי־הָֽאֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־בְּנ֣וֹת הָֽאָדָ֔ם כִּ֥י טֹבֹ֖ת הֵ֑נָּה וַיִּקְח֤וּ לָהֶם֙ נָשִׁ֔ים מִכֹּ֖ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר בָּחָֽרוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The chapter's keystone verse. Bnei ha-elohim — the sons of the Elohim — names a class of beings. The phrase recurs in Job 1:6, 2:1, 38:7, always in reference to a divine council — figures who attend YHWH and respond to him. The phrase does not refer to human Sethites or Israelite rulers; the lexical pattern across the Hebrew Bible is consistent.

This is the third explicit Genesis appearance of a plural-divine register, following 1:26 ('let us make humankind in our image') and 3:22 ('the human has become like one of us'). In 6:2 the plural is no longer voicing internal-council speech — it has sons, and those sons are agents on the land.

Four traditional readings. (1) Fallen angels — the Second Temple reading, dominant in 1 Enoch, Jubilees, the Watchers tradition, Jude 6, and 2 Peter 2:4. (2) Royal or judicial figures — Targum Onkelos' 'sons of the nobles', taking elohim in its 'authorities, judges' sense (cf. Ps 82). (3) The Sethite line — Augustine's reading, dominant in Western Christianity from late antiquity: the godly Sethite men paired with wayward Cainite women. (4) Divine beings — modern critical consensus, varying in degree of literalism.

The Hebrew text supports (1) and (4) most readily by the phrase's lexical pattern. The Augustine (3) reading requires assigning the article ha- before elohim a Sethite-line referent that has no other warrant in Genesis, and requires the b'not ha-adam phrase to refer to Cainites only — both inferences absent from the text. The text says what it says: the sons of the Elohim took the daughters of the human (humankind in general).

WoH reads the text straight. The Elohim is a multi-being class. Some of its members (or their sons — the chapter does not clarify whether the b'nei are the Elohim themselves or a generation downstream) interact reproductively with human women. The interaction is broad — mikkol asher bacharu, from any whom they chose, the verb (בָּחַר bachar) elsewhere used for YHWH's covenantal choice of Israel.

The consequence in v3 is divine withdrawal of breath — a limit on the duration the divine ruach will indwell human bodies. The consequence in v4 is the Nephilim — recognizable named heroes whose name will fall on this generation's offspring. The chapter does not explicitly condemn the union; it announces a limit and a generation.

3
YHWH said, "My breath shall not abide in the human forever — since he is flesh after all. His days shall be one hundred and twenty years."
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יְהוָ֗ה לֹֽא־יָד֨וֹן רוּחִ֤י בָֽאָדָם֙ לְעֹלָ֔ם בְּשַׁגַּ֖ם ה֣וּא בָשָׂ֑ר וְהָי֣וּ יָמָ֔יו מֵאָ֥ה וְעֶשְׂרִ֖ים שָׁנָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Three philological knots in a single verse.

Yadon — morphologically unique. Three roots compete: דּוּן/דִּין 'judge, contend', a hypothetical 'remain, dwell', and נָדַן 'sheath, contain'. The semantic stakes are real: contention (struggle), abiding (indwelling), or sheathing (housing). WoH chooses abide — making the verse a temporal limit on divine indwelling. The parallel grammar then becomes coherent with v2: the Elohim's sons took human women; YHWH's response is to bound the indwelling-time of his breath in human bodies.

B'shagam — another grammatical hapax. Read as ב + שֶׁ + גַּם ('in that also'), the verse becomes resigned-causal: 'since he is flesh after all'. Some philologists read it from שָׁגָה shagah ('to err, stray'), giving 'in their straying he is flesh' — which would tie v3's pronouncement directly to the union with the Elohim's sons.

Hundred and twenty years — either a cap on human lifespan or a countdown to the Flood. Both readings have ancient pedigree. The Targum Onkelos and Rashi favor countdown: 120 years of grace until destruction. Many modern translations favor cap: human lifespan reduced from the Genesis 5 register to a maximum of 120. Moses dies at exactly 120 (Deut 34:7), which can support either reading. Note that the immediate post-Flood patriarchs in Gen 11 still live well over 120 years (Shem 600, Arpachshad 438, Shelah 433, etc.) — only by Abraham (175), Isaac (180), Jacob (147), Joseph (110) does the human lifespan land in the modern range. The 120-cap takes generations to take effect under the lifespan reading; under the countdown reading, the 120 is already complete by 7:6 (Noah is 600 years old at the Flood; the countdown to the Flood started 120 years prior, when Noah was 480 — a chronology the chapter doesn't explicitly anchor).

4
The Nephilim were on the land in those days — and also afterward, when the sons of the Elohim came in to the daughters of the human and they bore to them. These were the mighty ones who were from of old, men of name.
הַנְּפִלִ֞ים הָי֣וּ בָאָרֶץ֮ בַּיָּמִ֣ים הָהֵם֒ וְגַ֣ם אַֽחֲרֵי־כֵ֗ן אֲשֶׁ֨ר יָבֹ֜אוּ בְּנֵ֤י הָֽאֱלֹהִים֙ אֶל־בְּנ֣וֹת הָֽאָדָ֔ם וְיָלְד֖וּ לָהֶ֑ם הֵ֧מָּה הַגִּבֹּרִ֛ים אֲשֶׁ֥ר מֵעוֹלָ֖ם אַנְשֵׁ֥י הַשֵּֽׁם׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The Nephilim are named. הַנְּפִלִים ha-Nephilim — the fallen ones / the giants — appear here and only once again in the Hebrew Bible: Numbers 13:33, where the spies sent into Canaan report seeing them. The Numbers reference creates a long-running textual puzzle: how do the Nephilim survive the Flood (Gen 7) if 'every living being on the face of the land was wiped out, from human to cattle, to creeping thing, to bird of the skies' (7:23)? The Numbers text takes their post-Flood presence as a given. Tradition has resolved this in different ways — hyperbole on the spies' part, localized Flood, re-emergence from one of Noah's daughters-in-law (Sefer ha-Yashar), the Anakim-as-different-Nephilim. The text refuses to comment.

And also afterward, when the sons of the Elohim came in — the temporal qualifier אַחֲרֵי־כֵן acharei-khen ('after that, afterward') extends the Nephilim's presence beyond the antediluvian period. The phrase signals that the union of the b'nei ha-elohim and b'not ha-adam was not a one-time event — it continued into a later era. This is the verse on which much of the Numbers 13 puzzle turns.

The mighty ones who were from of old, men of name. The three apposed phrases identify the offspring (and/or the Nephilim themselves) as the named heroes of legendary antiquity — the Hebrew Bible's only direct reference to the demigod / hero class that Mesopotamian, Greek, Egyptian, and other ANE mythologies developed in detail (the Sumerian apkallu, the Greek heroes, the Egyptian henmemet). The chapter places its own narrative explicitly within the broader ANE heroic-age framework — and routes that framework through the specific theological grammar of the Elohim and their sons.

The verse names the Nephilim and identifies them with the legendary heroes; the chapter then immediately turns (v5) to YHWH's grief over the wickedness of the human — without explicitly blaming the union, but with the textual proximity strongly implicating it. The chapter does not say the Elohim's sons caused the wickedness; the chapter says and then YHWH saw that humanity was wicked. The cause-effect is left to the reader.

5
YHWH saw that the wickedness of the human was great on the land, and that every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all day long.
וַיַּ֣רְא יְהוָ֔ה כִּ֥י רַבָּ֛ה רָעַ֥ת הָאָדָ֖ם בָּאָ֑רֶץ וְכָל־יֵ֙צֶר֙ מַחְשְׁבֹ֣ת לִבּ֔וֹ רַ֥ק רַ֖ע כָּל־הַיּֽוֹם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

Total assessment. The verse stacks intensifiers: rabbah ('great'), kol-yetzer ('every inclination'), raq ra ('only evil'), kol-ha-yom ('all day'). The picture is comprehensive corruption — the entire pattern of human inner life judged in one sweeping sentence.

The noun יֵצֶר yetzer ('inclination, formation') comes from the same root as יָצַר yatsar ('to form'), the potter's verb that shaped the human in 2:7. What was formed in clay is now forming evil. The verb that named the divine craftsmanship is being used for the human's inner mental craftsmanship. The yetzer noun will become a major term in later Jewish ethics (yetzer ha-tov / yetzer ha-ra, the good inclination and the evil inclination). Here it appears in its first major instance, and the verdict on this generation's yetzer is unsparing.

6
YHWH regretted that he had made the human on the land, and he was grieved to his heart.
וַיִּנָּ֣חֶם יְהוָ֔ה כִּֽי־עָשָׂ֥ה אֶת־הָֽאָדָ֖ם בָּאָ֑רֶץ וַיִּתְעַצֵּ֖ב אֶל־לִבּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

The divine grief. Two verbs in tight pair. Vayinnachem — YHWH regretted, was sorry, changed his mind. The same verb is used in 1 Sam 15:11 of YHWH regretting making Saul king. The Hebrew is plain anthropopathic: YHWH-as-actor regrets and is sorry.

Vayit'atsev — he was grieved — uses the same root עצב as etzev in 3:16 (the woman's childbearing-hardship), 3:17 (the human's ground-working-hardship), and 5:29 (Lamech naming Noah from this very hardship). The chapter has just deployed the root in a stunning new register: the creator who gave etzev to his creatures now receives the same root upon himself. The pain of creation runs in both directions.

Much theological tradition has labored to explain how an omniscient and impassible God can regret or be grieved. The Hebrew makes no such concession; the text uses the vocabulary it uses. The WoH frame, which reads the Elohim as a class of advanced beings rather than as an omniscient absolute, has no such interpretive labor to perform — beings of high but finite capacity can regret what they have set in motion.

7
YHWH said, "I will wipe out the human whom I shaped from the face of the ground — from human to cattle, to creeping thing, to bird of the skies — for I regret that I have made them."
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר יְהוָ֗ה אֶמְחֶ֨ה אֶת־הָאָדָ֤ם אֲשֶׁר־בָּרָ֙אתִי֙ מֵעַל֙ פְּנֵ֣י הָֽאֲדָמָ֔ה מֵֽאָדָם֙ עַד־בְּהֵמָ֔ה עַד־רֶ֖מֶשׂ וְעַד־ע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם כִּ֥י נִחַ֖מְתִּי כִּ֥י עֲשִׂיתִֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The verb of erasure. Emcheh — מָחָה machah — is the verb for wiping clean, blotting out, erasing. The verb's primary register is not destruction-with-fire but erasure-with-cloth: wiping a dish (2 Kgs 21:13), wiping tears (Isa 25:8), blotting out a name from a record (Ex 32:32). The intention announced in v7 is clean-slate, not punishment-as-spectacle. The Flood will be — in the chapter's word — a wiping.

The scope is total: the human plus the entire animal kingdom (cattle, creeping things, birds). The same five categories that 1:24–26 itemized for the sixth day are now itemized in reverse-procedure for the wiping. The creation-week of chapter 1 and the wiping of chapter 6 use parallel categorical language; the un-creation matches the creation in structure.

8
And Noah found favor in the eyes of YHWH.
וְנֹ֕חַ מָ֥צָא חֵ֖ן בְּעֵינֵ֥י יְהוָֽה׃ (פ)
Translation Commentary Verse 8

The exception. Eight words in Hebrew, five in English: a single sentence that names the thread the Flood narrative will run on. Matsa chen b'einei YHWH — found favor in the eyes of YHWH — is the standard idiom for divine regard (cf. Gen 19:19, Ex 33:12-17, Ruth 2:10). The phrase will recur throughout the Hebrew Bible at moments of divine selection.

Note the vav of contrast that opens the verse: וְ v- 'and/but'. The previous verse closed YHWH's wipe-out decision; this verse opens with a reversing conjunction. The Hebrew narrative grammar signals: the wiping will not be total. Noah is the exception. The thread is identified before the Flood begins.

9
These are the begettings of Noah. Noah was a righteous man, blameless in his generations. Noah walked about with the Elohim.
אֵ֚לֶּה תּוֹלְדֹ֣ת נֹ֔חַ נֹ֗חַ אִ֥ישׁ צַדִּ֛יק תָּמִ֥ים הָיָ֖ה בְּדֹֽרֹתָ֑יו אֶת־הָֽאֱלֹהִ֖ים הִֽתְהַלֶּךְ־נֹֽחַ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

Noah is Chanoch's only structural parallel. The phrase et ha-elohim hithallech Noach — 'with the Elohim Noah walked-about' — is the same construction used of Chanoch in 5:22, 24: same verb (hithallech, hithpael of halach), same direct-object marker (et, normally a marker of direct object rather than the preposition 'with'). The two pre-Flood figures the chapter singles out for this distinct verbal-relational marker with the Elohim are Chanoch and Noah. The pattern is conspicuous: of all the patriarchs in Genesis 5, only Chanoch carries the phrase; of all the figures in Genesis 6, only Noah. The verbal parallel ties their roles structurally — Chanoch is taken before the Flood, Noah is preserved through it.

Tzaddiq tamim — righteous, blameless — pairs two technical terms. צַדִּיק tzaddiq covers moral-juridical uprightness; תָּמִים tamim covers ritual-and-moral wholeness, the same word used of unblemished sacrificial animals (Lev 1:3). The pair names Noah as both legally upright and structurally whole.

B'dorotav — in his generations (plural) — invites the famous rabbinic question: was Noah righteous absolutely, or only relatively to his very wicked generation? Rashi cites both readings (one favorable, one unfavorable). The plural dorot ('generations') is unusual — Noah is righteous across multiple generations, the rabbinic tradition wonders whether the qualifier limits or commends. The Hebrew does not adjudicate.

10
Noah fathered three sons — Shem, Ham, and Yafet.
וַיּ֥וֹלֶד נֹ֖חַ שְׁלֹשָׁ֣ה בָנִ֑ים אֶת־שֵׁ֖ם אֶת־חָ֥ם וְאֶת־יָֽפֶת׃
11
The land was corrupted before the Elohim, and the land was filled with violence.
וַתִּשָּׁחֵ֥ת הָאָ֖רֶץ לִפְנֵ֣י הָֽאֱלֹהִ֑ים וַתִּמָּלֵ֥א הָאָ֖רֶץ חָמָֽס׃
12
The Elohim saw the land, and behold — it was corrupted; for all flesh had corrupted its way upon the land.
וַיַּ֧רְא אֱלֹהִ֛ים אֶת־הָאָ֖רֶץ וְהִנֵּ֣ה נִשְׁחָ֑תָה כִּֽי־הִשְׁחִ֧ית כָּל־בָּשָׂ֛ר אֶת־דַּרְכּ֖וֹ עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 12

The verb of corruption is the verb of destruction. Shachat — שָׁחַת — covers both 'corrupt, ruin' (in the passive niphal: the land was corrupted) and 'destroy' (in the active hiphil: all flesh corrupted [intransitively-causative]; YHWH will destroy). The chapter is playing on the bidirectional sense:

  • v11: vatishachet ha-aretz — the land was corrupted (niphal, passive — what happened to it).
  • v12: hishchit kol basar et darko — all flesh corrupted its way (hiphil, active — what they did).
  • v13: hinni mashchitam et ha-aretz — YHWH is destroying them with the land (hiphil participle — what YHWH will do).

Same root, three stems, three speakers. The Flood is not external punishment but the natural escalation of a verb that the land was already enacting on itself. The chapter's diagnosis is structurally unified: corruption is what is happening; destruction is what will happen; both share a root.

13
The Elohim said to Noah, "The end of all flesh has come before me, for the land is filled with violence because of them. Behold, I am about to destroy them with the land.
וַיֹּ֨אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֜ים לְנֹ֗חַ קֵ֤ץ כָּל־בָּשָׂר֙ בָּ֣א לְפָנַ֔י כִּֽי־מָלְאָ֥ה הָאָ֛רֶץ חָמָ֖ס מִפְּנֵיהֶ֑ם וְהִנְנִ֥י מַשְׁחִיתָ֖ם אֶת־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The first apocalyptic announcement. Qets kol basar — the end of all flesh — opens a register that will run through Daniel, Joel, and later prophetic-apocalyptic literature. Here at its origin, qets names a real terminus: not the eschatological End in the later technical sense, but the first end-of-the-world the Hebrew Bible records. The Flood is presented to Noah in this register — not as judgment-on-individuals but as cosmic terminus.

Note also: mashchitam et ha-aretz is grammatically ambiguous. The most common reading is I am about to destroy them with/and the land (a parallel destruction). Less commonly read: I am about to destroy them from the land (preposition supplied). The Hebrew accusative et can carry either sense in this construction. WoH chooses with the land, preserving the dual destruction — the corruption-saturated land goes down with the violators on it.

14
Make for yourself an ark of gopher wood. Make the ark with compartments, and coat it inside and out with pitch.
עֲשֵׂ֤ה לְךָ֙ תֵּבַ֣ת עֲצֵי־גֹ֔פֶר קִנִּ֖ים תַּֽעֲשֶׂ֣ה אֶת־הַתֵּבָ֑ה וְכָֽפַרְתָּ֥ אֹתָ֛הּ מִבַּ֥יִת וּמִח֖וּץ בַּכֹּֽפֶר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 14

Two unusual words. Tevah — תֵּבָה — is the rare noun used here and only in Exodus 2:3, 5 (for the basket carrying infant Moses). The two great Hebrew Bible water-deliverance narratives use the same word, with the same pitch-coating instruction. The verbal parallel binds the Flood and Exodus narratives at the level of vocabulary; the rescue-vessel is, in Hebrew, a tevah twice.

Atzei-gofer — gopher wood — appears only in this chapter. The wood is unidentified; possibilities include cypress (the Akkadian gushuru), cedar, pine. The Hebrew is silent. The English 'gopher' transliterates the Hebrew without identifying the species. The word is one of the genuinely lost terms of the Hebrew Bible.

15
This is how you shall make it: the length of the ark three hundred cubits, its breadth fifty cubits, and its height thirty cubits.
וְזֶ֕ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר תַּֽעֲשֶׂ֖ה אֹתָ֑הּ שְׁלֹ֧שׁ מֵא֣וֹת אַמָּ֗ה אֹ֚רֶךְ הַתֵּבָ֔ה חֲמִשִּׁ֤ים אַמָּה֙ רָחְבָּ֔הּ וּשְׁלֹשִׁ֥ים אַמָּ֖ה קוֹמָתָֽהּ׃
16
Make a tsohar for the ark, and finish it to a cubit from above. Set the door of the ark in its side; make it with lower, second, and third decks.
צֹ֣הַר ׀ תַּֽעֲשֶׂ֣ה לַתֵּבָ֗ה וְאֶל־אַמָּה֙ תְּכַלֶ֣נָּה מִלְמַ֔עְלָה וּפֶ֥תַח הַתֵּבָ֖ה בְּצִדָּ֣הּ תָּשִׂ֑ים תַּחְתִּיִּ֛ם שְׁנִיִּ֥ם וּשְׁלִשִׁ֖ים תַּֽעֲשֶֽׂהָ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

Tsohar — light or roof? The noun צֹהַר tsohar appears only here in the Hebrew Bible. Two derivations have been proposed: (1) from the root צהר meaning 'shine, be bright' — giving 'a light-opening, window, skylight'. (2) the same root in a different stem meaning 'noon, midday' — giving 'a covering open to the noon-sun'. The traditional reading is window (KJV), supplying light to the otherwise-windowless ark; some rabbinic readings imagined the tsohar as a gem providing supernatural light. The construction al ammah t'khalenna milmalah ('and to a cubit you shall complete it from above') is also unclear — is the tsohar itself a cubit-wide opening, or is the ark's roof being capped a cubit above? The verse's architectural detail is one of the hardest passages in Genesis to render with confidence. WoH renders tsohar transliterated to preserve the obscurity.

17
As for me — behold, I am about to bring the deluge of waters upon the land, to destroy all flesh in which is the breath of life from under the skies. Everything that is on the land shall perish.
וַאֲנִ֗י הִנְנִי֩ מֵבִ֨יא אֶת־הַמַּבּ֥וּל מַ֙יִם֙ עַל־הָאָ֔רֶץ לְשַׁחֵ֣ת כָּל־בָּשָׂ֗ר אֲשֶׁר־בּוֹ֙ ר֣וּחַ חַיִּ֔ים מִתַּ֖חַת הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם כֹּ֥ל אֲשֶׁר־בָּאָ֖רֶץ יִגְוָֽע׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

Mabbul — the deluge. The word appears only here in Genesis-through-the-flood (chapters 6–11) and once in Ps 29:10. It is the specific noun for the Noahic flood — not the generic word for flood (which would be shittafon or mashbar). The exclusive use signals theological-historical uniqueness: this is the deluge, not a flood. The English 'deluge' preserves the specificity that 'flood' obscures.

Ruach chayim — the breath of life — recalls 2:7 ('he breathed into his nostrils the breath of life'). The same divine breath that animated the human at creation is now what is being un-given. The chapter's reversal-of-creation grammar continues.

18
I will establish my covenant with you; and you shall come into the ark — you, your sons, your wife, and your sons' wives with you.
וַהֲקִמֹתִ֥י אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֖י אִתָּ֑ךְ וּבָאתָ֙ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֔ה אַתָּ֕ה וּבָנֶ֛יךָ וְאִשְׁתְּךָ֥ וּנְשֵֽׁי־בָנֶ֖יךָ אִתָּֽךְ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The first covenant. בְּרִית b'rit — covenant — appears here for the first time in the Hebrew Bible. The organizing concept of Israelite theology — that YHWH binds himself in formal agreement with chosen partners — has its first textual instance with Noah, before the Flood, as part of the ark instructions. The full establishment-formula will come in chapter 9 (with the rainbow as sign); here in 6:18 the promise of covenant is given as the framework within which the ark-building takes place.

The chapter that opens with the dissolution of the boundary between divine and human (the b'nei ha-elohim taking b'not ha-adam) ends with the establishment of a different boundary: a covenantal one. The reproductive-dissolution is replaced by a formal-binding. Two ways of structuring divine-human relation are set side by side in the same chapter; the chapter chooses the second.

19
Of every living thing, of all flesh, two of each you shall bring into the ark to keep alive with you — male and female they shall be.
וּמִכָּל־הָ֠חַי מִֽכָּל־בָּשָׂ֞ר שְׁנַ֧יִם מִכֹּ֛ל תָּבִ֥יא אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֖ה לְהַחֲיֹ֣ת אִתָּ֑ךְ זָכָ֥ר וּנְקֵבָ֖ה יִֽהְיֽוּ׃
20
Of the birds according to their kind, and of the cattle according to their kind, and of every creeping thing of the ground according to its kind — two of each shall come to you, to be kept alive.
מֵהָע֣וֹף לְמִינֵ֗הוּ וּמִן־הַבְּהֵמָה֙ לְמִינָ֔הּ מִכֹּ֛ל רֶ֥מֶשׂ הָֽאֲדָמָ֖ה לְמִינֵ֑הוּ שְׁנַ֧יִם מִכֹּ֛ל יָבֹ֥אוּ אֵלֶ֖יךָ לְהַֽחֲיֽוֹת׃
21
Take for yourself every kind of food that is eaten, and gather it to you — and it shall be food for you and for them."
וְאַתָּ֣ה קַח־לְךָ֗ מִכָּל־מַֽאֲכָל֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר יֵֽאָכֵ֔ל וְאָסַפְתָּ֖ אֵלֶ֑יךָ וְהָיָ֥ה לְךָ֛ וְלָהֶ֖ם לְאָכְלָֽה׃
22
Noah did so — all that the Elohim commanded him, so he did.
וַיַּ֖עַשׂ נֹ֑חַ כְּ֠כֹל אֲשֶׁ֨ר צִוָּ֥ה אֹת֛וֹ אֱלֹהִ֖ים כֵּ֥ן עָשָֽׂה׃ (ס)
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The chapter closes on a single verb. Vaya'as Noach … ken asah — Noah did … so he did. The doubled verb עָשָׂה asah (made/did) frames Noah's response as exact obedience. The same verb that ran through chapter 1 ("the Elohim made the dome", "the Elohim made the luminaries", "the Elohim made the beasts") returns to describe Noah's making-and-doing. The chapter that contemplated wiping out everything the Elohim made (v7) ends with Noah's own making — a small-scale, exact echo of the creation-making.

The verse is short on purpose. The full execution of the ark project — taking 120 years if v3 is read as countdown — is condensed into seven Hebrew words. The chapter is structurally tight: opens with the b'nei ha-elohim dissolving the divine-human boundary, closes with Noah obediently rebuilding within the boundaries the Elohim set. The chapter is bracketed by two opposed images of relation: erotic class-crossing and covenantal class-keeping.

Chapter 7

Genesis 7 — The deluge begins

1
YHWH said to Noah, "Come — you and all your household — into the ark; for I have seen you righteous before me in this generation.
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר יְהוָה֙ לְנֹ֔חַ בֹּֽא־אַתָּ֥ה וְכָל־בֵּיתְךָ֖ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֑ה כִּֽי־אֹתְךָ֥ רָאִ֛יתִי צַדִּ֥יק לְפָנַ֖י בַּדּ֥וֹר הַזֶּֽה׃
2
Of every clean animal you shall take with you seven pairs, male and his female; and of every animal that is not clean, two, male and his female;
מִכֹּ֣ל ׀ הַבְּהֵמָ֣ה הַטְּהוֹרָ֗ה תִּֽקַּח־לְךָ֛ שִׁבְעָ֥ה שִׁבְעָ֖ה אִ֣ישׁ וְאִשְׁתּ֑וֹ וּמִן־הַבְּהֵמָ֡ה אֲ֠שֶׁר לֹ֣א טְהֹרָ֥ה הִ֛וא שְׁנַ֖יִם אִ֥ישׁ וְאִשְׁתּֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

Two textual seams at once.

First, the count contradicts 6:19–20, which instructed Noah to bring two of each — without the clean/unclean distinction. Here in 7:2 a finer instruction is given: seven pairs of clean animals, two of unclean. The two instructions reflect different source-strands in the documentary hypothesis (P at 6:19–20; J at 7:2–3). The redactor preserved both side-by-side. The common harmonization: the second instruction is the specific override for clean animals, with the general two-of-each rule running underneath.

Second, the clean / unclean (tahor / lo tahor) categories appear before the Sinai legislation (Lev 11) that codifies them. The chapter assumes both Noah and the reader know which animals fall in each category. Genesis repeatedly assumes Sinai-categorized phenomena in pre-Sinai narrative: sabbath in 2:2–3, sacrifice in 4:3–5, circumcision (still ahead) in 17. The narrative writes from a post-Sinai standpoint into a pre-Sinai world without flagging the anachronism.

3
Also of the birds of the skies — seven pairs, male and female — to keep seed alive on the face of all the land.
גַּ֣ם מֵע֧וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֛יִם שִׁבְעָ֥ה שִׁבְעָ֖ה זָכָ֣ר וּנְקֵבָ֑ה לְחַיּ֥וֹת זֶ֖רַע עַל־פְּנֵ֥י כָל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
4
For in seven more days I will cause it to rain on the land for forty days and forty nights; and I will wipe out every living thing that I have made from the face of the ground."
כִּי֩ לְיָמִ֨ים ע֜וֹד שִׁבְעָ֗ה אָֽנֹכִי֙ מַמְטִ֣יר עַל־הָאָ֔רֶץ אַרְבָּעִ֣ים י֔וֹם וְאַרְבָּעִ֖ים לָ֑יְלָה וּמָחִ֗יתִי אֶֽת־כָּל־הַיְקוּם֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר עָשִׂ֔יתִי מֵעַ֖ל פְּנֵ֥י הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

Two periods named in one verse. Seven more days (until the Flood begins) plus forty days and forty nights (the rainfall duration). The seven-day countdown is a small grace-period — just enough time to finish loading the ark. The forty-day rainfall is the chapter's central duration. Forty days will return as a structural unit at 8:6 (the wait before opening the window). The number forty, here in its first chronological function in Genesis, will recur throughout the Hebrew Bible as a structural duration: forty years in the wilderness (Numbers 14, 32), forty days on Sinai (Ex 24:18), forty days of Goliath's challenge (1 Sam 17:16), forty days of Jesus' wilderness fast (Mt 4:2). The number's fortyness is a duration-unit, not a calendrical fact.

Umachiti — and I will wipe out — recurs the verb of 6:7. The intention is again erasure, not destruction-by-force.

5
Noah did all that YHWH commanded him.
וַיַּ֖עַשׂ נֹ֑חַ כְּכֹ֥ל אֲשֶׁר־צִוָּ֖הוּ יְהוָֽה׃
6
Noah was six hundred years old when the deluge of waters came upon the land.
וְנֹ֕חַ בֶּן־שֵׁ֥שׁ מֵא֖וֹת שָׁנָ֑ה וְהַמַּבּ֣וּל הָיָ֔ה מַ֖יִם עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
7
Noah came into the ark — and his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives with him — because of the waters of the deluge.
וַיָּ֣בֹא נֹ֗חַ וּ֠בָנָיו וְאִשְׁתּ֧וֹ וּנְשֵֽׁי־בָנָ֛יו אִתּ֖וֹ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֑ה מִפְּנֵ֖י מֵ֥י הַמַּבּֽוּל׃
8
Of the clean animals, and of the animals that are not clean, and of the birds, and of everything that creeps on the ground —
מִן־הַבְּהֵמָה֙ הַטְּהוֹרָ֔ה וּמִן־הַ֨בְּהֵמָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר אֵינֶ֖נָּה טְהֹרָ֑ה וּמִ֨ן־הָע֔וֹף וְכֹ֥ל אֲשֶׁר־רֹמֵ֖שׂ עַל־הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
9
they came to Noah into the ark, two by two, male and female, as the Elohim commanded Noah.
שְׁנַ֨יִם שְׁנַ֜יִם בָּ֧אוּ אֶל־נֹ֛חַ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֖ה זָכָ֣ר וּנְקֵבָ֑ה כַּֽאֲשֶׁ֛ר צִוָּ֥ה אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶת־נֹֽחַ׃
10
After the seven days, the waters of the deluge came upon the land.
וַֽיְהִ֖י לְשִׁבְעַ֣ת הַיָּמִ֑ים וּמֵ֣י הַמַּבּ֔וּל הָי֖וּ עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
11
In the six hundredth year of Noah's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month — on this very day — all the fountains of the great deep burst open, and the floodgates of the skies were opened.
בִּשְׁנַ֨ת שֵׁשׁ־מֵא֤וֹת שָׁנָה֙ לְחַיֵּי־נֹ֔חַ בַּחֹ֙דֶשׁ֙ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י בְּשִׁבְעָֽה־עָשָׂ֥ר י֖וֹם לַחֹ֑דֶשׁ בַּיּ֣וֹם הַזֶּ֗ה נִבְקְעוּ֙ כָּֽל־מַעְיְנֹת֙ תְּה֣וֹם רַבָּ֔ה וַאֲרֻבֹּ֥ת הַשָּׁמַ֖יִם נִפְתָּֽחוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The reversal of Day Two and Day Three. Two cosmic barriers fail simultaneously. From below: kol maayanot t'hom rabbah nivqe'u — all the fountains of the great deep burst open. From above: arubot ha-shamayim niftachu — the floodgates of the skies were opened.

Reading back to Genesis 1: on Day Two (1:6–8) the raqia (dome) was made to separate the upper waters from the lower waters; the upper waters now pour down through opened floodgates. On Day Three (1:9–10) the lower waters were gathered to one place so dry land could appear; those waters now burst forth from below. The Flood is, in the chapter's grammar, the un-doing of the second and third days of creation. The boundary-keeping work of Day Two is reversed (raqia is breached); the gathering-work of Day Three is reversed (waters released back).

T'hom rabbah — the great deep — is the same noun (t'hom) that named the primordial waters in 1:2 ('darkness was over the face of the deep'). The cognate of Akkadian tiāmtu, the same root behind Tiamat of the Enuma Elish. Genesis 1 demythologized the deep; Genesis 7 has it bursting forth again. The chapter is invoking the cosmic-primordial water that was supposedly sealed by the creation week.

The specific date — year 600 of Noah, second month, seventeenth day — is the first absolute date in the Hebrew Bible. The narrative cares about calendrical precision: this is a one-time event with a recorded start-time, not a mythic eternal-recurrence. The Flood narrative spans about a year in the chapter's chronology (from 7:11 to 8:14 — a solar year plus 11 days, matching the lunar/solar calendar gap).

12
The rain was upon the land forty days and forty nights.
וַֽיְהִ֥י הַגֶּ֖שֶׁם עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ אַרְבָּעִ֣ים י֔וֹם וְאַרְבָּעִ֖ים לָֽיְלָה׃
13
On that very same day Noah came in — and Shem, Ham, and Yafet, Noah's sons, and Noah's wife, and the three wives of his sons with them — into the ark,
בְּעֶ֨צֶם הַיּ֤וֹם הַזֶּה֙ בָּ֣א נֹ֔חַ וְשֵׁם־וְחָ֥ם וָיֶ֖פֶת בְּנֵי־נֹ֑חַ וְאֵ֣שֶׁת נֹ֗חַ וּשְׁלֹ֧שֶׁת נְשֵֽׁי־בָנָ֛יו אִתָּ֖ם אֶל־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
14
they and every wild animal according to its kind, and every domesticated animal according to its kind, and every creeping thing that creeps on the land according to its kind, and every bird according to its kind — every bird, every winged thing.
הֵ֜מָּה וְכָל־הַֽחַיָּ֣ה לְמִינָ֗הּ וְכָל־הַבְּהֵמָה֙ לְמִינָ֔הּ וְכָל־הָרֶ֛מֶשׂ הָרֹמֵ֥שׂ עַל־הָאָ֖רֶץ לְמִינֵ֑הוּ וְכָל־הָע֣וֹף לְמִינֵ֔הוּ כֹּ֖ל צִפּ֥וֹר כָּל־כָּנָֽף׃
15
They came to Noah into the ark, two by two, of all flesh in which is the breath of life.
וַיָּבֹ֥אוּ אֶל־נֹ֖חַ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֑ה שְׁנַ֤יִם שְׁנַ֙יִם֙ מִכָּל־הַבָּשָׂ֔ר אֲשֶׁר־בּ֖וֹ ר֥וּחַ חַיִּֽים׃
16
Those that came in, male and female of all flesh, came in as the Elohim had commanded him; and YHWH closed him in.
וְהַבָּאִ֗ים זָכָ֨ר וּנְקֵבָ֤ה מִכָּל־בָּשָׂר֙ בָּ֔אוּ כַּֽאֲשֶׁ֛ר צִוָּ֥ה אֹת֖וֹ אֱלֹהִ֑ים וַיִּסְגֹּ֥ר יְהוָ֖ה בַּֽעֲדֽוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 16

The hand at the door. The verse closes with a tender, almost domestic gesture: vayisgor YHWH baado — and YHWH closed him in. The verb סָגַר sagar (qal, transitive) takes YHWH as subject — not Noah, not a generic 'it was closed'. YHWH personally seals the ark from the outside. The preposition בְּעַד baad ('behind, for the protection of, on behalf of') gives the act a custodial register — YHWH is taking custody of the closing.

The chapter's covenant (6:18) is enacted here in a single gesture. Inside: Noah, his family, the animals. Outside: YHWH. Between: a sealed door. The Flood will commence in the next verse; before it does, the closure-of-protection is made personally. Note the divine-name shift: the same verse calls God Elohim (in the commanding) and YHWH (in the closing) — the chapter is moving between the two names in close proximity, as the documentary hypothesis would predict from a redacted P + J seam, and as the chapter's own grammar absorbs without comment.

17
The deluge was on the land forty days; the waters increased and lifted up the ark, and it rose above the land.
וַֽיְהִ֧י הַמַּבּ֛וּל אַרְבָּעִ֥ים י֖וֹם עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִּרְבּ֣וּ הַמַּ֗יִם וַיִּשְׂאוּ֙ אֶת־הַתֵּבָ֔ה וַתָּ֖רָם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָֽרֶץ׃
18
The waters prevailed and increased greatly on the land, and the ark went on the face of the waters.
וַיִּגְבְּר֥וּ הַמַּ֛יִם וַיִּרְבּ֥וּ מְאֹ֖ד עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַתֵּ֥לֶךְ הַתֵּבָ֖ה עַל־פְּנֵ֥י הַמָּֽיִם׃
19
The waters prevailed more and more upon the land, and all the high mountains under all the skies were covered.
וְהַמַּ֗יִם גָּֽבְר֛וּ מְאֹ֥ד מְאֹ֖ד עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיְכֻסּ֗וּ כָּל־הֶֽהָרִים֙ הַגְּבֹהִ֔ים אֲשֶׁר־תַּ֖חַת כָּל־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
20
The waters prevailed fifteen cubits upward, and the mountains were covered.
חֲמֵ֨שׁ עֶשְׂרֵ֤ה אַמָּה֙ מִלְמַ֔עְלָה גָּבְר֖וּ הַמָּ֑יִם וַיְכֻסּ֖וּ הֶהָרִֽים׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

A draft of fifteen cubits. The ark's height was thirty cubits (6:15). The water rises fifteen cubits above the highest mountains. Rashi reads the symmetry: the ark sat in the water at half its height — fifteen cubits submerged, fifteen above the surface — and floated exactly clearing the highest peaks below. The mountains pass under the ark with the precise clearance of its draft. The chapter does not comment on its own arithmetic; the geometry is left for the reader to assemble.

21
All flesh that moved on the land perished — birds, cattle, wild animals, every swarming creature that swarms on the land — and every human.
וַיִּגְוַ֞ע כָּל־בָּשָׂ֣ר ׀ הָרֹמֵ֣שׂ עַל־הָאָ֗רֶץ בָּע֤וֹף וּבַבְּהֵמָה֙ וּבַ֣חַיָּ֔ה וּבְכָל־הַשֶּׁ֖רֶץ הַשֹּׁרֵ֣ץ עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וְכֹ֖ל הָאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The verb of quiet death. Vayigwa — expired — is distinct from the generic verb for dying. The same verb is used in the Hebrew Bible for the patriarchs' deaths: Abraham expired and was gathered to his people (Gen 25:8); Isaac expired (35:29); Jacob expired (49:33). In each case the register is full-of-years, peaceful, gathered-to-kin. Here the same verb is applied at mass scale: vayigwa kol basar — all flesh expired. The chapter chooses a vocabulary of peaceful expiration to report a violent mass extinction. The narrative refuses spectacle; it lists categories (birds, cattle, wild animals, swarmers, humans) in the order of 1:20–26, and reports that all of them have expired.

22
Everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life, of all that was on the dry ground, died.
כֹּ֡ל אֲשֶׁר֩ נִשְׁמַת־ר֨וּחַ חַיִּ֜ים בְּאַפָּ֗יו מִכֹּ֛ל אֲשֶׁ֥ר בֶּחָֽרָבָ֖ה מֵֽתוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The reversal of 2:7. The phrase נִשְׁמַת רוּחַ חַיִּים nishmat ruach chayim unusually doubles the breath-vocabulary, pairing neshamah with ruach. Compare 2:7, where YHWH-Elohim breathed nishmat chayim (breath of life) into the human's nostrils, and the human became a living being. Here in 7:22 the same vocabulary is used in reverse: everything in whose nostrils was the breath of the spirit of life — died. The animation-by-breath of 2:7 is undone by the un-breath of 7:22. The chapter is reversing creation's specific act of inspiration.

23
He wiped out every living thing on the face of the ground — from human to cattle, to creeping thing, to bird of the skies — they were wiped out from the land. Only Noah remained, and those with him in the ark.
וַיִּ֜מַח אֶֽת־כָּל־הַיְק֣וּם ׀ אֲשֶׁ֣ר ׀ עַל־פְּנֵ֣י הָֽאֲדָמָ֗ה מֵאָדָ֤ם עַד־בְּהֵמָה֙ עַד־רֶ֙מֶשׂ֙ וְעַד־ע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמַ֔יִם וַיִּמָּח֖וּ מִן־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִשָּׁ֧אֶר אַךְ־נֹ֛חַ וַֽאֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּ֖וֹ בַּתֵּבָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

The remnant. The verse closes the chapter with the word that will become a major theological term: vayisha'er ach Noach — only Noah remained. The verb שָׁאַר sha'ar (here in the niphal: was left, remained) is the lexical family of שְׁאֵרִית she'erit — the remnant — the technical term in later prophetic literature for the surviving faithful few preserved through national catastrophe (Isa 10:20–22, Mic 2:12, Jer 31:7, Zeph 3:13). Genesis 7:23 stands at the origin of the Hebrew Bible's remnant-theology: a faithful core preserved through total destruction.

The verse also closes the chapter's wiping-vocabulary: the verb machah — wipe, erase, blot out — appears twice in active and passive: vayyimach ('he wiped') and vayyimmachu ('they were wiped'). The wiping that v4 announced is completed here. The chapter ends with the verb of erasure achieved, and the remnant clause attached.

The restrictive adverb אַךְ ach ('only, just') frames the verse with its key image: from total population to only Noah. The thread of preservation has narrowed to a single household in a sealed wooden box on top of an ocean that has covered the highest mountains.

24
The waters prevailed upon the land one hundred and fifty days.
וַיִּגְבְּר֥וּ הַמַּ֖יִם עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים וּמְאַ֖ת יֽוֹם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

The chapter ends suspended. No release, no recession, no dove yet — just the duration. Chamishim u-meat yom — 150 days of prevailing waters. The chapter ends with the picture: a single floating ark on a sea over a buried world, day 150 of the prevailing. The next chapter opens with vayyizkor Elohim et Noach — and the Elohim remembered Noah. The 150 days are the longest stretch of the Flood narrative without a single divine action recorded. The chapter lets the silence stand.

Chapter 8

Genesis 8 — The waters recede, the altar built

1
The Elohim remembered Noah, and every wild animal and every domesticated animal that was with him in the ark; and the Elohim caused a breath to pass over the land, and the waters subsided.
וַיִּזְכֹּ֤ר אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶת־נֹ֔חַ וְאֵ֤ת כָּל־הַֽחַיָּה֙ וְאֶת־כָּל־הַבְּהֵמָ֔ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר אִתּ֖וֹ בַּתֵּבָ֑ה וַיַּעֲבֵ֨ר אֱלֹהִ֥ים ר֙וּחַ֙ עַל־הָאָ֔רֶץ וַיָּשֹׁ֖כּוּ הַמָּֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

Two echoes of Genesis 1 in a single verse.

First, vayyizkor — the Elohim remembered. After 150 days of prevailing waters at the close of chapter 7, with no divine action recorded across that stretch, the chapter opens with a turning verb. The same verb will mark each major covenantal turning point in the Hebrew Bible: 'YHWH remembered Sarah' (Gen 21:1), 'the Elohim remembered Rachel' (30:22), 'the Elohim remembered his covenant with Abraham, with Isaac, and with Jacob' at the start of the Exodus (Ex 2:24). In every case, remembering names the moment when divine attention returns to the preserved-and-waiting individual after a long stretch. The chapter is doing a literary thing it will do again at every covenantal hinge.

Second, vayya'aver Elohim ruach al ha-aretz — the Elohim caused a breath to pass over the land. Compare Gen 1:2: ruach Elohim merachefet al p'nei ha-mayim — the breath of the Elohim was hovering over the face of the waters. Same noun (ruach), same agents, the same waters being acted on. The chapter is rebooting Genesis 1's grammar. The un-creation of the Flood is being reversed into a re-creation, with the same divine ruach serving as the medium. What hovered over the waters at the beginning now passes over the land at the resumption.

2
The fountains of the deep and the floodgates of the skies were closed, and the rain from the skies was held back.
וַיִּסָּֽכְרוּ֙ מַעְיְנֹ֣ת תְּה֔וֹם וַֽאֲרֻבֹּ֖ת הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם וַיִּכָּלֵ֥א הַגֶּ֖שֶׁם מִן־הַשָּׁמָֽיִם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The reversal reversed. The two cosmic openings of 7:11 — the fountains of the great deep and the floodgates of the skies — are now closed. The verbs are passive (niphal): vayyissakhru ('were closed', from סָכַר sakar — to shut, stop up) and vayyikkale ('was held back', from כָּלָא kala — to restrain, withhold). The chapter is undoing 7:11 with mirror-grammar. The raqia of Genesis 1, breached in 7:11, is now resealed; the lower waters of Genesis 1, released in 7:11, are now contained. The cosmological collapse of chapter 7 is structurally reversed in two verbs of chapter 8.

3
The waters receded from upon the land, going and returning; and at the end of one hundred and fifty days the waters had diminished.
וַיָּשֻׁ֧בוּ הַמַּ֛יִם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָ֖רֶץ הָל֣וֹךְ וָשׁ֑וֹב וַיַּחְסְר֣וּ הַמַּ֔יִם מִקְצֵ֕ה חֲמִשִּׁ֥ים וּמְאַ֖ת יֽוֹם׃
4
In the seventh month, on the seventeenth day of the month, the ark came to rest on the mountains of Ararat.
וַתָּ֤נַח הַתֵּבָה֙ בַּחֹ֣דֶשׁ הַשְּׁבִיעִ֔י בְּשִׁבְעָה־עָשָׂ֥ר י֖וֹם לַחֹ֑דֶשׁ עַ֖ל הָרֵ֥י אֲרָרָֽט׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The ark rests. The verb תָּנַח tanach is from נוּחַ nuach — the same root as Noah's name (5:29). The ark of Noah comes-to-rest on the mountains of Ararat. The chapter's name-and-verb wordplay is structural: the resting one (Noach) is in a resting (tanach) vessel; the rest happens exactly when the divine ruach has done its work over the waters.

The date — seventh month, seventeenth day — is precisely five months (150 days) after the Flood began (7:11: second month, seventeenth day). The chapter is keeping a calendar; the durations are not symbolic durations only but counted days. The five months equal 150 days only if each month is exactly 30 days — implying the chapter uses a schematic 30-day-month, 360-day-year calendar (the same calendar later used in 1 Enoch's astronomical book and in priestly liturgical reckoning). The chapter's calendrical care is precise enough to surface its own calendrical assumptions.

Harei Ararat — mountains of Ararat, plural. The Hebrew specifies a mountainous region (Urartu, around Lake Van in eastern Anatolia / Armenia), not a single peak. The traditional identification of Mount Ararat (Ağrı Dağı in Turkey, 5,137m) became fixed in late antique and medieval Christian tradition; the Hebrew is broader.

5
The waters continued to recede until the tenth month; in the tenth month, on the first day of the month, the tops of the mountains appeared.
וְהַמַּ֗יִם הָיוּ֙ הָל֣וֹךְ וְחָס֔וֹר עַ֖ד הַחֹ֣דֶשׁ הָֽעֲשִׂירִ֑י בָּֽעֲשִׂירִי֙ בְּאֶחָ֣ד לַחֹ֔דֶשׁ נִרְא֖וּ רָאשֵׁ֥י הֶֽהָרִֽים׃
6
At the end of forty days, Noah opened the window of the ark which he had made.
וַֽיְהִ֕י מִקֵּ֖ץ אַרְבָּעִ֣ים י֑וֹם וַיִּפְתַּ֣ח נֹ֔חַ אֶת־חַלּ֥וֹן הַתֵּבָ֖ה אֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשָֽׂה׃
7
He sent out the raven, and it went out, going to and fro, until the waters dried up from the land.
וַיְשַׁלַּ֖ח אֶת־הָֽעֹרֵ֑ב וַיֵּצֵ֤א יָצוֹא֙ וָשׁ֔וֹב עַד־יְבֹ֥שֶׁת הַמַּ֖יִם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 7

The raven first. Noah sends out ha-orev — the raven — and it leaves and returns repeatedly without reporting back conclusively. The Mesopotamian flood narratives (Gilgamesh Tablet XI, Atrahasis) have a different bird-sequence: Utnapishtim sends a dove, then a swallow, then a raven — and the raven does not return, signaling the waters have receded. Genesis has reversed the order: raven first, dove second, and the dove's behavior is what tells Noah the land has dried.

The raven's going to and fro (יָצוֹא וָשׁוֹב yatzo va-shov, an infinitive-absolute construction) is iterative — the bird shuttles without settling. The raven is unclean (by later Levitical categorization, Lev 11:15) and a carrion-eater; the chapter's logic is that the raven could subsist on floating bodies and emerging carcasses, requiring no settled landing. The dove, by contrast, will return because there is nowhere clean yet to alight. The two birds' contrasting behaviors are not arbitrary — they reflect each species' ecological capacity.

8
Then he sent out the dove from him, to see whether the waters had lightened from the face of the ground.
וַיְשַׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־הַיּוֹנָ֖ה מֵאִתּ֑וֹ לִרְאוֹת֙ הֲקַ֣לּוּ הַמַּ֔יִם מֵעַ֖ל פְּנֵ֥י הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
9
But the dove found no resting place for the sole of her foot, and she returned to him to the ark, for the waters were on the face of all the land. He put out his hand and took her, and brought her to him into the ark.
וְלֹֽא־מָצְאָה֩ הַיּוֹנָ֨ה מָנ֜וֹחַ לְכַף־רַגְלָ֗הּ וַתָּ֤שָׁב אֵלָיו֙ אֶל־הַתֵּבָ֔ה כִּי־מַ֖יִם עַל־פְּנֵ֣י כָל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיִּשְׁלַ֤ח יָדוֹ֙ וַיִּקָּחֶ֔הָ וַיָּבֵ֥א אֹתָ֛הּ אֵלָ֖יו אֶל־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The resting-place vocabulary. Manoach l-khaf-raglah — a resting-place for the sole of her foot. The noun מָנוֹחַ manoach is again from the root נוּחַ nuach — rest, settle. The chapter's Noah / Noach / tanach / manoach wordplay continues: the resting one (Noach) sends the dove to look for a resting-place (manoach), and the dove cannot find one — yet. Noah gently takes her (וַיִּקָּחֶהָ vayyiqqachehah) — the verbal tenderness is striking after the chapter's mass-extinction vocabulary; put out his hand (vayyishlach yado) and brought her in. The interaction is small-scale, intimate, and named in domestic verbs.

10
He waited another seven days, and again sent the dove out from the ark.
וַיָּ֣חֶל ע֔וֹד שִׁבְעַ֥ת יָמִ֖ים אֲחֵרִ֑ים וַיֹּ֛סֶף שַׁלַּ֥ח אֶת־הַיּוֹנָ֖ה מִן־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
11
The dove came to him at evening time — and behold, a fresh olive leaf in her mouth! Noah knew that the waters had lightened from upon the land.
וַתָּבֹ֨א אֵלָ֤יו הַיּוֹנָה֙ לְעֵ֣ת עֶ֔רֶב וְהִנֵּ֥ה עֲלֵה־זַ֖יִת טָרָ֣ף בְּפִ֑יהָ וַיֵּ֣דַע נֹ֔חַ כִּי־קַ֥לּוּ הַמַּ֖יִם מֵעַ֥ל הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The chapter's iconic image. Two verbal precisions and a botanical note.

The verb טָרַף taraf has two senses: plucked (active — the dove plucked it) or fresh / newly-broken-off (stative passive — the leaf was new). Modern translations favor fresh: the leaf is new growth, evidence that vegetation has resumed on the land. The chapter's evidence is botanical: there is now a re-emerging biosphere.

The choice of olive is precise. The olive tree (Olea europaea) is one of the most flood-resistant and quickly-regenerating Mediterranean species, capable of resprouting from roots after extended submersion. ANE-Mediterranean farmers knew this; the olive was the tree one would expect to see leafing first. The chapter's botany is observational, not arbitrary.

The image — yonah carrying a fresh olive leaf — will become one of the Hebrew Bible's most-reproduced icons. Christian art will make the dove the Holy Spirit; modern peace iconography will use the olive branch as its sign. The chapter does neither — it simply records evening, dove, olive, knowledge. The afterlife of the image dwarfs its modest verse.

12
He waited another seven days, and sent out the dove; and she did not return to him again.
וַיִּיָּ֣חֶל ע֔וֹד שִׁבְעַ֥ת יָמִ֖ים אֲחֵרִ֑ים וַיְשַׁלַּח֙ אֶת־הַיּוֹנָ֔ה וְלֹֽא־יָסְפָ֥ה שׁוּב־אֵלָ֖יו עֽוֹד׃
13
In the six hundred and first year, on the first day of the first month, the waters dried up from on the land. Noah removed the covering of the ark and looked — and behold, the face of the ground had dried.
וַֽ֠יְהִי בְּאַחַ֨ת וְשֵׁשׁ־מֵא֜וֹת שָׁנָ֗ה בָּֽרִאשׁוֹן֙ בְּאֶחָ֣ד לַחֹ֔דֶשׁ חָֽרְב֥וּ הַמַּ֖יִם מֵעַ֣ל הָאָ֑רֶץ וַיָּ֤סַר נֹ֙חַ֙ אֶת־מִכְסֵ֣ה הַתֵּבָ֔ה וַיַּ֕רְא וְהִנֵּ֥ה חָֽרְב֖וּ פְּנֵ֥י הָֽאֲדָמָֽה׃
14
In the second month, on the twenty-seventh day of the month, the land was dry.
וּבַחֹ֙דֶשׁ֙ הַשֵּׁנִ֔י בְּשִׁבְעָ֧ה וְעֶשְׂרִ֛ים י֖וֹם לַחֹ֑דֶשׁ יָבְשָׁ֖ה הָאָֽרֶץ׃ (ס)
15
The Elohim spoke to Noah, saying,
וַיְדַבֵּ֥ר אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶל־נֹ֥חַ לֵאמֹֽר׃
16
"Come out of the ark — you, your wife, your sons, and your sons' wives with you.
צֵ֖א מִן־הַתֵּבָ֑ה אַתָּ֕ה וְאִשְׁתְּךָ֛ וּבָנֶ֥יךָ וּנְשֵֽׁי־בָנֶ֖יךָ אִתָּֽךְ׃
17
Bring out with you every living thing that is with you, of all flesh — birds, cattle, every creeping thing that creeps upon the land — that they may swarm on the land, and be fruitful and multiply on the land."
כָּל־הַחַיָּ֨ה אֲשֶֽׁר־אִתְּךָ֜ מִכָּל־בָּשָׂ֗ר בָּע֧וֹף וּבַבְּהֵמָ֛ה וּבְכָל־הָרֶ֛מֶשׂ הָרֹמֵ֥שׂ עַל־הָאָ֖רֶץ הוצא [הַיְצֵ֣א] אִתָּ֑ךְ וְשָֽׁרְצ֣וּ בָאָ֔רֶץ וּפָר֥וּ וְרָב֖וּ עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 17

The creation blessing repeated. Sharetzu ... peru u-rvu — swarm ... be fruitful and multiply — is the explicit verbal echo of the creation blessings of Gen 1:22 (to the sea-creatures: 'be fruitful and multiply, and fill the waters') and 1:28 (to humans: 'be fruitful and multiply, and fill the land'). The Flood erased; the disembarkation re-mandates. The chapter's re-creation grammar is exact: the same blessing-vocabulary that named the original creation's fertility-mandate is repeated to the post-Flood remnant. The species-categories Noah brought through the Flood are now told to do again what they were told to do before — fill the categories with kind.

18
Noah came out — his sons, his wife, and his sons' wives with him.
וַיֵּ֖צֵא־נֹ֑חַ וּבָנָ֛יו וְאִשְׁתּ֥וֹ וּנְשֵֽׁי־בָנָ֖יו אִתּֽוֹ׃
19
Every living thing, every creeping thing, and every bird — everything that moves upon the land — went out from the ark by their families.
כָּל־הַֽחַיָּ֗ה כָּל־הָרֶ֙מֶשׂ֙ וְכָל־הָע֔וֹף כֹּ֖ל רוֹמֵ֣שׂ עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ לְמִשְׁפְּחֹ֣תֵיהֶ֔ם יָצְא֖וּ מִן־הַתֵּבָֽה׃
20
Noah built an altar to YHWH; and he took some of every clean animal and of every clean bird, and offered whole burnt offerings on the altar.
וַיִּ֥בֶן נֹ֛חַ מִזְבֵּ֖חַ לַֽיהוָ֑ה וַיִּקַּ֞ח מִכֹּ֣ל ׀ הַבְּהֵמָ֣ה הַטְּהוֹרָ֗ה וּמִכֹּל֙ הָע֣וֹף הַטָּהֹ֔ר וַיַּ֥עַל עֹלֹ֖ת בַּמִּזְבֵּֽחַ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Two firsts in one verse. First mizbeach (altar) and first olah (whole burnt offering) in the Hebrew Bible. Cain and Hevel's earlier offerings (4:3–4) were minchot — gifts/grain-offerings — and were brought without a recorded altar. Here in 8:20 the cultic vocabulary that will run through Leviticus and Numbers makes its founding appearance: built altar, slaughtered offering, ascending smoke. The chapter places the institution of formal altar-and-burnt-offering at the post-Flood threshold — before circumcision (chapter 17), before the priesthood (Exodus 28), before the tabernacle (Exodus 25), but the cultic vocabulary they will all use is here.

The surplus from the seven-pair instruction (7:2) is what makes the offering possible — Noah has extras of the clean animals beyond breeding pairs, available for sacrifice. The chapter's earlier numerical instruction was the practical-cultic preparation for this moment. The two textual moves bind: 7:2 made room for surplus; 8:20 uses it.

21
YHWH smelled the soothing aroma, and YHWH said in his heart, "I will not again curse the ground because of the human, for the inclination of the heart of the human is evil from his youth; and I will not again strike down every living thing as I have done.
וַיָּ֣רַח יְהוָה֮ אֶת־רֵ֣יחַ הַנִּיחֹחַ֒ וַיֹּ֨אמֶר יְהוָ֜ה אֶל־לִבּ֗וֹ לֹֽא־אֹ֠סִף לְקַלֵּ֨ל ע֤וֹד אֶת־הָֽאֲדָמָה֙ בַּעֲב֣וּר הָֽאָדָ֔ם כִּ֠י יֵ֣צֶר לֵ֧ב הָאָדָ֛ם רַ֖ע מִנְּעֻרָ֑יו וְלֹֽא־אֹסִ֥ף ע֛וֹד לְהַכּ֥וֹת אֶת־כָּל־חַ֖י כַּֽאֲשֶׁ֥ר עָשִֽׂיתִי׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The chapter's keystone verse — three load-bearing claims.

First, vayyarach YHWH et reach hannichoach — YHWH smelled the soothing aroma. The Hebrew is anthropopathic and concrete: YHWH smells the rising smoke. The ANE parallel is unmistakable. In the Atrahasis epic and Gilgamesh Tablet XI, after the flood, Utnapishtim builds an altar and offers sacrifice, and the gods smell the sweet savor and gather around it like flies — because they have been starved during the flood (no human sacrifices reached them while humanity was drowned). Genesis's grammar of the smelling is exactly that of the Mesopotamian parallel, but Genesis's theology of it is different: the gods of Atrahasis are hungry, the YHWH of Genesis is moved-to-resolve. The verb and image are shared with the broader ANE flood traditions; the consequence drawn from the smelling differs.

Second, lo osif l-qallel od et ha-adamah ba-avur ha-adam — I will not again curse the ground because of the human. The chapter delivers on the hope Lamech named in 5:29: the ground which YHWH cursed (a callback to 3:17, arurah ha-adamah ba-avurecha) is now not-going-to-be-cursed-again. Note the verb-shift: 3:17 used אָרַר arar (the formal curse-pronouncement verb); 8:21 uses קָלַל qalel (a lighter curse-verb). The commitment is to not adding curses, not to removing the formal arar of 3:17. The post-Flood condition is the curse stays operative; no new curses will be added.

Third, ki yetzer lev ha-adam ra mi-neurav — for the inclination of the heart of the human is evil from his youth. The same diagnosis given as the reason for the Flood (6:5: every inclination of the thoughts of his heart was only evil all day long) is now given as the reason for not repeating the Flood. The exact same observation has produced opposite divine decisions. The rabbinic tradition (Rashi) reads the inversion as theological breakthrough: pre-Flood, human evil was the reason to destroy; post-Flood, recognition that human evil is constitutional (mi-neurav — from youth, present from the outset, structural rather than acquired) makes destruction-as-policy futile. Destruction did not solve the problem of human evil, because the problem is in the yetzer — in the inclining-faculty itself. The chapter's theology of divine restraint: rather than respond to human evil with cyclic destruction, the divine commits to continuity and lets the evil-prone humans persist. The Flood was the response that did not work; non-destruction is the response that follows.

22
While the land yet endures, seedtime and harvest, cold and heat, summer and winter, day and night — these shall not cease."
עֹ֖ד כָּל־יְמֵ֣י הָאָ֑רֶץ זֶ֡רַע וְ֠קָצִיר וְקֹ֨ר וָחֹ֜ם וְקַ֧יִץ וָחֹ֛רֶף וְי֥וֹם וָלַ֖יְלָה לֹ֥א יִשְׁבֹּֽתוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

Four pairs name the restored order. Zera v'qatzir (seed and harvest — agricultural cycle), qor va-chom (cold and heat — thermal cycle), qayitz va-choref (summer and winter — seasonal cycle), yom va-laylah (day and night — diurnal cycle). The chapter closes with eight words that re-establish what the Flood had threatened.

Note the verb לֹא יִשְׁבֹּתוּ lo yishbotu — they shall not cease. The root is שָׁבַת shabat — the root behind shabbat, the cessation. The cycles will not sabbath. The chapter is using the cessation-vocabulary in the negative: the rhythms that organize life will not stop, will not rest, will not cease. The Sabbath cycle (Gen 2:2–3) is, in Hebrew theology, the cessation that gives shape to the others; here at the post-Flood threshold the other cycles are promised non-cessation. The Sabbath cessation continues to structure the week, but the larger seasonal, diurnal, and agricultural rhythms continue without interruption. The chapter closes on the most basic guarantee the Hebrew Bible offers: the world's rhythms are pinned in place against another collapse like the Flood.

Chapter 9

Genesis 9 — The covenant, the bow in the cloud, and Noah's vineyard

1
The Elohim blessed Noah and his sons, and said to them: be fruitful and multiply and fill the land.
וַיְבָ֣רֶךְ אֱלֹהִ֔ים אֶת־נֹ֖חַ וְאֶת־בָּנָ֑יו וַיֹּ֧אמֶר לָהֶ֛ם פְּר֥וּ וּרְב֖וּ וּמִלְא֥וּ אֶת־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 1

The Genesis 1 blessing, re-issued. Word-for-word, this is the blessing of 1:28 — peru u-revu u-mil'u et ha-aretz — spoken now to the survivors at the start of the second human age. The chapter is doing the structural thing it has been doing since chapter 8: rebooting Genesis 1's grammar onto the post-Flood world.
