Adam and his wife received this blessing as the first humans; Noah and his sons receive it again as the second-first humans. The Flood narrative has positioned Noah as a second Adam — same blessing, same mandate.

What is missing is also revealing. Genesis 1:28 included kivshuha (subdue it) and redu (rule over). Here those verbs are dropped — only the demographic command (be fruitful, multiply, fill) is preserved. The dominion mandate is being reissued separately in v2 with new and harsher vocabulary.

2
The dread of you and the terror of you shall be upon every living thing of the land, and upon every bird of the skies — upon everything that the soil teems with, and upon all the fish of the sea: into your hand they are given.
וּמוֹרַאֲכֶ֤ם וְחִתְּכֶם֙ יִֽהְיֶ֔ה עַ֚ל כָּל־חַיַּ֣ת הָאָ֔רֶץ וְעַ֖ל כָּל־ע֣וֹף הַשָּׁמָ֑יִם בְּכֹל֩ אֲשֶׁ֨ר תִּרְמֹ֧שׂ הָֽאֲדָמָ֛ה וּֽבְכָל־דְּגֵ֥י הַיָּ֖ם בְּיֶדְכֶ֥ם נִתָּֽנוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 2

The dominion mandate, re-issued in fear-vocabulary. Where Genesis 1:28 told the first humans to rule (radah) and subdue (kavash) the animals — verbs of governance and ordering — the post-Flood mandate uses mora (dread) and chittah (terror). These are war-vocabulary words. Mora appears in Deuteronomy and the conquest narratives for the dread that falls on Israel's enemies (Deut 11:25, 'no man shall be able to stand before you; YHWH your Elohim will lay the dread of you'). Chittah (from חתת chatat — to be shattered, broken) names the way enemies collapse in fear (Job 6:21).

The rhetorical move is precise. Before the Flood, animal and human relations are pictured under the verbs radah and kavash — verbs that have a meaning-range from 'rule' (functional) to 'tread down' (forceful), but which name a relationship. After the Flood, the relationship is named by the psychological state the animals will live in: dread, terror. The next verse explains why.

3
Every moving thing that lives shall be yours for food; as the green plants, I have given you everything.
כָּל־רֶ֙מֶשׂ֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר הוּא־חַ֔י לָכֶ֥ם יִהְיֶ֖ה לְאָכְלָ֑ה כְּיֶ֣רֶק עֵ֔שֶׂב נָתַ֥תִּי לָכֶ֖ם אֶת־כֹּֽל׃
Translation Commentary Verse 3

Meat enters the human diet. This verse is the precise inverse of 1:29–30. There, the first humans were given every herb yielding seed and every tree with seed-bearing fruit — and only that — as food; the animals likewise received only the green plants. The Edenic and antediluvian diet, on the chapter's own account, is vegetarian.

Here, post-Flood, the dietary permission is expanded: kol remes asher hu-chai lakhem yihyeh l'akhlah — every moving thing that lives shall be yours for food. The comparative phrase k-yereq esev (as the green plants) makes the analogy explicit: meat is now given on the same footing that vegetation was given in 1:29. Two ages of human diet, with the Flood as the hinge.

The chapter's editorial logic seems to be: the violent world of chapter 6 (where 'all flesh had corrupted its way upon the land,' 6:12) is being acknowledged. Pure herbivory was the Edenic ideal; in the post-Flood, post-violence world, the chapter concedes meat-eating — but with the immediate restrictions of vv. 4–6.

4
Only — flesh with its life, its blood, you shall not eat.
אַךְ־בָּשָׂ֕ר בְּנַפְשׁ֥וֹ דָמ֖וֹ לֹ֥א תֹאכֵֽלוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 4

The blood-life equation. The Hebrew is densely compressed: basar b'nafsho damo lo tokhelu — literally flesh in-its-nefesh its-blood, you shall not eat. The blood is in apposition to nefesh (life-soul, breath-self); the chapter is asserting an identification, not a separate rule. The blood IS the nefesh, the carrier of the life-force.

This is the first explicit dietary prohibition in the Torah. It is repeated and elaborated in Leviticus 17:10–14 ('the nefesh of the flesh is in the blood'), Deuteronomy 12:23 ('the blood is the nefesh, and you shall not eat the nefesh with the flesh'), and underlies all kosher slaughter regulations (shechitah's central concern is the complete drainage of blood). The chapter is establishing the principle that grounds an entire later legal tradition.

The theological logic, on the chapter's terms: meat is permitted, but the life-force in the meat must be returned (poured out, drained) and not consumed. To consume the blood would be to consume the nefesh of the animal — a kind of category-violation that the chapter prohibits even as it concedes the meat itself.

5
And further — your own blood, for your lives, I will demand; from the hand of every living thing I will demand it, and from the hand of the human, from the hand of each man's brother, I will demand the life of the human.
וְאַ֨ךְ אֶת־דִּמְכֶ֤ם לְנַפְשֹֽׁתֵיכֶם֙ אֶדְרֹ֔שׁ מִיַּ֥ד כָּל־חַיָּ֖ה אֶדְרְשֶׁ֑נּוּ וּמִיַּ֣ד הָֽאָדָ֗ם מִיַּד֙ אִ֣ישׁ אָחִ֔יו אֶדְרֹ֖שׁ אֶת־נֶ֥פֶשׁ הָֽאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 5

The reckoning for human blood. The verb darash (to seek, inquire, demand) is the chapter's strongest word for divine accountability. The construction edrosh — edreshennu — edrosh (three repetitions in one verse) gives the line its weight: I will demand, I will demand it, I will demand. The triple emphasis pivots from animal predation (animals that kill humans will be held to account, v5a) to human violence (humans who kill humans will be held to account, v5b).

The phrase mi-yad ish achiv — from the hand of each man's brother — is a deliberate echo of chapter 4. Cain killed Hevel his brother and YHWH said the blood of your brother cries out to me from the ground (4:10). The chapter is invoking that scene by name. The first-recorded fratricide was met then with a curse and a mark; now, after the Flood that the human violence of chapter 6 helped trigger, the chapter installs a standing divine demand against brother-killing-brother.

6
One who sheds the blood of the human — by the human his blood shall be shed; for in the image of the Elohim, he made the human.
שֹׁפֵךְ֙ דַּ֣ם הָֽאָדָ֔ם בָּֽאָדָ֖ם דָּמ֣וֹ יִשָּׁפֵ֑ךְ כִּ֚י בְּצֶ֣לֶם אֱלֹהִ֔ים עָשָׂ֖ה אֶת־הָאָדָֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 6

Murder is a wound to the Elohim's likeness. The verse is structured as a tight chiasm: shofech / dam / ha-adam / ba-adam / damo / yishshafekh — sheds / blood / of-the-human / by-the-human / his-blood / shall-be-shed. Six words mirroring around their center, with the act and its consequence locked in formal symmetry.

The grounding clause is the load-bearing one for the chapter's anthropology: ki b'tselem Elohim asah et ha-adam — for in the image of the Elohim, he made the human. This is the third invocation of tselem Elohim in Genesis (1:26–27, 5:1, now 9:6) and the only one that draws a legal consequence. Killing a human is now framed as an injury to the resemblance the human carries.

On the WoH frame: the tselem and demut of 1:26 are read as physical resemblance, not metaphor. Here that reading does not weaken — it strengthens — the prohibition. To kill a human is to destroy a body that physically resembles the Elohim who made it. The chapter is not arguing from a 'dignity' that is symbolic or abstract; it is arguing from a literal resemblance that grounds a literal claim. The post-Flood legal order is being founded on the fact that humans look like the Elohim.

7
As for you — be fruitful and multiply, teem upon the land, and multiply in it.
וְאַתֶּ֖ם פְּר֣וּ וּרְב֑וּ שִׁרְצ֥וּ בָאָ֖רֶץ וּרְבוּ־בָֽהּ׃
8
The Elohim said to Noah and to his sons with him, saying:
וַיֹּ֤אמֶר אֱלֹהִים֙ אֶל־נֹ֔חַ וְאֶל־בָּנָ֥יו אִתּ֖וֹ לֵאמֹֽר׃
9
As for me — here I am, establishing my covenant with you, and with your offspring after you,
וַאֲנִ֕י הִנְנִ֥י מֵקִ֛ים אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֖י אִתְּכֶ֑ם וְאֶֽת־זַרְעֲכֶ֖ם אַֽחֲרֵיכֶֽם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 9

The first explicit covenant in the Torah. Brit was first promised to Noah in 6:18 (and I will establish my brit with you); here it is enacted. The verb is meqim — establishing, from קוּם qum (to rise, stand). A brit is not declared; it is raised up between parties, like a structure being set in place.

What is novel is the scope. The chapter will widen it in v10: this brit is not only with humans but with every living nefesh aboard the ark — birds, cattle, every wild animal. The chapter is establishing the first universal cosmic covenant, addressed to all life. The later Abrahamic covenant (chapters 15, 17) and the Sinaitic covenant (Exodus 19–24) will both be narrower in scope: a single family, a single people. The Noahic covenant is the canopy under which the rest sit.

10
and with every living being that is with you — among the birds, among the cattle, and among every wild creature of the land that is with you; all of them that came out of the ark, every living thing of the land.
וְאֵ֨ת כָּל־נֶ֤פֶשׁ הַֽחַיָּה֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתְּכֶ֔ם בָּע֧וֹף בַּבְּהֵמָ֛ה וּֽבְכָל־חַיַּ֥ת הָאָ֖רֶץ אִתְּכֶ֑ם מִכֹּל֙ יֹצְאֵ֣י הַתֵּבָ֔ה לְכֹ֖ל חַיַּ֥ת הָאָֽרֶץ׃
11
I will establish my covenant with you: never again shall all flesh be cut off by the waters of a flood; never again shall there be a flood to ruin the land.
וַהֲקִמֹתִ֤י אֶת־בְּרִיתִי֙ אִתְּכֶ֔ם וְלֹֽא־יִכָּרֵ֧ת כָּל־בָּשָׂ֛ר ע֖וֹד מִמֵּ֣י הַמַּבּ֑וּל וְלֹֽא־יִהְיֶ֥ה ע֛וֹד מַבּ֖וּל לְשַׁחֵ֥ת הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 11

The 'never again' written into the brit. In 8:21 the never-again promise was internal (YHWH said in his heart, an inner resolve). Here in 9:11 it is externalized as a public covenant clause with witnesses. The structure of chapters 8–9 is: first the private divine resolution, then the public divine ratification. The chapter is staging the post-Flood promise twice — first as inward decision, then as outward covenant — and pairing them with the soothing-aroma offering (8:20–21) and the bow-in-the-cloud sign (9:13).

The verb yikkaret (shall be cut off) is the technical verb for treaty-violation consequences in the ancient Near East. The chapter is using treaty-vocabulary. The brit is structured like a treaty: parties named, terms specified, sign affixed, witnesses called.

12
The Elohim said: this is the sign of the covenant that I am giving between me and you, and between every living being that is with you, for the generations of forever:
וַיֹּ֣אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֗ים זֹ֤את אֽוֹת־הַבְּרִית֙ אֲשֶׁר־אֲנִ֣י נֹתֵ֗ן בֵּינִי֙ וּבֵ֣ינֵיכֶ֔ם וּבֵ֛ין כָּל־נֶ֥פֶשׁ חַיָּ֖ה אֲשֶׁ֣ר אִתְּכֶ֑ם לְדֹרֹ֖ת עוֹלָֽם׃
13
My bow I have placed in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of covenant between me and the land.
אֶת־קַשְׁתִּ֕י נָתַ֖תִּי בֶּֽעָנָ֑ן וְהָֽיְתָה֙ לְא֣וֹת בְּרִ֔ית בֵּינִ֖י וּבֵ֥ין הָאָֽרֶץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 13

The bow that hangs in the cloud. The Hebrew noun is קֶשֶׁת qeshet — and elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible it almost exclusively means war-bow, the weapon. Qeshet is the bow of the warrior (Gen 27:3, 48:22), the bow of YHWH as divine warrior (Hab 3:9, 'you uncovered your bow'), the bow that is broken in eschatological peace texts (Hos 1:5, 2:18, Zech 9:10). The vocabulary is martial.

The chapter's choice to call the rainbow qeshet is not casual. The same noun that names the weapon that humans use against each other (cf. 5:5's darosh-edrosh logic) and that the divine warrior wields against enemies is now declared hung up, placed in the cloud, pointed (in the iconography of an unstrung bow held against a cloudy sky) away from the land and toward the heavens. The Elohim is unstringing the weapon.

On the WoH frame: the chapter is preserving an extraordinary image. The post-Flood promise is signed not with an abstract symbol but with an inverted weapon. The Elohim, having used cosmic violence on humanity once, hangs up the bow as a continuous public sign that the violence is over.

Comparative note: similar imagery appears in Mesopotamian flood traditions. In Atrahasis and the Gilgamesh XI flood, the goddess raises her necklace of lapis lazuli (the rainbow) and swears never again to forget the destruction. The motif of a celestial colored arc as a post-flood promise-token is shared; the Hebrew chapter has reshaped the lapis necklace into an unstrung war-bow.

14
Whenever I bring a cloud over the land, the bow shall be seen in the cloud.
וְהָיָ֕ה בְּעַֽנְנִ֥י עָנָ֖ן עַל־הָאָ֑רֶץ וְנִרְאֲתָ֥ה הַקֶּ֖שֶׁת בֶּעָנָֽן׃
15
And I will remember my covenant which is between me and you and every living being among all flesh; the waters shall never again become a flood to ruin all flesh.
וְזָכַרְתִּ֣י אֶת־בְּרִיתִ֗י אֲשֶׁ֤ר בֵּינִי֙ וּבֵ֣ינֵיכֶ֔ם וּבֵ֛ין כָּל־נֶ֥פֶשׁ חַיָּ֖ה בְּכָל־בָּשָׂ֑ר וְלֹֽא־יִֽהְיֶ֨ה ע֤וֹד הַמַּ֙יִם֙ לְמַבּ֔וּל לְשַׁחֵ֖ת כָּל־בָּשָֽׂר׃
Translation Commentary Verse 15

The Elohim binds the Elohim's own memory. The verb is zakharti — the same root as 8:1's vayyizkor Elohim et Noach (the Elohim remembered Noah). At the start of the Flood-reversal, divine memory was the act that turned the waters back. Here that same memory is built into the covenant as the function of the sign. The bow is not a memory aid for humans (humans do not need a reminder that they are alive); it is a memory aid the Elohim establishes for the Elohim. Whenever a cloud comes, the bow appears, and the Elohim's covenant-memory is triggered.

The chapter has now established the architecture of biblical covenant memory: a sign is given (natatti — v13), the sign is seen (nirathah — v14), the covenant is remembered (vezakharti — v15). Three verbs, three steps. The same architecture will recur at Sinai (the law on tablets-of-stone as covenant-memory) and in the prophets ('I have remembered my covenant').

16
The bow shall be in the cloud, and I will see it — to remember the everlasting covenant between the Elohim and every living being among all flesh that is upon the land.
וְהָיְתָ֥ה הַקֶּ֖שֶׁת בֶּֽעָנָ֑ן וּרְאִיתִ֗יהָ לִזְכֹּר֙ בְּרִ֣ית עוֹלָ֔ם בֵּ֣ין אֱלֹהִ֔ים וּבֵין֙ כָּל־נֶ֣פֶשׁ חַיָּ֔ה בְּכָל־בָּשָׂ֖ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
17
The Elohim said to Noah: this is the sign of the covenant which I have established between me and all flesh that is upon the land.
וַיֹּ֥אמֶר אֱלֹהִ֖ים אֶל־נֹ֑חַ זֹ֤את אֽוֹת־הַבְּרִית֙ אֲשֶׁ֣ר הֲקִמֹ֔תִי בֵּינִ֕י וּבֵ֥ין כָּל־בָּשָׂ֖ר אֲשֶׁ֥ר עַל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
18
The sons of Noah who came out of the ark were Shem and Ham and Yapheth; and Ham — he is the father of Canaan.
וַיִּֽהְי֣וּ בְנֵי־נֹ֗חַ הַיֹּֽצְאִים֙ מִן־הַתֵּבָ֔ה שֵׁ֖ם וְחָ֣ם וָיָ֑פֶת וְחָ֕ם ה֖וּא אֲבִ֥י כְנָֽעַן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 18

The narrator pre-frames the curse. Note the timing: the chapter names Ham's three brothers, then immediately re-identifies Ham as the father of Canaan — before any incident has been narrated. The chapter is editorially priming the reader for v25 (the curse on Canaan, not on Ham himself). The phrase Cham hu avi Kena'an is repeated in v22, bookending the incident. The chapter is making sure the reader can connect the post-Flood family to the later Israelite-Canaanite political geography: the curse that falls in this scene will explain the conquest narratives later in the Torah.

19
These three were the sons of Noah, and from these the whole land was scattered.
שְׁלֹשָׁ֥ה אֵ֖לֶּה בְּנֵי־נֹ֑חַ וּמֵאֵ֖לֶּה נָֽפְצָ֥ה כָל־הָאָֽרֶץ׃
20
Noah, a man of the soil, began — he planted a vineyard.
וַיָּ֥חֶל נֹ֖חַ אִ֣ישׁ הָֽאֲדָמָ֑ה וַיִּטַּ֖ע כָּֽרֶם׃
Translation Commentary Verse 20

Noah the second Adam, planting. The phrase ish ha-adamah — man of the soil — echoes 2:5's ein adam la'avod et ha-adamah (there was no human to till the soil) and 4:2's Qayin hayah oved adamah (Cain was a tiller of soil). Noah is being named as a continuation of the soil-cultivation line. The first human was placed in a garden; the first human's first son was a soil-tiller; the second-first human's first recorded post-Flood act is planting a vineyard. The chapter is tracking an evolving agricultural identity: garden → field → vineyard.

Vayyachel — he began. Some rabbinic readings (Bereshit Rabbah 36) heard the verb as vayyachel from חלל chalal (to profane, to make common) — Noah profaned. The Hebrew is ambiguous; the chapter does not commit. But the wordplay sits at the start of the scene, and the events of v21 will retroactively color the verb.

21
He drank of the wine and became drunk, and uncovered himself within his tent.
וַיֵּ֥שְׁתְּ מִן־הַיַּ֖יִן וַיִּשְׁכָּ֑ר וַיִּתְגַּ֖ל בְּת֥וֹךְ אָהֳלֹֽה׃
Translation Commentary Verse 21

The first drunkenness, the first uncovering. Three perfect-consecutive verbs in tight sequence — vayyesht (and he drank), vayyishkar (and he became drunk), vayyitgal (and he uncovered himself). The chapter does not condemn the wine, the drink, or the drunkenness directly; it narrates the sequence as fact and then narrates what happens next.

Vayyitgal — from גָּלָה galah, the same root as Galilee and galut (exile, uncovering). The reflexive form (hithpa'el) is rare and pointed: he uncovered himself. The next verse (v22) speaks of Ham seeing — but the chapter narrates Noah unmaking his own cover first. The vulnerability is self-induced and accidental, not assault.

The scene rhymes with chapter 3. There: a piece of fruit, a knowing, a discovered nakedness, a covering with fig leaves. Here: a vine's fruit (wine), a stupor, a self-uncovering, a covering with a garment. The chapter is doing a second-Adam pattern in miniature — Noah's vineyard scene as the post-Flood echo of Eden.

22
Ham, the father of Canaan, saw the nakedness of his father, and told his two brothers outside.
וַיַּ֗רְא חָ֚ם אֲבִ֣י כְנַ֔עַן אֵ֖ת עֶרְוַ֣ת אָבִ֑יו וַיַּגֵּ֥ד לִשְׁנֵֽי־אֶחָ֖יו בַּחֽוּץ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 22

The contested verse. The Hebrew is short, and that brevity is part of the interpretive problem. Vayyar Cham et ervat aviv — Ham saw the nakedness of his father. Vayyaged li-shenei echav ba-chutz — and he told his two brothers outside.

Three readings have circulated through the history of interpretation:

  1. Plain reading: Ham literally saw his father's naked body and gossiped about it to his brothers. The transgression is the seeing-and-telling — the violation of Noah's dignity, especially as compared with the brothers' careful aversion in v23. This is the reading most consistent with the chapter's own next-verse demonstration of the right response.

  2. Euphemism for sexual assault: The phrase ervat aviv (the nakedness of one's father) functions as a sexual idiom elsewhere in the Hebrew Bible (Lev 18:7–8, 20:11), where it can name sexual contact with the father's wife. Some readers (already in late antique Jewish tradition, e.g. b. Sanhedrin 70a) infer that something worse than a glance occurred. The chapter's text does not say so; the legal idiom is later.

  3. Euphemism for paternal castration: A minority reading (also in Sanhedrin 70a) extrapolates that Ham injured Noah in some way that prevented him from fathering a fourth son. The text gives no support beyond the heavy curse that follows.

The chapter itself is silent on which reading is correct. What the chapter does commit to is that whatever happened, it was sufficient to warrant the curse on Canaan in v25.

The re-identification of Ham as avi Kena'an (the father of Canaan) is repeated here for the third time in five verses (vv. 18, 22, 25). The chapter is hammering the genealogical link. Whoever Ham was, what is being set up is the etiology of the people Canaan — the indigenous population of the land that Israel would later occupy. The narrative is doing the political work of explaining a future conquest by placing its origin in a primal family scene.

23
Shem and Yapheth took the garment and laid it on the shoulders of the two of them, and walked backward, and covered the nakedness of their father; their faces were backward, and the nakedness of their father they did not see.
וַיִּקַּח֩ שֵׁ֨ם וָיֶ֜פֶת אֶת־הַשִּׂמְלָ֗ה וַיָּשִׂ֙ימוּ֙ עַל־שְׁכֶ֣ם שְׁנֵיהֶ֔ם וַיֵּֽלְכוּ֙ אֲחֹ֣רַנִּ֔ית וַיְכַסּ֕וּ אֵ֖ת עֶרְוַ֣ת אֲבִיהֶ֑ם וּפְנֵיהֶם֙ אֲחֹ֣רַנִּ֔ית וְעֶרְוַ֥ת אֲבִיהֶ֖ם לֹ֥א רָאֽוּ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 23

Ostentatious carefulness as moral demonstration. The chapter spends a long verse on a small action. The brothers (i) took a garment, (ii) placed it on the shoulders of both of them together, (iii) walked backward into the tent, (iv) covered their father's nakedness without turning, (v) kept their faces backward, and (vi) did not see. Six clauses for one act of covering.

The verbal contrast with v22 is the point. Ham saw (vayyar) and told (vayyaged). Shem and Yapheth placed (vayyasimu), walked (vayelchu), covered (vaykhasu), and did not see (lo ra'u). The chapter is using verb-architecture to do moral commentary without commentary: one brother accumulates verbs of exposure, two brothers accumulate verbs of covering. The narrator's evaluation is in the syntax.

24
Noah awoke from his wine, and knew what his youngest son had done to him.
וַיִּ֥יקֶץ נֹ֖חַ מִיֵּינ֑וֹ וַיֵּ֕דַע אֵ֛ת אֲשֶׁר־עָ֥שָׂה־ל֖וֹ בְּנ֥וֹ הַקָּטָֽן׃
Translation Commentary Verse 24

'His youngest son' — bno ha-qatan. The phrase has produced an old textual puzzle. The chapter has been listing Noah's sons as Shem, Ham, Yapheth (vv. 18, 19, 23) — that ordering places Ham as the middle son, not the youngest. Either (a) the listing order is by prominence rather than birth order, with Ham actually the youngest; or (b) the phrase bno ha-qatan is doing other work (the diminutive qatan can mean 'small' in dignity, not only in age, in some idioms). The Hebrew is not unambiguous. The chapter does not stop to resolve.

Vayyeda et asher asah lo — and he knew what he had done to him. The verb yada (to know) — the same verb that organized Eden in 2:9 (the tree of the knowing) and the recognition of nakedness in 3:7 (and they knew that they were naked). Noah waking from wine into knowing-what-his-son-did continues the chapter's Eden-rhyme. The first knowing led to the first hiding; the second knowing leads to the first curse-by-a-human.

25
He said: cursed be Canaan; a slave of slaves shall he be to his brothers.
וַיֹּ֖אמֶר אָר֣וּר כְּנָ֑עַן עֶ֥בֶד עֲבָדִ֖ים יִֽהְיֶ֥ה לְאֶחָֽיו׃
Translation Commentary Verse 25

The curse skips the offender. Ham is the one who saw and told. Canaan is the one who is cursed. The chapter does not explain the skip, and the silence has been heavily theologized across the history of interpretation — none of it grounded in the text. What the text gives is the brute political function: the people Canaan, whose territory Israel will later occupy, are being preassigned to slave-status to Shem's line (the Israelite line) and Yapheth's line.

Eved avadim — slave of slaves. The grammatical construct (construct of intensification, like holy of holies, song of songs) is superlative: the lowest possible status. The chapter is installing the political grammar of conquest in a primal-family curse.

The verse has a brutal reception history: in colonial-era Christian readings, the curse-on-Canaan was racialized and repurposed to justify the African slave trade (the so-called 'curse of Ham', stripped of its actual textual referent to Canaan, the people of the southern Levant). The text is not racial; it is regional and political. But the misreading was load-bearing for centuries of racial-religious ideology, and the historical violence is real. WoH commentary will not minimize that reception, and will not minimize the chapter's own ethnocentric framing either: the curse-of-Canaan formula was the Torah's first attempt to give a divinely-sanctioned origin story to its conflicts with neighbors.

26
He said: blessed be YHWH, Elohim of Shem; and let Canaan be a slave to him.
וַיֹּ֕אמֶר בָּר֥וּךְ יְהֹוָ֖ה אֱלֹ֣הֵי שֵׁ֑ם וִיהִ֥י כְנַ֖עַן עֶ֥בֶד לָֽמוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 26

YHWH is identified as the elohim of Shem. This is the first time in the Torah that YHWH is identified by relationship to a specific human line. Before this, YHWH has been the Elohim of the humans (collectively); here YHWH is the elohei Shem — the Elohim of Shem. The pattern will repeat: Elohei Avraham (Elohim of Abraham), Elohei Yitzchaq (Elohim of Isaac), Elohei Yisrael (Elohim of Israel). The chapter is opening the genealogical bracket inside which the rest of the Torah's covenant theology will operate. The future Israelite line is being demarcated, here, in a blessing on Shem.

27
May the Elohim enlarge Yapheth; and may he dwell in the tents of Shem; and let Canaan be a slave to him.
יַ֤פְתְּ אֱלֹהִים֙ לְיֶ֔פֶת וְיִשְׁכֹּ֖ן בְּאָֽהֳלֵי־שֵׁ֑ם וִיהִ֥י כְנַ֖עַן עֶ֥בֶד לָֽמוֹ׃
Translation Commentary Verse 27

The name-pun. Yapht Elohim l-Yephet — may the Elohim enlarge for Yapheth. The verb yapht (from פתה patah — to be wide, to open up, to enlarge) is the same root as the name Yephet itself. The chapter is folding a wish into a name: may the wide one widen. The same naming-by-pun pattern recurs throughout Genesis (Adam from adamah, Hevel from hevel, Yitzchaq from tzachaq, Yisrael from sarah).

Yishkon b'oholei Shem — may he dwell in the tents of Shem. The clause is grammatically ambiguous: does he refer back to Yapheth (Yapheth will dwell in Shem's tents — a peaceful coexistence reading), or does it refer to the Elohim (the Elohim will dwell in Shem's tents — an early sanctuary-theology reading)? Rabbinic readers split. The chapter does not commit. Either way the structural point is that Shem's tents become the privileged location.

28
Noah lived after the Flood three hundred years and fifty years.
וַֽיְחִי־נֹ֖חַ אַחַ֣ר הַמַּבּ֑וּל שְׁלֹ֤שׁ מֵאוֹת֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וַֽחֲמִשִּׁ֖ים שָׁנָֽה׃
29
All the days of Noah were nine hundred years and fifty years, and he died.
וַיִּֽהְיוּ֙ כָּל־יְמֵי־נֹ֔חַ תְּשַׁ֤ע מֵאוֹת֙ שָׁנָ֔ה וַחֲמִשִּׁ֖ים שָׁנָ֑ה וַיָּמֹֽת׃
Translation Commentary Verse 29

The last antediluvian-scale lifespan. Noah lives 950 years total — longer than every patriarch in chapter 5 except Methuselah (969) and Yared (962), and 19 years longer than Adam (930). His lifespan straddles the Flood: 600 years before, 350 years after. The chapter closes the second half of his life in a single verse, mirroring the closing formulae of chapter 5 (and all his days were X years, and he died).

Noah is the last person in the Torah to live near a millennium. After him, the lifespans contract sharply: Shem lives 600 (11:11), Arpachshad 438 (11:13), and the curve descends until Abraham at 175 (25:7) and Moses at 120 (Deut 34:7). The chapter is setting up the post-Flood lifespan-decay arc that will run for the next two chapters and shape all later patriarchal accounts.

The 120-year ceiling announced in 6:3 does not bind here yet — Noah and his immediate descendants still live far beyond it. The chapter's calendar of life-lengths is a slow descent across generations, not an instant cap.

Contents

  • 2 Genesis 2 — The garden in Eden
  • 3 Genesis 3 — The serpent and the expulsion
  • 4 Genesis 4 — Cain and Hevel
  • 5 Genesis 5 — The record of the begettings of Adam
  • 6 Genesis 6 — The sons of the Elohim and the Flood announced

Contents

    Wheel of Heaven is an open, multilingual reading of the ancient world's creation traditions — Raëlian, biblical, Mesopotamian, and others — through a single working hypothesis: that the beings called Elohim were a small advanced human civilization that came here, designed life on Earth, and left a record in the religious texts. The reading runs across twelve ages of the precessional cycle, from the project's beginning twenty-two thousand years ago to the present age of disclosure and into the long future the next Great Year is opening. The site is free, source-aware, and built so the work can be checked.
